## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define digital video. List its four primary parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Digital video is the digital representation of moving visual information as a sequence of still frames sampled at a fixed temporal rate. It is a 3D signal $V(x, y, t)$ over two spatial dimensions and one temporal dimension.

The four primary parameters are:
1. **Spatial Resolution** $(W \times H)$ — pixels per frame. *(1 mark)*
2. **Frame Rate** $(F)$ — frames per second. *(1 mark)*
3. **Color Sampling Format / Bit Depth** — e.g., 4:2:0, 8 bits/sample. *(0.5 marks)*
4. **Aspect Ratio** — e.g., 16:9. *(0.5 marks)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is chroma subsampling? Why is 4:2:0 widely used in video compression?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Chroma subsampling is the technique of reducing the spatial resolution of chrominance (Cb, Cr) components relative to luma (Y), exploiting the human visual system's lower sensitivity to color detail. *(1.5 marks)*

In **4:2:0**, across every 2×2 block of luma (4 samples), only one Cb and one Cr sample is retained, giving **12 bits/pixel** instead of 24 — a **2:1 saving** before any other compression. *(1 mark)* It is widely used in **DVD, Blu-ray, streaming (H.264/HEVC)** because the bandwidth saving is significant and visual quality loss is imperceptible for most content. *(0.5 marks)*

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Select ONE)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]

#### **(a)** Explain the **hybrid video coding architecture** used in H.26x/MPEG standards with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the encoder's "decoder loop." (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** The hybrid DPCM/DCT architecture combines **Differential Pulse Code Modulation (DPCM)** in the temporal domain with the **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)** in the spatial domain. It underpins **H.261, H.263, H.264/AVC, H.265/HEVC, MPEG-1, MPEG-2, and MPEG-4**.

**Block-by-block explanation (5 marks):**

1. **Subtractor (–):** Computes the **residual (prediction error)** $R(x,y) = I_t(x,y) - \hat{I}_t(x,y)$ where $\hat{I}_t$ is the motion-compensated prediction.
2. **DCT:** Transforms the residual from spatial to frequency domain. Energy is compacted into low-frequency coefficients, which are easier to compress. Uses an 8×8 (H.261/3) or 4×4/8×8 (H.264) block.
3. **Quantizer (Q):** The **only lossy stage**. Divides DCT coefficients by a quantization step and rounds, throwing away perceptually-insignificant high-frequency detail. Larger $Q$ = smaller file = lower quality.
4. **Entropy Encoder (VLC/CAVLC/CABAC):** Lossless coding — assigns shorter codes to more-probable symbols.
5. **Inverse Quantizer (Q⁻¹), IDCT, Adder (+):** These three blocks **reconstruct the reference frame** that will be used to predict the next frame.
6. **Frame Store (Memory):** Buffers reconstructed frames.
7. **Motion Estimation (ME) & Motion Compensation (MC):** ME searches for the best-matching block in a previous frame within a search window; MC produces $\hat{I}_t$ from the chosen motion vector.

**Why the encoder has a decoder loop (1 mark):** Because the decoder has only *previously-decoded* frames as references. To keep encoder and decoder in **synchronization** and avoid **drift error**, the encoder must use the **same reconstructed (quantized + dequantized)** reference — not the original. This loop guarantees bit-exact reproducibility.

#### **(b)** A 10-second video clip has resolution **640 × 480**, **30 fps**, and is stored using **4:2:2** subsampling at 8 bits/sample. Compute the uncompressed file size in **MB**. If the compressed file size is **5 MB**, find the **compression ratio**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Bits per pixel for 4:2:2** *(1 mark)*
In 4:2:2: For every 4 luma samples, Cb = 2, Cr = 2. Total = 4+2+2 = 8 samples per 4 pixels = **16 bits/pixel**.

**Step 2: Bits per frame** *(1 mark)*
$$
B_{\text{frame}} = 640 \times 480 \times 16 = 4{,}915{,}200 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3: Total bits for 10 seconds at 30 fps** *(1 mark)*
$$
\text{Total frames} = 30 \times 10 = 300
$$

$$
B_{\text{total}} = 300 \times 4{,}915{,}200 = 1{,}474{,}560{,}000 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to MB** *(2 marks)*
$$
\text{Size (bytes)} = \frac{1{,}474{,}560{,}000}{8} = 184{,}320{,}000 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Size (MB)} = \frac{184{,}320{,}000}{1024 \times 1024} = \frac{184{,}320{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx \mathbf{175.78 \text{ MB}}
$$

**Step 5: Compression Ratio** *(2 marks)*
$$
\text{CR} = \frac{175.78 \text{ MB}}{5 \text{ MB}} = \mathbf{35.16 : 1}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

#### **(a)** Explain **motion estimation** and **motion compensation** with block-matching algorithm. List and briefly describe **two fast search algorithms**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Motion Estimation (ME) (2 marks):** The process of finding the **best-matching block** in a reference (previous or future) frame for each macroblock in the current frame. The displacement is encoded as a **Motion Vector (MV)** $(dx, dy)$.

**Motion Compensation (MC) (1 mark):** The process of using the MV to fetch the matched block from the reference frame, producing a **prediction** $\hat{I}_t$ that is subtracted from the original to yield a small **residual** for coding.

**Full Search (Exhaustive) Block Matching (1 mark):**
For every candidate position $(dx, dy)$ in a search window of size $\pm p$ pixels, compute:
$$
\text{SAD}(dx, dy) = \sum_{i=0}^{15}\sum_{j=0}^{15} \vert I_t(i,j) - I_{t-1}(i+dx, j+dy) \vert
$$
The MV is the $(dx, dy)$ minimizing SAD. Complexity: $(2p+1)^2$ candidates per MB. For $p = 16$, this is 1089 candidates — **too slow** for real-time.

**Fast Search Algorithms (3 marks):**

1. **Three-Step Search (TSS):** *(1.5 marks)*
   - **Step 1:** Evaluate 9 points: center + 8 points at step size $S = p/2 = 8$.
   - **Step 2:** Move minimum point as new center; reduce $S \to S/2 = 4$; evaluate 8 neighbors.
   - **Step 3:** Reduce $S \to S/2 = 2$; evaluate 8 neighbors and pick minimum → final MV.
   - Total evaluations: **25** vs. 1089 (full search) — ~43× faster.
   - Drawback: can be trapped in local minima for fast motion.

2. **Diamond Search (DS):** *(1.5 marks)*
   - Uses **Large Diamond Search Pattern (LDSP)** of 9 points (4 inner + 4 outer + center).
   - Center moves to minimum; pattern shrinks to **Small Diamond Search Pattern (SDSP)** of 5 points.
   - Final minimum = MV. More robust than TSS for small motions; used in **H.264/AVC reference software**.

#### **(b)** Differentiate between **I, P, and B frames** in video compression. With a neat diagram, explain a **Group of Pictures (GOP)** of length 12 with the pattern `IBBPBBPBBPBB`. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | I-frame | P-frame | B-frame |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Full name | Intra-coded | Predictive | Bi-directional |
| References | None (self) | One past I/P | Past **and** future I/P |
| Coding | Spatial only (DCT) | Temporal + spatial | Temporal + spatial (two refs) |
| Compression | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| File size | Largest | Medium | Smallest |
| Used for | Random access, GOP start | Forward prediction | Maximum efficiency |
| Error propagation | None | Propagates | Propagates (less) |

**GOP Diagram (2 marks):** *(see Mermaid schematic in Section 4.3)*

For the pattern `IBBPBBPBBPBB` with GOP length 12:
- **1 I-frame** + **3 P-frames** + **8 B-frames** = 12 frames.
- Each P-frame follows a "BB" pair: e.g., I → B → B → P → B → B → P → B → B → P → B → B.
- I-frame is fully self-contained; P-frames reference the previous I/P; B-frames reference the nearest surrounding I/P frames.

**Display order vs. coding order (1 mark):** B-frames need a *future* reference, so they are coded **after** their future reference. The encoder reorders frames to: `I P B B P B B P B B P B B` for coding, while the display order is `I B B P B B P B B P B B`. This requires a **reordering buffer** at both encoder and decoder.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students write **MSE = SSD** (or skip the $1/N^2$ division). The correct relation is $\text{MSE} = \text{SSD} / N^2$. Marks deducted: 1.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** In subsampling calculations, students confuse **samples per 4 pixels** with **samples per 1 pixel**. Always re-derive the bpp for each format.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting that the encoder has a **decoder loop** — this is worth 2 marks in any H.26x/MPEG question.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Using $10^6$ instead of $1024 \times 1024$ (i.e., 1,048,576) for converting bytes to MB. The KTU paper allows both, but if "MB" is asked, **always show the binary conversion explicitly** for full marks.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Confusing **frame rate** (Hz) with **bit rate** (bps). Be precise with units.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Final-minute revision checklist — print, screenshot, or memorize.**

- ✅ Digital video = **sequence of frames** + **spatial** (resolution) + **temporal** (fps) + **color** (YCbCr) dimensions.
- ✅ Standard 8-bit rates: **RGB = 24 bpp**, **4:4:4 = 24 bpp**, **4:2:2 = 16 bpp**, **4:2:0 = 12 bpp**, **4:1:1 = 12 bpp**.
- ✅ Uncompressed bit rate formula: $R = W \times H \times \text{bpp} \times F$.
- ✅ Conversion: 1 byte = 8 bits; 1 MB = $2^{20}$ bytes = **1,048,576 bytes**.
- ✅ **Compression Ratio** = (Uncompressed size) / (Compressed size). Higher = better.
- ✅ **PSNR** = $10 \log_{10}(MAX_I^2 / \text{MSE})$; MSE = SSD/$N^2$.
- ✅ **Hybrid encoder = DPCM (temporal) + DCT (spatial) + Entropy (statistical)**.
- ✅ Encoder must include a **decoder loop** to prevent drift.
- ✅ **I-frame** = intra-coded, anchor, largest. **P-frame** = forward-predicted. **B-frame** = bi-directional, smallest.
- ✅ **GOP length** = number of frames between consecutive I-frames (or I-to-I).
- ✅ **Motion Vector (MV)** = $(dx, dy)$ displacement; reduces to **residual** coding.
- ✅ **SAD** is preferred for real-time ME; **SSD/MSE** for quality.
- ✅ **Three-Step Search** = $25$ evaluations; full search = $(2p+1)^2$.
- ✅ Standards evolution (ascending complexity): **H.261 → MPEG-1 → MPEG-2/H.262 → H.263 → MPEG-4 → H.264/AVC → H.265/HEVC → H.266/VVC**.
- ✅ Hybrid coding is **lossy** (due to quantization) + **lossless** (entropy coding on top).
- ✅ **Random access** = I-frame; **Seek speed** improves with shorter GOP.
- ✅ **Chroma subsampling exploits** human visual system's **2–4× lower chroma acuity** vs. luma.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Compensation, MPEG Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST524 - DATA COMPRESSION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Motion Compensation, MPEG Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Motion Compensation — The Engineering Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Motion Compensation (MC)** is a temporal compression technique used in inter-frame video coding that predicts the contents of a *current* video frame from a previously decoded (reference) frame by estimating and transmitting the displacement of pixels or pixel blocks, called **motion vectors (MV)**. The residual error between the predicted block and the actual block is then DCT-coded.

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats Motion Compensation as the *prediction half* of the inter-frame redundancy removal process. The full operation is actually two coupled processes:

- **Motion Estimation (ME):** the encoder searches the reference frame for the best-matching block (computationally heavy, not standardized).
- **Motion Compensation (MC):** the decoder applies the transmitted motion vector to reconstruct the predicted block (must be standardized for interoperability).

## 1.2 The Real-World Analogy — "Sticker Book Animation"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you are flipping through a child's sticker book. When the child wants to show a ball moving across the page, she does **not** redraw the ball at every new position. Instead, she draws the ball once on a transparent sticker, peels it off the old position, and **translates (moves) it** to the new position. The line "translate 2 cm to the right" is a tiny instruction — much shorter than redrawing the ball. The little smudge left behind where the sticker used to be is the **residual error**.

That is exactly what Motion Compensation does:

| Child's Sticker Book | Motion Compensation |
|---|---|
| Drawn ball (original) | Reference frame pixel block |
| "Move 2 cm right" instruction | 2-D **Motion Vector** |
| Sticker at new spot | Predicted frame block |
| Smudge / misalignment | Residual (DCT-coded) error |

So Motion Compensation turns a difficult "redraw everything" problem into a cheap "tell me where it moved" problem — which is exactly why video codecs achieve **100:1 compression**.

## 1.3 MPEG — The Family of Standards

**MPEG (Moving Picture Experts Group)** is the ISO/IEC working group (JTC 1/SC 29/WG 11) that has defined a layered family of international standards for coded representation of moving pictures, audio, and their combination. The KTU syllabus specifically asks you to know the differences between **MPEG-1, MPEG-2, MPEG-4 (Parts 2 & 10/AVC)**, and the audio/visual system layer.

The big engineering insight behind MPEG is that a video is *not* a stack of independent frames — it has **spatial** redundancy (handled by DCT + quantization, as you studied in Module 2) and **temporal** redundancy (handled by Motion Compensation). MPEG marries the two.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term to Memorize (Bloom Level: Remember):**
> - **Macroblock** = **16 × 16** luminance block + two **8 × 8** chrominance blocks (4:2:0 chroma). It is the smallest unit of motion compensation in MPEG-1/2/4 Part 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Motion Estimation — The Encoder Side

The encoder partitions the current frame $F_t$ into non-overlapping macroblocks of size $N \times N$ (typically $N=16$). For each macroblock $B_t(x, y)$ centered at $(x, y)$, it searches a window of size $(2p+1) \times (2p+1)$ in the previous frame $F_{t-1}$ for the best-matching block.

### 2.1.1 Matching Criteria (Cost Functions)

Three cost functions are standard in the KTU syllabus:

**1. Mean Absolute Difference (MAD):**
$$
\text{MAD}(m,n) \;=\; \frac{1}{N^{2}}\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}\left\vert B_{t}(x+i,\,y+j) \;-\; B_{t-1}(x+m+i,\,y+n+j) \right\vert
$$

**2. Sum of Absolute Differences (SAD):** same as MAD but without the $\frac{1}{N^2}$ factor. SAD is preferred in hardware because the division is skipped.
$$
\text{SAD}(m,n) \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{N-1}\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}\left\vert B_{t}(x+i,\,y+j) \;-\; B_{t-1}(x+m+i,\,y+n+j) \right\vert
$$

**3. Mean Squared Error (MSE):**
$$
\text{MSE}(m,n) \;=\; \frac{1}{N^{2}}\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}\left[\,B_{t}(x+i,\,y+j) \;-\; B_{t-1}(x+m+i,\,y+n+j)\,\right]^{2}
$$

The **motion vector** is the displacement $(m^{\*}, n^{\*})$ that minimizes the cost:
$$
(m^{\*}, n^{\*}) \;=\; \arg\min_{(m,n)\in S}\;\text{MAD}(m,n)
$$
where $S$ is the search window, usually $(-p \le m \le p,\;-p \le n \le p)$ with $p = 7$ or $15$ (i.e., search range $\pm 7$ or $\pm 15$ pixels).

### 2.1.2 Block-Matching Search Algorithms

| Algorithm | Search Points (worst case) | Complexity | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Search (Exhaustive)** | $(2p+1)^2$ | $\mathcal{O}(p^2)$ | Very High |
| **Three-Step Search (TSS)** | $1+8\log_2(p+1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\log p)$ | High |
| **2-D Logarithmic Search** | depends | $\mathcal{O}(\log p)$ | Medium |
| **Diamond Search (DS)** | variable | near-optimal | Low (mostly H.264) |
| **Hexagonal Search** | variable | near-optimal | Low |

### 2.2 Frame Types in MPEG

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The MPEG bitstream contains three principal picture-coding types. Each has different compression efficiency and different access capability.

- **I-frame (Intra-coded picture):** coded using only spatial redundancy (DCT + quantization, no motion compensation). Acts as a random-access point. Compression ratio ≈ **1 : 7**.
- **P-frame (Predictive-coded picture):** motion-compensated prediction from the *previous* I- or P-frame. Compression ratio ≈ **1 : 20**.
- **B-frame (Bidirectionally-predictive picture):** predicted from both the *previous* and *next* I/P frames. Compression ratio ≈ **1 : 50**. Requires frame reordering at the encoder and decoder (I/P must be decoded before B).

The **Group of Pictures (GOP)** is the smallest random-access unit. A typical MPEG-2 GOP is:
$$
GOP = \text{IBBPBBPBBPBB} \quad (N=12,\; M=3)
$$
where $N$ = GOP length, $M$ = distance between consecutive P-frames.

### 2.3 MPEG Layers — The Big Picture

MPEG is decomposed into *layers*, each designed by the standard but free at the next layer:

| Layer | Function | Standardized? |
|---|---|---|
| **Compression Layer** | Coding algorithm details | ❌ Free |
| **System Layer (Multiplex)** | Combine audio + video + timing | ✅ Fixed |
| **Transport / Sync Layer** | Clock recovery, framing | ✅ Fixed |

This separation is what lets every brand of DVD player play every DVD — the syntax and timing are standard, but the *internal* compression tricks may differ.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table verbatim — these are the formulas that reappear in every KTU ESE paper.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Macroblock size | $16 \times 16$ luma + $8 \times 8 \times 2$ chroma | Pixels |
| 2 | Block matching error (SAD) | $\text{SAD}(m,n) = \sum\sum \vert I_t - I_{t-1} \vert$ | Sum of abs. diffs |
| 3 | MAD normalization | $\text{MAD} = \text{SAD}/N^2$ | 0–255 average |
| 4 | MSE | $\frac{1}{N^2}\sum\sum (I_t - I_{t-1})^2$ | squared intensity |
| 5 | PSNR | $20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{255}{\sqrt{\text{MSE}}}\right)$ dB | 30–50 dB typical |
| 6 | Motion vector bits | $\lceil\log_2(2p+1)\rceil \times 2$ | bits per MV |
| 7 | Compression ratio | $R_c = \dfrac{\text{Original size}}{\text{Compressed size}}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | MPEG-1 bit-rate | up to **1.5 Mbps** | CD-ROM |
| 9 | MPEG-2 main profile | **4 – 15 Mbps** SDTV | DVD / DVB |
| 10 | MPEG-4 ASP target | **64 – 1024 kbps** | Internet streaming |
| 11 | Frame rate | 25 fps (PAL) / 29.97 fps (NTSC) | fps |
| 12 | B-frame memory requirement | 2 reference frames | I+P |

### Real-World Utility

Motion compensation + DCT is the foundation of **every** modern video codec — MPEG-1, MPEG-2 (used in every DVD and DVB-T broadcast in India), MPEG-4 Part 2 (DivX, Xvid), MPEG-4 Part 10 / H.264 (YouTube, Blu-ray, DTH), and HEVC (4K Netflix). Without these algorithms, a single 90-minute movie would be **> 100 GB** instead of ~700 MB on a CD or ~4–8 GB on a DVD.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Full-Search Block Matching — Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Pattern, Bloom Level: Apply):**
> Search for the motion vector of a 2 × 2 block with $p = 1$ (so a 3 × 3 search window). The reference-block intensity values and the candidate offsets are given. Use **MAD** as the cost function.

### Step 1 — Define the current block $B_t$

The current frame block (2 × 2) is:
$$
B_{t} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 100 & 110 \\ 90 & 120 \end{bmatrix}
$$
Centered at $(x, y)$, with block size $N = 2$.

### Step 2 — Define the search window in $F_{t-1}$

The 3 × 3 search window centred at the same position in the previous frame is:

$$
W \;=\; \begin{bmatrix}
80 & 95 & 105 \\
75 & 100 & 115 \\
85 & 95 & 125
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The candidate displacements are $(m, n) \in \{-1, 0, +1\} \times \{-1, 0, +1\}$.

### Step 3 — Compute the MAD for each candidate

We must compute $\text{MAD}(m, n) = \frac{1}{4}\sum_{i=0}^{1}\sum_{j=0}^{1}\left\vert B_t(i,j) - W(m+1+i,\, n+1+j) \right\vert$.

**Candidate $(m, n) = (0, 0)$ — the centre.**

Pick the 2 × 2 sub-block of $W$ centred at index (1,1):
$$
W_{0,0} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 95 & 105 \\ 100 & 115 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute the absolute differences:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\vert 100 - 95 \right\vert &= 5 \\
\left\vert 110 - 105 \right\vert &= 5 \\
\left\vert 90 - 100 \right\vert &= 10 \\
\left\vert 120 - 115 \right\vert &= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum = $5 + 5 + 10 + 5 = 25$. MAD = $25 / 4 = \mathbf{6.25}$.

**Candidate $(m, n) = (1, 0)$ — shift right by 1.**

Take the 2 × 2 sub-block starting at $W$ row 1, column 2:
$$
W_{1,0} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 105 & ? \\ 115 & ? \end{bmatrix}
$$
But the 2 × 2 sub-block needs two columns, so it wraps: columns 2 and 0 (mod 3 in toy example). Take columns 2 and 0:
$$
W_{1,0} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 105 & 95 \\ 115 & 100 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\vert 100 - 105 \right\vert &= 5 \\
\left\vert 110 - 95 \right\vert &= 15 \\
\left\vert 90 - 115 \right\vert &= 25 \\
\left\vert 120 - 100 \right\vert &= 20
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum = $5 + 15 + 25 + 20 = 65$. MAD = $65 / 4 = \mathbf{16.25}$.

**Candidate $(m, n) = (-1, 0)$ — shift left by 1.**

Sub-block columns 0 and 1, rows 1 and 2:
$$
W_{-1,0} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 100 & 115 \\ 95 & 125 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\vert 100 - 100 \right\vert &= 0 \\
\left\vert 110 - 115 \right\vert &= 5 \\
\left\vert 90 - 95 \right\vert &= 5 \\
\left\vert 120 - 125 \right\vert &= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum = $0 + 5 + 5 + 5 = 15$. MAD = $15 / 4 = \mathbf{3.75}$.

### Step 4 — Pick the minimum

The candidate MADs we computed so far:
- $(0, 0) \to 6.25$
- $(1, 0) \to 16.25$
- $(-1, 0) \to 3.75$ ✅ **Minimum**

### Step 5 — Conclude the Motion Vector

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{MV} = (m^{\*}, n^{\*}) = (-1, 0)}
$$

The motion vector points to the **left by 1 pixel**, meaning the block in the current frame has shifted leftward compared to the previous frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** The examiner awards full marks *only* if you show the SAD/MAD arithmetic for *at least* 3 candidate positions and explicitly state $\arg\min$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Bit-Budget of MPEG-2

For a standard MPEG-2 SDTV stream at 4 Mbps with 25 fps, frame size 720 × 576, GOP $N = 12$, $M = 3$:

| Frame Type | Count in GOP | Avg. bits/frame (≈) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | 1 | 400 000 | Highest bit cost (no MC) |
| P | 3 | 200 000 | Forward MC only |
| B | 8 | 80 000 | Bidirectional MC, lowest cost |
| **GOP total** | **12 frames** | **≈ 1 944 000 bits** | @ 25 fps ≈ 4.86 Mbps |

The B-frame's bit cost is ~5× lower than the I-frame, which is *exactly* why modern codecs keep increasing the proportion of B-frames (HEVC allows **forward + backward + bidirectional**).

## 3.3 Analytic Derivation: Number of Motion-Vector Bits per Frame

For a CIF frame (352 × 288) with 16 × 16 macroblocks, and search range $\pm p$ pixels:

$$
\text{Number of MBs} \;=\; \frac{352}{16}\times\frac{288}{16} \;=\; 22 \times 18 \;=\; 396
$$

Each MV has horizontal + vertical components. Each component needs $\log_2(2p+1)$ bits.

$$
\text{Bits per MV} \;=\; 2 \times \lceil\log_2(2p+1)\rceil
$$

For $p = 7$: $\lceil\log_2(15)\rceil = 4$ bits per component ⇒ **8 bits per MV**.

$$
\text{Total MV bits per frame} \;=\; 396 \times 8 \;=\; 3168 \text{ bits}
$$

At 25 fps: $3168 \times 25 = 79{,}200$ bits/sec ≈ **0.08 Mbps** for MV side info only — confirming that MV overhead is negligible compared to residual DCT data.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MPEG-2 Hybrid Encoder Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Current Frame Ft] --> fmode{Frame Type}
    fmode -- I-Frame --> dctIn1[Intra Mode]
    dmode{Frame Type Decoder}
    fmode -- P-Frame --> mcIn[Subtract MC Prediction]
    fmode -- B-Frame --> mcIn
    refA[Previous Reconstructed Frame] --> me[Motion Estimation]
    refB[Future Reconstructed Frame] --> me
    srcA --> me
    me --> mvOut[Side Info MV]
    mcIn --> errRes[Residual Error]
    dctIn1 --> dctQ1[DCT + Quantizer]
    errRes --> dctQ1
    dctQ1 --> vlc[Variable Length Coder]
    vlc --> bitstream[MPEG Bitstream]
    mvOut --> bitstream
    dctQ1 --> dctIQ[Inverse Quant + IDCT]
    dctIQ --> recFrame[Local Reconstructed Frame]
    recFrame --> refStore[Reference Store Buffer]
    refStore --> refA
    refStore --> refB
```

## 4.2 Block-Matching Search Pattern Comparison

```mermaid
    end

    end

```

## 4.3 MPEG Frame Dependency Graph (GOP = I B B P B B P B B P B B)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I0[I-Frame t=0]
    I12[I-Frame t=12]

    B10[B-Frame t=10]

    I0 --> P3
    I0 --> B1
    I0 --> B2
    P3 --> B4
    P3 --> B5

    P9 --> B10
    P9 --> B11

    I0 -.bidirectional.-> B1
    I0 -.bidirectional.-> B2
    P3 -.bidirectional.-> B4
    P3 -.bidirectional.-> B5
    P6 -.bidirectional.-> B7
    P6 -.bidirectional.-> B8
    P9 -.bidirectional.-> B10
    P9 -.bidirectional.-> B11
```

## 4.4 MPEG Standards Family — Timeline Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    m1["MPEG-1 1993 VCD 1.5 Mbps"]:::a
    m2["MPEG-2 1995 DVD DTV 4-15 Mbps"]:::b
    m4p2["MPEG-4 Pt2 1999 DivX Streaming"]:::c
    m4p10["MPEG-4 Pt10 AVC H.264 2003 Blu-ray"]:::d
    m7["MPEG-7 2002 Metadata Search"]:::e
    m21["MPEG-21 2004 Multimedia Framework"]:::f
    hevc["HEVC H.265 2013 4K 50 percent less"]:::g

    m1 --> m2 --> m4p2 --> m4p10 --> hevc
    m4p10 --> m7 --> m21

    classDef a fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    classDef b fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    classDef c fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    classDef d fill:#d62728,color:#fff

    classDef f fill:#8c564b,color:#fff
```
