
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Explain the difference between motion estimation and motion compensation. Which one is standardized in MPEG?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Motion Estimation (ME)** is the process by which the *encoder* searches a reference frame to find the best-matching block for each macroblock of the current frame. The output is the **motion vector (MV)** — a 2-D displacement $(m, n)$ that minimizes the matching cost (MAD/MSE). ME is **computationally expensive** and is left *unspecified* by MPEG.
>
> **Motion Compensation (MC)** is the *decoder-side reconstruction* process in which the transmitted MV is applied to the reference frame to synthesize the prediction block. This is *standardized* in MPEG so that every decoder produces identical reconstructed frames. Together, ME + MC form the predictive loop that exploits **temporal redundancy**, while DCT + quantization (Module 2) exploit *spatial* redundancy.
>
> **[1 mark each for ME, MC, and standardization statement.]**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*List any three differences between MPEG-1 and MPEG-2.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | MPEG-1 | MPEG-2 |
|---|---|---|
| Target application | VCD, CD-ROM storage | DVD, SDTV/HDTV broadcast |
| Bit rate | up to **1.5 Mbps** | **4 – 15 Mbps** SDTV; up to 80 Mbps HDTV |
| Coding tools | I, P, B frames; DCT-based hybrid | Adds scalable profiles, interlaced support |
| Chroma sampling | 4:2:0 only | 4:2:0, 4:2:2, 4:4:4 |
| Compression efficiency | lower | higher (≈ 30% better at same quality) |
| Profiles/Levels | none | 5 profiles × 4 levels (e.g., Main @ Main) |

**[1 mark per valid difference.]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, describe the **MPEG video compression** process. Explain the role of each functional block.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 4 × 4 block in the current frame has intensity values:
$$
B_t = \begin{bmatrix} 50 & 60 & 70 & 80 \\ 55 & 65 & 75 & 85 \\ 60 & 70 & 80 & 90 \\ 65 & 75 & 85 & 95 \end{bmatrix}
$$
The same-position 4 × 4 block in the previous frame is:
$$
B_{t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 52 & 62 & 72 & 82 \\ 57 & 67 & 77 & 87 \\ 62 & 72 & 82 & 92 \\ 67 & 77 & 87 & 97 \end{bmatrix}
$$
Compute the motion vector for a search range $p = 0$ using the **SAD** matching criterion.

---

### Solution to Question A

**(a) MPEG Video Compression Process [7 Marks]**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MPEG is a hybrid coder** — it combines **spatial** (DCT) and **temporal** (MC) redundancy removal.

**Block-by-block explanation (1 mark each, 7 blocks):**

1. **Frame Reorder Buffer** — accepts input frames in display order, reorders them into encoding/decoding order (B-frames require future I/P reference, so they are stored until references arrive).
2. **Motion Estimation (ME)** — the encoder partitions the current frame into 16 × 16 macroblocks and searches a window in the reference frame. Outputs motion vector MV = $(m^*, n^*) = \arg\min_{m,n}\text{SAD}$.
3. **Motion-Compensated Predictor** — using the chosen MV, the matched block from the previous reconstructed frame is fetched. The predictor is subtracted from the current macroblock to produce the **residual error**.
4. **DCT Block** — applies 8 × 8 Discrete Cosine Transform on each residual block. Concentrates energy in a few low-frequency coefficients.
5. **Quantizer (Q)** — divides each DCT coefficient by a quantizer step size; throws away perceptually unimportant high-frequency bits. This is the *primary* lossy step.
6. **Variable-Length Coder (VLC)** — runs zig-zag scan + run-length coding + Huffman to produce a compact bitstream.
7. **Local Decoder (Q⁻¹ + IDCT) + Reference Store** — the encoder contains a *local decoder* identical to the player's decoder. The reconstructed frame is stored for use as the next reference, ensuring encoder-decoder drift does not accumulate.

**(b) Motion Vector Computation [7 Marks]**

Since $p = 0$, there is **only one** candidate displacement $(m, n) = (0, 0)$.

**Step 1 — Subtract the two blocks element-wise.** The residual:
$$
B_t - B_{t-1} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -2 & -2 & -2 \\ -2 & -2 & -2 & -2 \\ -2 & -2 & -2 & -2 \\ -2 & -2 & -2 & -2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Take absolute values.**
$$
\left\vert B_t - B_{t-1} \right\vert \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum all 16 elements.**
$$
\text{SAD} \;=\; 16 \times 2 \;=\; 32
$$

**Step 4 — State the Motion Vector.**
$$
\boxed{\mathbf{MV} = (0, 0)}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** With $p=0$, only the zero-displacement was tested, and the block happens to match closely (only a constant +2 intensity offset). The motion vector is the zero vector; the residual (a constant DC of −2) will still be DCT-coded and quantized, allowing perfect reconstruction up to quantization error.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three picture-coding types** in MPEG (I, P, B frames). How does the presence of B-frames improve compression efficiency? Mention the trade-off.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 16 × 16 macroblock is searched using **Full Search** with a search window $p = 1$ (i.e., $\pm 1$ pixel, 9 candidate positions). For each candidate, the SAD values are:

| $(m, n)$ | SAD |
|---|---|
| (0, 0) | 245 |
| (1, 0) | 180 |
| (–1, 0) | 310 |
| (0, 1) | 215 |
| (0, –1) | 290 |
| (1, 1) | 410 |
| (–1, –1) | 195 |
| (1, –1) | 270 |
| (–1, 1) | 360 |

Determine: (i) the **optimal motion vector**, (ii) the **MAD** value, (iii) the **MSE** if the corresponding residual pixel differences were $[-2, +3, -1, +4]$ (assume N² = 4), and (iv) the **PSNR**.

---

### Solution to Question B

**(a) Three Picture-Coding Types [7 Marks]**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MPEG's compression efficiency comes from giving each frame the right "type" based on its role in the temporal chain.**

**I-frame (Intra-coded picture) [2 marks]**
- Encoded using **only spatial redundancy** (DCT + quantization).
- Acts as a **random-access point** — playback can start from any I-frame.
- Compression ratio ≈ **1 : 7**, lowest among the three.
- Does **not** depend on any other frame.

**P-frame (Predictive-coded picture) [2 marks]**
- Uses **motion-compensated prediction** from the most recent *previous* I- or P-frame (forward prediction).
- The MV describes "where did each macroblock come from in the past".
- Compression ratio ≈ **1 : 20**.
- Errors propagate from one P to the next (drift chain).

**B-frame (Bidirectionally-predictive picture) [2 marks]**
- Uses **two** reference frames — the previous AND the next I/P frame.
- Two MVs per macroblock (forward + backward). Can also use **direct** mode (interpolated MV).
- Highest compression ≈ **1 : 50**, because the average of past and future frames is a better predictor than either alone (occlusion handling).
- Trade-off (1 mark): requires **frame reordering** at both encoder and decoder, increases **latency** (must wait for future reference frame), and **doubles the reference-frame memory** needed.

**(b) Full-Search Block-Matching Analytics [7 Marks]**

**(i) Optimal Motion Vector [2 marks]**

Scan the table for the minimum SAD.

Minimum SAD = **180** at $(m, n) = (1, 0)$.

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{MV} = (1, 0)}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** the macroblock moved **1 pixel to the right** from the previous frame.

**(ii) MAD Value [1 mark]**

For a $16 \times 16$ macroblock, $N^2 = 256$.

$$
\text{MAD} \;=\; \frac{\text{SAD}}{N^2} \;=\; \frac{180}{256} \;=\; \mathbf{0.703}
$$

**(iii) MSE [2 marks]**

Given residual differences $r = [-2, +3, -1, +4]$, and $N^2 = 4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{4} r_i^{2} &= (-2)^2 + (3)^2 + (-1)^2 + (4)^2 \\
&= 4 + 9 + 1 + 16 \\
&= 30
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{MSE} \;=\; \frac{1}{N^2}\sum r_i^{2} \;=\; \frac{30}{4} \;=\; \mathbf{7.5}
$$

**(iv) PSNR [2 marks]**

$$
\text{RMSE} \;=\; \sqrt{\text{MSE}} \;=\; \sqrt{7.5} \;\approx\; 2.739
$$

$$
\text{PSNR} \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{255}{2.739}\right) \;\approx\; 20 \times \log_{10}(93.10) \;\approx\; 20 \times 1.969 \;\approx\; \mathbf{39.4 \text{ dB}}
$$

A PSNR ≥ 30 dB indicates acceptable broadcast quality, and ≥ 40 dB indicates visually lossless quality.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic**
>
> 1. **Confusing ME and MC.** Students write "ME is decoder-side, MC is encoder-side." This is *inverted* — ME happens in the **encoder**, MC happens in both encoder (prediction) and decoder (reconstruction).
> 2. **Forgetting the residual.** Motion compensation **does not eliminate the residual**. The predicted block is subtracted from the actual block, and the residual is still coded (typically via DCT). Always mention this in long answers.
> 3. **Wrong MV range.** The search window is $\pm p$ (so $2p+1$ positions per axis). Do not confuse $p$ with the half-width.
> 4. **B-frame memory.** Students forget that B-frames need **two** reference buffers (forward + backward). One mark is reserved for this trade-off point.
> 5. **PSNR units.** Always write "dB" after PSNR. Writing "PSNR = 39.4" without units is treated as incomplete by KTU examiners.
> 6. **MAD vs SAD notation.** If the question says MAD but you compute SAD without dividing by $N^2$, you lose 1 mark. Read the cost function carefully.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Motion Compensation & MPEG Standards**

- **Motion Compensation** = MC = prediction using transmitted motion vectors; works on **macroblocks of 16 × 16** luma + 8 × 8 × 2 chroma.
- **Motion Estimation** = encoder-only search; uses cost functions **SAD, MAD, MSE**.
- **Three Frame Types:** I (intra, random access, ~1:7), P (forward MC, ~1:20), B (bidirectional MC, ~1:50, but needs frame reordering).
- **GOP (Group of Pictures):** smallest random-access unit; example pattern is **IBBPBBPBBPBB** with $N = 12$, $M = 3$.
- **MPEG is a hybrid coder** combining DCT (spatial) + MC (temporal).
- **MPEG-1 (1993):** VCD, 1.5 Mbps, 4:2:0, no interlacing.
- **MPEG-2 (1995):** DVD, 4–15 Mbps SDTV, supports 4:2:2/4:4:4, profiles & levels, broadcast standard.
- **MPEG-4 Part 2 (1999):** DivX/Xvid, object-based coding, low-bitrate streaming.
- **MPEG-4 Part 10 / H.264 (2003):** Blu-ray, YouTube, 50% better than MPEG-2 at same quality.
- **HEVC / H.265 (2013):** 4K/8K video, ~50% better than H.264.
- **MPEG-7** = metadata / content description, **MPEG-21** = multimedia framework.
- **Full Search** = exhaustive, $O(p^2)$; **Three-Step Search** = logarithmic, $O(\log p)$.
- **Bidirectional prediction** removes **occlusion artefacts** better than forward-only.
- **PSNR** = $20\log_{10}(255/\sqrt{\text{MSE}})$ dB; values ≥ 30 dB acceptable, ≥ 40 dB visually lossless.
- **MC overhead is tiny**: at $p = 7$, MV side-info ≈ 0.08 Mbps of a 4 Mbps stream.
- **Frame reorder at encoder/decoder is mandatory** for B-frames (display order ≠ decode order).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: H.261
topics.content:
# H.261 — The Pioneer of Digital Video Telephony ## 1. Formal Academic Definition **H.261** is the first member of the ITU-T **H.26x family** of video coding standards, formally titled *"Video Codec for Audiovisual Services at p×64 kbit/s"*. Approved in **1990** (revised in 1993), H.261 was designed by the **International Telegraph and Telephone Consultative Committee (CCITT, now ITU-T)** to enable real-time digital video telephony and video conferencing over **Integrated Services Digital Network (ISDN)** channels. The standard specifies a **hybrid video coding architecture** that combines: 1. **Inter-picture prediction** (Motion Compensation) for temporal redundancy removal. 2. **2-D Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)** for spatial redundancy removal. 3. **Quantization + Entropy Coding (VLC)** for statistical redundancy removal. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** H.261 is mandatory in Module 3 of *Data Compression (PECST524)*. Examiner focus is on the **Hybrid Coding Framework**, the **Macroblock structure**, the **CIF/QCIF picture formats**, and the **p×64 kbps bitrate grammar**. Memorizing the codec's hierarchical block layout (Picture → GOB → Macroblock → Block) is the single highest-weight topic for Part B questions. | Parameter | Specification | |---|---| | **Standard Body** | ITU-T (formerly CCITT) | | **Year of Approval** | 1990 (Rev 1), 1993 (Rev 2) | | **Target Bitrate** | **40 kbps to 2 Mbps** (p×64, p = 1, 2, ..., 30) | | **Target Network** | ISDN (p×64 kbps channels) | | **Coding Type** | Hybrid (Motion Compensation + DCT) | | **Picture Format** | CIF (352×288) and QCIF (176×144) | | **Frame Rate** | Up to 30 fps (CIF for 625-line, 30 fps for 525-line) | | **Motion Vector Precision** | Integer pixel (±15 range) | | **Transform** | 8×8 DCT | | **Entropy Coding** | Variable Length Coding (VLC) | ## 2. Intuitive Analogy — The "Sticky-Note Wall" Imagine you are a cartoonist tasked with transmitting a comic strip to a colleague in another country via fax — one tiny strip at a time, paying by the inch. - **Picture (Frame)**: One complete panel of your comic (the whole drawing). - **Macroblock (16×16)**: A small square patch of the panel (say, one face or one fist). - **Motion Vector**: An arrow sticky-note saying *"this face is just the previous face, but shifted 3 squares to the right."* — instead of redrawing the face, you only send the **arrow**. - **DCT Residual**: The *tiny differences* between the predicted patch and the actual patch (e.g., the eyebrow raised an extra millimeter). - **Quantization**: Throwing away the imperceptible scribbles. *"Good enough!"* - **VLC (Variable Length Coding)**: Writing common sticky-notes in short abbreviations (**MV → 4 bits**) and rare ones in long sentences. This is the courier's postage efficiency. > [!NOTE] > **The Core Insight:** H.261 realized that to compress video dramatically, you must exploit *three* dimensions of redundancy — **spatial** (within a frame, via DCT), **temporal** (across frames, via motion), and **statistical** (via entropy coding). The "hybrid" terminology means the standard is a *hybrid of these three techniques*. This architecture outlived H.261 itself and is the blueprint for *every modern codec* including H.264, H.265 (HEVC), and VVC. --- # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 1. The Hybrid Coding Framework — Operational Pipeline The H.261 encoder consists of a **Differential Pulse Code Modulation (DPCM) loop coupled with a DCT transform coder**. Below is the structural breakdown of every operation the encoder performs on a single Macroblock (MB). ### Step 1 — Frame Partitioning The input frame is divided into hierarchical units: $$ \text{Picture} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Group of Blocks (GOB)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Macroblock (MB)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Block} $$ - **GOB**: 11 (CIF) or 3 (QCIF) macroblock rows of width 11 MBs. - **Macroblock (MB)**: 16×16 luma + 8×8 Cb + 8×8 Cr = **6 blocks (4Y + 1Cb + 1Cr)**. - **Block**: 8×8 pixels — the unit of DCT. ### Step 2 — Macroblock Decision: Intra vs. Inter? The encoder applies a *conditional* rule for every MB: - If motion-compensated prediction error is *large* → code as **INTRA** (force I-mode for that MB). - Otherwise → code as **INTER** (P-mode, send MV + residual). > [!NOTE] > **Why intra-refresh?** Without forced I-blocks, prediction errors would accumulate forever (drift). H.261 mandates that every MB be available in *intra* mode periodically. ### Step 3 — Motion Estimation & Compensation - Reference frame: **the previously decoded frame** (reconstructed from local decoder, not the original). - Search range: **±15 pixels** in both horizontal and vertical directions. - Vector precision: **Integer pixel only** (H.261 cannot do half-pel like H.263/MPEG). - Zero MV is allowed; MV outside frame is clipped to the boundary. ### Step 4 — DCT (Discrete Cosine Transform) - Applied to the **8×8 prediction error block** (in INTER mode) or the **8×8 luma block** (in INTRA mode). - The 2D-DCT formula is: $$ F(u,v) = \frac{C(u)\,C(v)}{4} \sum_{x=0}^{7} \sum_{y=0}^{7} f(x,y) \cos\!\left[\frac{(2x+1)u\pi}{16}\right] \cos\!\left[\frac{(2y+1)v\pi}{16}\right] $$ where the normalization constants are $C(0) = 1/\sqrt{2}$ and $C(k) = 1$ for $k \neq 0$. ### Step 5 — Quantization The DCT coefficients are quantized by simple uniform mid-tread quantization: $$ \text{COF}'(u,v) = \text{round}\!\left[\frac{\text{COF}(u,v)}{2\,Q}\right] $$ where **Q** is the quantizer step (1 to 31) controlled by the rate-buffer feedback. Coefficients equal to 0 are flagged as "no coefficient." Chrominance uses the same quantizer. ### Step 6 — Entropy Coding (VLC) - **Motion Vectors** are VLC-coded using a fixed table. - **DCT coefficients** are zig-zag scanned, then run-length + amplitude coded (similar to JPEG's Huffman tables). ### Step 7 — Loop Filter (Optional) A simple 2-D **spatial low-pass filter** (3-tap, coefficients 1/4, 1/2, 1/4) can be applied to the reconstructed frame in INTER mode to reduce mosquito noise. The filter state is signaled in the picture header. ## 2. The p×64 kbps Bitrate Grammar The defining parameter of H.261 is the **p** factor: $$ R = p \times 64 \text{ kbps} \quad \text{where} \quad p \in \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, 30\} $$ - **p = 1 (64 kbps)**: Barely acceptable face/head-and-shoulders video. - **p = 2 (128 kbps)**: Acceptable video quality. - **p ≥ 4 (≥ 256 kbps)**: Good quality. - **p = 30 (1920 kbps)**: Near-broadcast quality. > [!TIP] > **KTU Memory Trick — "P-Thirty Rule":** Remember that H.261 supports a **maximum of 30 × 64 = 1920 kbps ≈ 2 Mbps**. This is the answer boundary for *every* bitrate-related numerical in university exams. ## 3. Picture Format Math — CIF vs. QCIF | Format | Luma Resolution | Chroma Resolution | GOBs | MBs/Frame | Bitrate/p | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **CIF** | 352 × 288 | 176 × 144 (4:2:0) | **12** | 396 | 2 to 30 | | **QCIF** | 176 × 144 | 88 × 72 (4:2:0) | **3** | 99 | 1 to 30 | $$ \text{MBs in CIF} = \frac{352}{16} \times \frac{288}{16} = 22 \times 18 = 396 $$ $$ \text{MBs in QCIF} = \frac{176}{16} \times \frac{144}{16} = 11 \times 9 = 99 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Note:** CIF has **2 GOB columns × 6 GOB rows = 12 GOBs** *only* for CIF 625-line. For 525-line CIF, the layout is 2 × 5 = 10 GOBs (no, actually H.261 CIF is fixed at 352×288 with 12 GOBs; the 525 vs 625 distinction only affects frame rate framing). **Do not confuse** this with H.263 Sub-QCIF/QCIF/CIF/4CIF/16CIF family. ## 4. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Notes / Units | |---|---|---| | H.261 bitrate | $R = p \times 64$ kbps | p = 1 to 30 | | Bitrate range | 64 kbps to 1920 kbps | Fixed | | Macroblock size (luma) | 16 × 16 | 4 sub-blocks of 8×8 | | Macroblock size (chroma) | 8 × 8 (Cb) + 8 × 8 (Cr) | 4:2:0 sampling | | Blocks per MB | 6 | 4Y + 1Cb + 1Cr | | DCT transform size | 8 × 8 | Always integer pixel | | Motion vector precision | 1 pixel | Integer only | | MV search range | $\pm 15$ pixels | Both x and y | | Quantization | $\text{COF}' = \text{round}\!\left[\dfrac{\text{COF}}{2Q}\right]$ | Q = 1..31 | | Inverse quant step | $\text{COF} = 2\,Q \cdot \text{COF}' + \text{sign}(\text{COF}') \cdot Q$ | Mid-tread | | CIF MBs per frame | 396 | 22 cols × 18 rows | | QCIF MBs per frame | 99 | 11 cols × 9 rows | | CIF GOBs per frame | 12 | 2 × 6 layout | | QCIF GOBs per frame | 3 | 1 × 3 layout | | Loop filter taps | $[1/4, 1/2, 1/4]$ | Optional, intra MB = no filter | | Frame rate (625-line) | 25 fps | PAL regions | | Frame rate (525-line) | 29.97 fps | NTSC regions | | Chroma subsampling | 4:2:0 | Only mode in H.261 | | VLC table entries (MV) | Huffman-like, fixed | DC separate table | | DC coefficient size | 8 bits, VLC coded | First zig-zag coefficient | | AC coefficient coding | Run/Level/End pairs | Same as JPEG | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** H.261's architecture directly powers **video calling on legacy ISDN networks, early 3G-324M videophones, and embedded systems** in the late 1990s and early 2000s. The structural idea (hybrid coding, MB partitioning, intra/inter switch) is *still* the standard's backbone in **Zoom, Teams, and WhatsApp video calls** today, although they use H.264/HEVC at higher resolutions. --- # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section derives and verifies every formula and quantity a KTU examiner is likely to test. ## 1. Derivation — Number of Macroblocks in CIF & QCIF ### 1.1 CIF Computation The CIF luma resolution is $352 \times 288$ pixels. Each macroblock covers a $16 \times 16$ luma region. Hence: $$ N_{\text{MB, CIF}} = \frac{\text{Width}}{16} \times \frac{\text{Height}}{16} $$ $$ N_{\text{MB, CIF}} = \frac{352}{16} \times \frac{288}{16} $$ **Step 1** — Compute horizontal MBs: $$ \frac{352}{16} = 22 \text{ macroblocks per row} $$ **Step 2** — Compute vertical MBs: $$ \frac{288}{16} = 18 \text{ macroblocks per column} $$ **Step 3** — Total macroblocks: $$ N_{\text{MB, CIF}} = 22 \times 18 = 396 \text{ macroblocks} $$ ### 1.2 QCIF Computation QCIF luma resolution is $176 \times 144$ pixels. $$ N_{\text{MB, QCIF}} = \frac{176}{16} \times \frac{144}{16} $$ **Step 1** — Horizontal: $$ \frac{176}{16} = 11 $$ **Step 2** — Vertical: $$ \frac{144}{16} = 9 $$ **Step 3** — Total: $$ N_{\text{MB, QCIF}} = 11 \times 9 = 99 \text{ macroblocks} $$ ### 1.3 GOB Counts Derivation A GOB is **11 macroblocks wide × 1 macroblock row tall** in CIF (and the same width in QCIF). **CIF GOBs:** $$ \text{GOBs}_{\text{CIF}} = \frac{18 \text{ rows}}{1 \text{ row/GOB}} \times \frac{22 \text{ MBs/row}}{11 \text{ MBs/GOB}} $$ Wait — this would over-count. The correct definition is: a GOB = $\frac{1}{2}$ of the picture in CIF, stacked 6 vertically. Re-derivation: **Width of one GOB in MBs:** $$ W_{\text{MB}} = \frac{\text{Picture width in MBs}}{2} = \frac{22}{2} = 11 \text{ MBs} $$ **Number of GOBs (vertical) for CIF:** $$ H_{\text{GOB}} = \frac{18 \text{ MBs}}{3 \text{ MBs/GOB}} = 6 $$ $$ \text{Total GOBs}_{\text{CIF}} = 2 \times 6 = 12 $$ **For QCIF:** $$ W_{\text{MB, QCIF}} = \frac{11}{1} = 11 \text{ MBs (full width is one GOB in QCIF)} $$ $$ H_{\text{GOB, QCIF}} = \frac{9}{3} = 3 $$ $$ \text{Total GOBs}_{\text{QCIF}} = 1 \times 3 = 3 $$ ## 2. Derivation — Number of 8×8 Blocks per Frame Each macroblock contains **6 blocks** (4Y + 1 Cb + 1 Cr). ### CIF Total Blocks $$ N_{\text{blocks, CIF}} = 396 \times 6 = 2376 \text{ blocks/frame} $$ ### QCIF Total Blocks $$ N_{\text{blocks, QCIF}} = 99 \times 6 = 594 \text{ blocks/frame} $$ ## 3. Derivation — DCT Coefficient Count and DC Coding The 8×8 DCT yields **64 coefficients per block**: 1 DC + 63 AC. ### DC Coefficient Quantization The DC coefficient is the spatial mean of the block. In H.261, the DC is coded using a **fixed 8-bit VLC table** with a uniform step of 8: $$ \text{DC}_{\text{coded}} = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{\text{DC}_{\text{raw}}}{8}\right) $$ The DC is *always* transmitted (it carries the absolute brightness level), even when all AC coefficients are zero. ### AC Coefficient Zig-Zag Order Coefficients are read in zig-zag order (low-frequency first) to maximize run-length efficiency: $$ \text{Order:} (0,0) \to (0,1) \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (1,1) \to (0,2) \to \cdots \to (7,7) $$ This packs non-zero low-frequency coefficients first, exploiting the fact that natural images have most energy concentrated in the low-frequency region. ## 4. Derivation — Quantization and Inverse Quantization The H.261 quantizer is "uniform mid-tread" with no dead-zone. For a non-zero coefficient: $$ \text{COF}'(i,j) = \text{sgn}\!\left(\text{COF}(i,j)\right) \cdot \left\lfloor \frac{\vert \text{COF}(i,j)\vert - Q}{2Q} + 0.5 \right\rfloor $$ **Inverse (dequantization) at decoder:** $$ \widehat{\text{COF}}(i,j) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } \text{COF}'(i,j) = 0 \\[4pt] 2\,Q \cdot \text{COF}'(i,j) + \text{sgn}(\text{COF}'(i,j)) \cdot Q & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ ### Numerical Example Let the raw DCT coefficient be $\text{COF} = 47$ and quantizer step $Q = 4$. **Step 1** — Quantize: $$ \text{COF}' = \text{round}\!\left[\frac{47}{2 \cdot 4}\right] = \text{round}\!\left[\frac{47}{8}\right] = \text{round}(5.875) = 6 $$ **Step 2** — Dequantize (decode): $$ \widehat{\text{COF}} = 2 \cdot 4 \cdot 6 + 1 \cdot 4 = 48 + 4 = 52 $$ **Step 3** — Quantization error: $$ \Delta = \widehat{\text{COF}} - \text{COF} = 52 - 47 = 5 $$ The error $\Delta$ satisfies $\vert\Delta\vert \leq Q = 4$ only if the rounding behaves exactly. Here $\Delta = 5$ is slightly larger, which is an artifact of the mid-tread vs. mid-rise convention used. KTU students should remember that **the error is always bounded by $\pm Q$** for any standard rounding rule. ## 5. Derivation — Motion Vector Search Range and Total MV Codes ### 5.1 MV Range and Number of Possible MVs A motion vector $MV = (MV_x, MV_y)$ where each component is in $[-15, +15]$. $$ N_{\text{MV}} = 31 \times 31 = 961 \text{ possible vectors} $$ ### 5.2 MV Differential Coding H.261 does **not** code absolute MVs directly; it codes the **difference** between the current MV and the **previous MB's MV** (left neighbor, or wrap-around): $$ \Delta MV = MV_{\text{current}} - MV_{\text{prev}} $$ This exploits the spatial correlation of motion fields (a moving object has nearly identical MVs in adjacent MBs). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why differential MV coding?** It concentrates the probability mass around $\Delta MV = 0$, which the VLC table assigns the shortest codeword. For a slow-panning head-and-shoulders video, $\Delta MV = (0,0)$ is overwhelmingly the most common case — saving an enormous number of bits per MB. ## 6. Derivation — Total Bitrate Budget per Macroblock (Approximation) For a target rate $R = p \times 64$ kbps and frame rate $F$ fps, the bits available per frame are: $$ B_{\text{frame}} = \frac{R \times 1000}{F} \;\text{bits/frame} $$ For CIF at 25 fps with $p = 4$ (256 kbps): $$ B_{\text{frame}} = \frac{256 \times 1000}{25} = 10240 \text{ bits/frame} $$ Per MB (out of 396): $$ B_{\text{MB}} = \frac{10240}{396} \approx 25.86 \text{ bits/MB} $$ This tiny budget is the reason H.261 must **aggressively quantize** DCT coefficients and use the **simplest possible MV/VLC tables**. ## 7. Derivation — H.261 Block Hierarchy Bit Counts | Element | Fields (approximate) | Bits | |---|---|---| | Picture Header | Format, temp ref, etc. | ~30–40 | | GOB Header | GOB number, quantizer info | ~20 | | MB Header | MB address, type, quant | ~10 | | MV (differential) | VLC coded | 1–15 | | DC coefficient | 8-bit VLC | ~8 | | AC coefficients | RLE-VLC | 1–30/run-length pair | | EOB (End of Block) | Single flag | 2 | > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb for KTU Numericals:** A typical H.261 macroblock at 128 kbps averages **30 to 100 bits** in INTER mode, but can exceed **200 bits** in INTRA mode (e.g., scene cuts). The rate-control loop adjusts $Q$ inversely with buffer fullness: as the buffer fills, $Q$ increases, reducing the bit cost. --- # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 1. H.261 Encoder Block Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Frame] --> B[Frame Partitioning
CIF/QCIF] B --> C[MB Processing Loop] C --> D[Mode Decision
Intra vs Inter] D -->|Inter| E[Motion Estimation
Integer pixel +-15] D -->|Intra| F[Original 8x8 Block] E --> G[Subtract Predicted Block
Residual = Current - Predicted] G --> H[8x8 DCT Transform] F --> H H --> I[Quantization Q1..Q31] I --> J[VLC Encoder
RLE + Huffman] J --> K[Bitstream Multiplexer
MV + Texture] I --> L[Inverse Quantization] L --> M[Inverse DCT] M --> N[Add Predicted Block] E --> N N --> O[Reconstructed Frame
Stored for next frame] O --> P[Loop Filter Optional
3-tap 1-4 1-2 1-4] P --> Q[Reference Frame Buffer] Q --> E K --> R[Buffer Status] R --> S[Rate Control Q adjuster] S --> I ``` ## 2. H.261 Hierarchical Block Structure (Picture → Block) ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Picture Frame
352x288 CIF or 176x144 QCIF] --> G1[GOB 0
11 MBs wide] P --> G2[GOB 1] P --> Gn[GOB n] G1 --> M1[MB 0
16x16 Y + 8x8 Cb + 8x8 Cr] G1 --> M2[MB 1] G1 --> M3[MB 10] M1 --> B1[Y Block 0
8x8] M1 --> B2[Y Block 1
8x8] M1 --> B3[Y Block 2
8x8] M1 --> B4[Y Block 3
8x8] M1 --> B5[Cb Block
8x8] M1 --> B6[Cr Block
8x8] ``` ## 3. H.261 Hybrid Codec Block Architecture (Encoder–Decoder Pair) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ENC[H.261 Encoder] A1[Input Frame] --> A2[Subtract Prediction] A2 --> A3[DCT] A3 --> A4[Quantizer Q] A4 --> A5[VLC Encoder] A4 --> A6[Inverse Q] A6 --> A7[Inverse DCT] A7 --> A8[Add Prediction] A8 --> A9[Reconstruction] A9 --> A10[Loop Filter] A10 --> A11[Ref Frame Buffer] A11 --> A2 end subgraph TX[Channel] A5 --> A12[Bitstream] end subgraph DEC[H.261 Decoder] A12 --> B1[VLC Decoder] B1 --> B2[Inverse Q] B2 --> B3[Inverse DCT] B3 --> B4[Add Prediction] B4 --> B5[Reconstruction] B5 --> B6[Loop Filter] B6 --> B7[Ref Frame Buffer] B7 --> B4 B1 --> B8[MV Extractor] B8 --> B4 end A5 --> A12 B1 -.-> B8 ``` ## 4. CIF/QCIF Layout Grid ```mermaid flowchart TD A[CIF: 352x288 = 22x18 MBs = 12 GOBs] B[QCIF: 176x144 = 11x9 MBs = 3 GOBs] A --> A1[2 GOB columns x 6 GOB rows
Each GOB = 11 MBs x 3 rows] B --> B1[1 GOB column x 3 GOB rows
Each GOB = 11 MBs x 3 rows] ``` ## 5. Motion Estimation Search Window (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Current MB Position 0,0] --> B[Search Window
31x31 pixels] B --> C[Compare against
Reference Frame] C --> D[Min SAD Block
Selected as MV] D --> E[MV vector out
e.g. MVx=+4 MVy=-2] ``` ## 6. H.261 Bitstream Structure ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Picture Layer] --> B[Picture Header
PSC TR PTYPE PEI] A --> C[GOB Layer 0] A --> D[GOB Layer 1] A --> E[GOB Layer n] A --> F[End of Sequence] C --> G[GOB Header
GSBI GN GF Quant] C --> H[MB 0] C --> I[MB 1] C --> J[MB k] H --> K[MB Header
MBA COD MCBPC] H --> L[MV Differential] H --> M[Block 0 DCT Data] H --> N[Block 1 DCT Data] H --> O[Block 5 DCT Data] ``` --- # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — 3 Mark Short Answer Questions ### Question 1: Define H.261. State its target bitrate range. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **Model Answer:** H.261 is an ITU-T video coding standard (approved in 1990) titled *"Video Codec for Audiovisual Services at p×64 kbit/s"*. It is a **hybrid video compression standard** that uses inter-frame motion compensation (for temporal redundancy) and 8×8 DCT (for spatial redundancy), followed by quantization and VLC entropy coding. It was designed primarily for **videophone and video conferencing** over **ISDN** networks. **Target bitrate range:** $$ R = p \times 64 \text{ kbps}, \quad p \in \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, 30\} $$ This gives a range of **64 kbps to 1920 kbps (≈ 2 Mbps)**. ### Question 2: List the picture formats supported by H.261. State the resolution of each. `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]` **Model Answer:** H.261 supports two picture formats: 1. **CIF (Common Intermediate Format):** - Luma: 352 × 288 pixels - Chroma (Cb, Cr): 176 × 144 pixels (4:2:0 subsampling) 2. **QCIF (Quarter CIF):** - Luma: 176 × 144 pixels - Chroma (Cb, Cr): 88 × 72 pixels (4:2:0 subsampling) **Macroblock counts:** - CIF: 22 × 18 = **396 MBs/frame**, with 12 GOBs. - QCIF: 11 × 9 = **99 MBs/frame**, with 3 GOBs. --- ## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **H.261 hybrid coding framework** with a neat block diagram. Explain the function of each block in the encoder. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **CIF and QCIF picture formats** of H.261. Compute the number of macroblocks, GOBs, and 8×8 blocks per frame for each format. #### Model Solution — (a) The H.261 encoder is a **DPCM loop** (Differential Pulse Code Modulation) that uses motion compensation as the predictor, wrapped around a **DCT-based transform coder** for the prediction error. The functional blocks are: | Block | Function | |---|---| | **Frame Partitioner** | Splits the input picture into 16×16 MBs. | | **Mode Decision** | Chooses INTRA or INTER mode for the current MB based on prediction error energy. | | **Motion Estimator** | Searches ±15 pixel window in the previous reconstructed frame to find best-matching MB. | | **Subtractor** | Computes the residual (current MB − predicted MB). | | **DCT (8×8)** | Transforms each 8×8 block of residual into 64 frequency coefficients. | | **Quantizer (Q)** | Divides coefficients by 2Q and rounds; controls the rate. | | **VLC Encoder** | Run-length + Huffman codes the quantized AC coefficients; DC uses a fixed 8-bit VLC. | | **Inverse Quantizer & IDCT** | Reconstructs the quantized residual at the encoder (local decoder) so the encoder and decoder see identical reference frames. | | **Adder** | Adds the quantized residual back to the predicted MB to form the reconstructed MB. | | **Loop Filter** | Optional 2-D low-pass filter applied to inter-coded MBs to reduce mosquito noise. | | **Reference Buffer** | Stores the reconstructed frame; provides input to the motion estimator for the next frame. | | **Rate Control** | Monitors the output buffer fullness; raises/lowers Q to keep the bitrate at p×64 kbps. | This is a **closed-loop hybrid coder**: temporal redundancy is removed by prediction; spatial redundancy is removed by DCT; statistical redundancy is removed by VLC. #### Model Solution — (b) **Step 1 — CIF format:** Resolution: 352 × 288 luma; 176 × 144 chroma (4:2:0). Macroblock dimensions: 16 × 16 luma + 8 × 8 Cb + 8 × 8 Cr. **Step 2 — CIF macroblock count:** $$ N_{\text{MB, CIF}} = \frac{352}{16} \times \frac{288}{16} = 22 \times 18 = 396 \text{ MBs/frame} $$ **Step 3 — CIF GOB count:** A GOB = 11 MBs wide × 3 MBs tall. So: $$ \text{CIF GOBs} = \frac{22}{11} \times \frac{18}{3} = 2 \times 6 = 12 \text{ GOBs/frame} $$ **Step 4 — CIF block count:** $$ N_{\text{blocks, CIF}} = 396 \times 6 = 2376 \text{ 8×8 blocks/frame} $$ **Step 5 — QCIF format:** Resolution: 176 × 144 luma; 88 × 72 chroma. **Step 6 — QCIF macroblock count:** $$ N_{\text{MB, QCIF}} = \frac{176}{16} \times \frac{144}{16} = 11 \times 9 = 99 \text{ MBs/frame} $$ **Step 7 — QCIF GOB count:** $$ \text{QCIF GOBs} = \frac{11}{11} \times \frac{9}{3} = 1 \times 3 = 3 \text{ GOBs/frame} $$ **Step 8 — QCIF block count:** $$ N_{\text{blocks, QCIF}} = 99 \times 6 = 594 \text{ 8×8 blocks/frame} $$ **Final Comparison Table:** | Parameter | CIF | QCIF | |---|---|---| | Luma resolution | 352 × 288 | 176 × 144 | | Chroma resolution | 176 × 144 | 88 × 72 | | MBs per frame | 396 | 99 | | GOBs per frame | 12 | 3 | | 8×8 blocks per frame | 2376 | 594 | --- ### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) and Quantization steps** in the H.261 encoder. How is the quantizer step Q chosen at runtime? **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **motion compensation algorithm** in H.261. What is the search range, the precision of the motion vector, and the rationale for differential MV coding? #### Model Solution — (a) **DCT Step:** After subtraction of the predicted MB from the current MB, the residual is split into 8×8 blocks. Each 8×8 block is transformed by the 2D-DCT: $$ F(u,v) = \frac{C(u)C(v)}{4} \sum_{x=0}^{7} \sum_{y=0}^{7} f(x,y) \cos\!\left[\frac{(2x+1)u\pi}{16}\right] \cos\!\left[\frac{(2y+1)v\pi}{16}\right] $$ with $C(0) = 1/\sqrt{2}$ and $C(k) = 1$ for $k \neq 0$. The transform produces **64 frequency coefficients** (1 DC + 63 AC) per block. The DC represents the mean luminance/chrominance; the AC coefficients represent high-frequency detail. **Quantization Step:** The DCT coefficients are uniformly quantized: $$ \text{COF}'(u,v) = \text{round}\!\left[\frac{\text{COF}(u,v)}{2\,Q}\right] $$ The dequantization at the decoder is: $$ \widehat{\text{COF}}(u,v) = \begin{cases} 0, & \text{COF}' = 0 \\ 2\,Q \cdot \text{COF}' + \text{sgn}(\text{COF}') \cdot Q, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Q-selection (Rate Control):** - Q is an integer in the range **1 to 31**. - A higher Q gives more compression (more zeros) but more visual distortion. - A lower Q gives better quality but consumes more bits. - Q is dynamically adjusted by the **rate-control feedback loop** based on the **bitrate buffer occupancy**: - If buffer is filling too fast → **increase Q** to reduce bits. - If buffer is emptying too fast → **decrease Q** to generate more bits. - This keeps the average bitrate at the target $p \times 64$ kbps. #### Model Solution — (b) **Motion Compensation Algorithm in H.261:** 1. **Reference frame selection:** The previous **reconstructed** (decoded) frame is used as the reference. The encoder's local decoder ensures both encoder and decoder use *identical* reference frames (preventing drift). 2. **Macroblock search:** For each 16×16 MB in the current frame, the encoder searches a window in the reference frame to find the MB that best matches the current MB. 3. **Matching criterion:** Typically the **Sum of Absolute Differences (SAD)** is minimized: $$ \text{SAD}(dx, dy) = \sum_{i=0}^{15} \sum_{j=0}^{15} \vert I_t(i, j) - I_{t-1}(i + dx, j + dy) \vert $$ 4. **Motion vector (MV):** The displacement $(dx, dy)$ that minimizes SAD is selected as the MV: $$ MV = \arg\min_{dx, dy} \text{SAD}(dx, dy) $$ 5. **Search range:** $dx, dy \in [-15, +15]$ pixels, giving $31 \times 31 = 961$ candidate vectors. 6. **MV precision:** **Integer pixel accuracy only** (no half-pel or quarter-pel as in H.263/H.264). 7. **Prediction residual:** The MV points to a 16×16 block in the reference frame. The residual is: $$ R(i, j) = I_t(i, j) - I_{t-1}(i + MV_x, j + MV_y) $$ This residual is then DCT-coded. 8. **Skip mode (COD = 1):** If the MB matches the reference almost perfectly (SAD below a threshold) and the MV is zero, the MB is *skipped* (no data sent), saving bits. **Differential MV coding rationale:** H.261 transmits **MV difference** $\Delta MV = MV_{\text{current}} - MV_{\text{prev}}$ rather than the absolute MV. This is because: - Adjacent MBs in natural video have very similar motion (the same object covers multiple MBs). - $\Delta MV \approx (0, 0)$ for most MBs, which the VLC table codes with the shortest codeword. - This dramatically reduces the average bits per MV, improving compression efficiency. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing CIF GOB count for 525 vs. 625-line systems.** The 12-GOB structure is universal for H.261 CIF; the 525/625 distinction is about the *frame rate framing* (29.97 vs. 25 fps), NOT the GOB count. > 2. **Forgetting the 4:2:0 chroma subsampling.** Many students write "CIF = 352 × 288 chroma" which is wrong. Chroma is half in each dimension. > 3. **Confusing H.261 with H.263.** H.263 supports **half-pel** MV precision; H.261 does *not*. H.263 also supports more picture formats (Sub-QCIF, 4CIF, 16CIF); H.261 only has CIF and QCIF. > 4. **Writing "H.261 uses Huffman coding."** Strictly speaking, H.261 uses a *Huffman-like* VLC table fixed in the standard, not a learned Huffman tree. The word "Huffman" is acceptable, but for full marks, say "fixed VLC table similar to Huffman." > 5. **Skipping the local decoder explanation.** Examiners *love* asking why the encoder contains an inverse quantizer + IDCT. The answer: "to ensure the encoder and decoder reference the same reconstructed frame; otherwise drift would accumulate and the decoder would diverge from the encoder." > 6. **Forgetting that the loop filter is optional** and **only applied to inter-coded MBs** (not intra). --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — H.261** - ✅ H.261 is a **hybrid DCT + motion compensation** standard from **ITU-T (1990)**. - ✅ Designed for **ISDN videophone and video conferencing**. - ✅ Bitrate grammar: **$R = p \times 64$ kbps, $p \in [1, 30]$**, so **64 kbps ≤ R ≤ 1920 kbps**. - ✅ Two picture formats: **CIF (352×288)** and **QCIF (176×144)**. - ✅ **4:2:0 chroma subsampling** always. - ✅ Hierarchical structure: **Picture → GOB → Macroblock → Block (8×8)**. - ✅ **Macroblock = 16×16 luma + 8×8 Cb + 8×8 Cr = 6 blocks (4Y + 1Cb + 1Cr)**. - ✅ CIF: **22 × 18 = 396 MBs/frame, 12 GOBs, 2376 8×8 blocks/frame**. - ✅ QCIF: **11 × 9 = 99 MBs/frame, 3 GOBs, 594 8×8 blocks/frame**. - ✅ **8×8 DCT** for spatial redundancy; 1 DC + 63 AC coefficients per block. - ✅ Quantization: $\text{COF}' = \text{round}[\text{COF} / (2Q)]$, where $Q = 1..31$. - ✅ **Integer pixel motion vectors** with **±15 pixel** search range (31 × 31 = 961 candidates). - ✅ **Differential MV coding** exploits spatial motion correlation; VLC assigns shortest code to $\Delta MV = 0$. - ✅ **Mode decision**: INTRA (force periodically to prevent drift) vs. INTER (with MC + residual). - ✅ **Skip mode (COD)** for static MBs — saves bits when MB is unchanged from reference. - ✅ **Local decoder inside encoder** ensures encoder and decoder reference identical frames. - ✅ **Optional loop filter**: 2-D 3-tap (1/4, 1/2, 1/4) low-pass on INTER MBs only. - ✅ **Zig-zag scan** of DCT coefficients for run-length VLC efficiency. - ✅ **DC coefficient** coded with fixed 8-bit VLC; AC uses RLE + amplitude VLC. - ✅ **Rate control** via dynamic Q adjustment based on buffer occupancy. - ✅ **25 fps (625-line PAL)** and **29.97 fps (525-line NTSC)** are supported. - ✅ H.261 is the **architectural ancestor** of H.263, MPEG-1, MPEG-2, MPEG-4, H.264/AVC, HEVC, and VVC — all preserve the hybrid-DCT-MC paradigm. > [!TIP] > **Top 5 Exam-Day Traps (Avoid These):** > 1. Don't write **H.261 supports MPEG-style B-frames** — it does not. H.261 has only **I and P** pictures. > 2. Don't confuse **CIF macroblock count (396)** with **QCIF macroblock count (99)** — both are KTU favorites. > 3. Don't say "DCT is lossless" — DCT itself is lossless, but the **quantization after DCT is lossy**. > 4. Don't forget the **inverse quantizer + IDCT inside the encoder** (local decoder). > 5. Don't state that H.261 has only one picture format. It has **two**: **CIF and QCIF**, and CIF must be supported by all decoders; QCIF is the lower-resolution alternative. ---

topics.title: Audio Compression - Companding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST524 - DATA COMPRESSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Audio Compression - Companding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Companding** (a portmanteau of **COMpressing** and ex**PANDING**) is a non-linear signal processing technique used in audio compression and telecommunications to improve the Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) of signals that have a non-uniform amplitude distribution — such as human speech. It is implemented by **compressing** the dynamic range of a signal at the transmitter (encoder) using a logarithmic curve, and then **expanding** it symmetrically back to its original dynamic range at the receiver (decoder).

The process effectively converts a **non-uniform quantization** problem into a tractable **uniform quantization** problem by applying a non-linear transformation before uniform quantization and the inverse transformation after uniform de-quantization.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Whisper-to-Roar" Microphone

Imagine you are recording a conversation in a large auditorium:

- The speaker **whispers softly** (low amplitude) at one moment and **shouts loudly** (high amplitude) the next.
- If you use a **fixed, uniform recording scale**, the soft whisper gets buried in background noise (poor quality for low signals), while the loud shout might clip/distort.
- A **compander** is like a smart audio engineer: he **automatically turns up the volume** when people whisper and **turns it down** when they shout, *before* recording. At playback, the inverse is applied — so the listener hears the original dynamics.
- The result: **whispers become clear and crisp**, while shouts remain controlled, all using the same limited digital bit budget.

### 1.3 Standard Companding Standards (Globally Adopted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two International Companding Standards** are universally recognized and tested in KTU examinations:

| Standard | Used In | Compression Parameter | Standard Reference |
|----------|---------|----------------------|--------------------|
| **μ-law (mu-law)** | USA, Canada, Japan | **μ = 255** | ITU-T G.711 |
| **A-law** | Europe, India, Most of World | **A = 87.6** (≈ 87.56) | ITU-T G.711 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The compressed sample value is **normalized** to the range **[-1, +1]** before applying companding, and the resulting compressed value is also restricted to **[-1, +1]**.

### 1.4 Why Companding? — The Engineering Justification

> [!NOTE]
> **Speech signals exhibit a high Dynamic Range (DR) but a non-uniform probability density function (PDF).** Studies show that the PDF of speech resembles a **Laplacian (double-exponential) distribution** — small amplitudes occur far more frequently than large amplitudes.

- Uniform quantization wastes bits on rare, high-amplitude regions and starves the frequently occurring low-amplitude regions.
- Companding solves this by **allocating more quantization levels to low-amplitude signals** (where probability is high) and **fewer levels to high-amplitude signals** (where probability is low).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Block Diagram of Companding

The companding system can be conceptually decomposed into three blocks:

1. **Compressor Block (C)** — at the Transmitter: applies non-linear mapping $y = C(x)$.
2. **Uniform Quantizer (Q)** — quantizes the compressed signal uniformly.
3. **Expander Block (C⁻¹)** — at the Receiver: applies inverse mapping $\hat{x} = C^{-1}(\hat{y})$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Non-uniform quantization $\equiv$ Compressor $\rightarrow$ Uniform Quantizer $\rightarrow$ Expander. This mathematical equivalence is a favorite KTU 2-mark question.

### 2.2 Mathematical Formulation — μ-law Companding

For a normalized input signal $x \in [-1, +1]$, the μ-law compressor is defined as:

$$
F_{\mu}(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \frac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1 + \mu)}
$$

Where:
- $F_{\mu}(x)$ is the compressed (output) signal
- $\text{sgn}(x)$ is the signum function (preserves polarity)
- $\mu$ is the companding parameter (typically **255**)
- $|x|$ is the absolute value of the normalized input

The μ-law expander (inverse) is:

$$
F_{\mu}^{-1}(y) = \text{sgn}(y) \cdot \frac{(1 + \mu)^{\vert y \vert} - 1}{\mu}
$$

### 2.3 Mathematical Formulation — A-law Companding

For A-law, the input range $[-1, +1]$ is split at $\pm 1/A$, giving a piecewise-linear-on-log-scale characteristic:

$$
F_{A}(x) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{A \cdot \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A}, & 0 \leq \vert x \vert \leq \dfrac{1}{A} \\[10pt] \dfrac{1 + \ln(A \cdot \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln A}, & \dfrac{1}{A} \leq \vert x \vert \leq 1 \end{cases}
$$

The A-law expander (inverse) is:

$$
F_{A}^{-1}(y) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{\vert y \vert \cdot (1 + \ln A)}{A}, & 0 \leq \vert y \vert \leq \dfrac{1}{1 + \ln A} \\[10pt] \dfrac{\exp\left(\vert y \vert \cdot (1 + \ln A) - 1\right)}{A}, & \dfrac{1}{1 + \ln A} \leq \vert y \vert \leq 1 \end{cases}
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following high-yield table. These formulas appear repeatedly in KTU board examinations.

| **Parameter / Concept** | **μ-law** | **A-law** |
|--------------------------|-----------|-----------|
| Compression Equation | $F(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \frac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1 + \mu)}$ | Piecewise: linear for small $\vert x \vert \leq 1/A$, logarithmic for larger |
| Standard Parameter | $\mu = \mathbf{255}$ | $A = \mathbf{87.6}$ |
| Compression at $x = 0$ | $F(0) = 0$ | $F(0) = 0$ |
| Compression at $\vert x \vert = 1$ | $F(1) = 1$ | $F(1) = 1$ |
| Approximate Expansion | $\approx 1 + \mu \vert x \vert$ for small $\vert x \vert$ | $\approx A \cdot \vert x \vert$ for small $\vert x \vert$ |
| Slope at Origin | $\frac{\mu}{\ln(1 + \mu)}$ | $\frac{A}{1 + \ln A}$ |
| Bits per Sample (PCM) | **8 bits** (in G.711) | **8 bits** |
| SQNR Improvement (approx.) | ~ **24 dB to 30 dB** over uniform PCM | ~ **24 dB to 30 dB** over uniform PCM |
| Region of Operation | Globally used, especially North America | Globally used, especially Europe & India |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Companding is the silent workhorse behind every digital phone call you make.**
> - **Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN)** — every landline call uses **A-law** (India) or **μ-law** (USA).
> - **VoIP (Voice over IP)** and **ISDN** — digital telephony inherits G.711 companded PCM.
> - **Audio CDs and Digital Broadcasting** — variations of companding are used in Dolby noise reduction systems.
> - **Satellite Communication** — companding reduces in-band noise contribution for weak signals.
> - **Hearing Aids** — dynamic range compression (DRC), a companding cousin, assists hearing-impaired users.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem Setup

**Given (Sample KTU Problem):**
A μ-law compander is used with $\mu = 255$. The normalized input is $x = +0.45$. Compute the compressed output value $F_\mu(x)$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Formula**
The μ-law compression formula is:

$$
F_{\mu}(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \frac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1 + \mu)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Known Values**
We have $\mu = 255$, $x = 0.45$, so $\text{sgn}(x) = +1$ and $|x| = 0.45$.

$$
F_{\mu}(0.45) = (+1) \cdot \frac{\ln(1 + 255 \cdot \vert 0.45 \vert)}{\ln(1 + 255)}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Numerator Argument**

$$
1 + 255 \cdot 0.45 = 1 + 114.75 = 115.75
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Numerator**

$$
\ln(115.75) \approx 4.7510
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Denominator**

$$
\ln(1 + 255) = \ln(256) \approx 5.5452
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Final Ratio**

$$
F_{\mu}(0.45) \approx \frac{4.7510}{5.5452} \approx 0.8567
$$

**Step 7 — Verification**
Since $|x| = 0.45$ (a moderate value), the compressed output $0.8567$ should be closer to $1$ than $|x|$ is — this is correct, confirming compression of the dynamic range. ✓

### 3.2 Worked Example — A-law Companding

**Given:**
An A-law compander with $A = 87.6$ receives a normalized input $x = 0.02$.

**Step 1 — Identify the Piecewise Region**
We must check whether $|x| \leq 1/A$ or $|x| \geq 1/A$:

$$
\frac{1}{A} = \frac{1}{87.6} \approx 0.01142
$$

Since $|x| = 0.02 > 0.01142$, we are in the **logarithmic region**.

**Step 2 — Apply the Logarithmic Branch**

$$
F_{A}(x) = \frac{1 + \ln(A \cdot \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln A}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate Inner Term**

$$
A \cdot \vert x \vert = 87.6 \cdot 0.02 = 1.752
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Numerator**

$$
1 + \ln(1.752) \approx 1 + 0.5606 = 1.5606
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Denominator**

$$
1 + \ln(87.6) \approx 1 + 4.4732 = 5.4732
$$

**Step 6 — Final Computation**

$$
F_{A}(0.02) \approx \frac{1.5606}{5.4732} \approx 0.2852
$$

### 3.3 SQNR Derivation — Why Companding Improves Performance

For uniform quantization with $L = 2^n$ levels over peak-to-peak range $2V_{pp}$:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{uniform}} = \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{\left(\pi \cdot e\right)^2} \cdot \left(\frac{V_{rms}}{V_{pp}}\right)^2 \quad \text{[for full-load sinusoid]}
$$

For companded PCM, the equivalent SQNR for a sinusoidal input with amplitude $V$ (peak) is given approximately by:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{companded}} \approx 2^{2n} \cdot \frac{3 \cdot \mu^2}{\pi^2 \cdot \left(1 + \mu^2 \cdot (V/V_{pp})^2\right) \cdot \ln^2(1+\mu)}
$$

**Step-by-Step Comparison for $\mu = 255$, $n = 8$, sinusoidal input:**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{uniform}}(n=8) \approx 49.8 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{companded}}(n=8, \mu=255) \approx 74.7 \text{ dB} \quad \text{(approx. for mid-level signals)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net Gain ≈ 25 dB** for the **same bit rate (8 bits/sample)**. This is why companded 8-bit PCM rivals uniform 12-bit PCM in perceived quality.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Companded PCM System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputX[/Analog Speech Input x t/]
    comp[Compressor Block C x]
    uniformQ[Uniform Quantizer n bits]
    channel[Channel Encoder Transmit]
    channelX[Channel Decoder Receive]
    uniformDQ[Uniform De-Quantizer]
    expander[Expander Block C inv y]
    outputX[/Reconstructed Signal x hat t/]
    qnoise1[/Quantization Noise/]

    inputX -->|non uniform amplitude| comp
    comp -->|compressed y in 1 to 1| uniformQ
    uniformQ -->|8 bit codeword| channel
    channel -->|bits| channelX
    channelX --> uniformDQ
    uniformDQ -->|recovered y hat| expander
    expander -->|expanded x hat| outputX
    uniformQ -.->|adds| qnoise1

    classDef analog fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#B7791F,color:#000000
    classDef digital fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#3D5A80,color:#000000
    classDef proc fill:#B5E48C,stroke:#52B788,color:#000000

    class inputX,outputX,qnoise1 analog
    class channel,channelX,uniformQ,uniformDQ digital
    class comp,expander proc
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison of A-law vs μ-law Compression Curves

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start([Start KTU Companding Comparison])
    q1{Input Signal x}
    branch1[mu law branch with mu equals 255]
    branch2[A law branch with A equals 87.6]
    formula1[Formula y equals sgn x times ln 1 plus mu mod x divided by ln 1 plus mu]
    formula2A[Region 1 mod x less than or equal 1 over A — Linear Piece]
    formula2B[Region 2 mod x greater than 1 over A — Logarithmic Piece]
    out1[Output y in 1 to 1]
    out2[Output y in 1 to 1]

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|mu law selected| branch1
    q1 -->|A law selected| branch2
    branch1 --> formula1 --> out1
    branch2 --> formula2A
    branch2 --> formula2B
    formula2A --> out2
    formula2B --> out2

    classDef region fill:#FFD6A5,stroke:#B7791F,color:#000000
    classDef formula fill:#B5E48C,stroke:#52B788,color:#000000
    classDef terminal fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#3D5A80,color:#000000

    class q1 region
    class formula1,formula2A,formula2B formula
    class start,out1,out2 terminal
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Effect of Companding on Quantization Step Size

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Amplitude Region of Signal]
    B[High Amplitude Region of Signal]
    C[Without Companding — Uniform Step Size across range]
    D[With Companding — Smaller Steps in Low Amplitude Region]
    E[With Companding — Larger Steps in High Amplitude Region]
    F[Result: Better Resolution for Whisper Quiet Sounds]

    A --> D
    B --> E
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F

    classDef region fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#B7791F,color:#000000
    classDef benefit fill:#B5E48C,stroke:#52B788,color:#000000
    classDef neg fill:#FFADAD,stroke:#9D0208,color:#000000

    class A,B region
    class C neg
    class D,E,F benefit
```
