## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Define the term Companding. Why is it preferred over uniform quantization in speech coding?**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Companding** is the combined process of **COMpressing** a signal at the transmitter and **exPANDING** it at the receiver using a non-linear (logarithmic) transfer characteristic.
> **(1 Mark)** — Formal definition.
> **Reason:** Speech signals exhibit a **Laplacian amplitude distribution**, where low amplitudes are far more probable than high amplitudes. Uniform quantization wastes resolution on rare loud sounds and under-resolves frequent quiet sounds. Companding allocates **finer quantization steps to small amplitudes** and **coarser steps to large amplitudes**, thereby improving the **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** for typical speech levels by approximately **24–30 dB** without increasing the bit rate.
> **(2 Marks)** — Justification.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Compare A-law and μ-law companding standards.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | μ-law | A-law |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| Parameter value | $\mu = 255$ | $A = 87.6$ |
| Continuity at origin | Continuous, smooth | Piecewise (kink at $1/A$) |
| Usage region | North America, Japan | Europe, India, Rest of World |
| Complexity | Simple single equation | Piecewise linear + log |
| **Slight SNR advantage** | Slightly better for low-level signals | Slightly better at origin (linear region avoids $\ln 0$) |

**(1 Mark)** — Common context; **(2 Marks)** — Tabulated comparison.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Model)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Companding Module]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the μ-law compressor output. With $\mu = 255$, compute the compressed value for input $x = 0.30$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a block diagram how companding improves the signal-to-quantization-noise ratio in PCM systems. Compare it with uniform quantization. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation and Numerical Computation:**

> **Derivation of μ-law Compressor Expression (5 Marks):**

The μ-law compressor aims to achieve a logarithmic compression characteristic. For a normalized input $x \in [-1, +1]$, the output is:

$$
F_{\mu}(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \frac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1 + \mu)}
$$

**Derivation Logic (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1** — The general form of a logarithmic compressor is sought such that the output spans $[-1, +1]$ when input spans $[-1, +1]$.

**Step 2** — Consider only the positive half $x \geq 0$ for simplicity. We need:

$$
y = \frac{\ln(1 + \mu x)}{\ln(1 + \mu)} \quad \text{for } 0 \leq x \leq 1
$$

**Step 3** — Boundary check at $x = 1$:

$$
y \Big\vert_{x=1} = \frac{\ln(1 + \mu)}{\ln(1 + \mu)} = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4** — Boundary check at $x = 0$:

$$
y \Big\vert_{x=0} = \frac{\ln(1 + 0)}{\ln(1 + \mu)} = \frac{0}{\ln(1 + \mu)} = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5** — Slope at the origin (tangent):

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} \bigg\vert_{x=0} = \frac{\mu}{\ln(1 + \mu)}
$$

For $\mu = 255$:

$$
\frac{255}{\ln(256)} \approx \frac{255}{5.545} \approx 45.97
$$

This high slope at the origin means **small input signals are amplified strongly**, improving resolution. ✓

**Step 6** — Sign preservation via $\text{sgn}(x)$ extends to the full $[-1, +1]$ range.

> **Numerical Computation for $x = 0.30$, $\mu = 255$ (2 Marks):**

$$
F_{\mu}(0.30) = (+1) \cdot \frac{\ln(1 + 255 \cdot 0.30)}{\ln(1 + 255)}
$$

Computing the inner term:

$$
1 + 255 \cdot 0.30 = 1 + 76.5 = 77.5
$$

Computing the logs:

$$
\ln(77.5) \approx 4.3503
$$

$$
\ln(256) \approx 5.5452
$$

Final result:

$$
F_{\mu}(0.30) \approx \frac{4.3503}{5.5452} \approx 0.7845
$$

**Answer:** $F_\mu(0.30) \approx 0.7845$

---

**Part (b) — Block Diagram and SQNR Comparison (7 Marks):**

> **Block Diagram Description (3 Marks):**

The companded PCM system has the following sequence at the **transmitter**:

1. **Anti-aliasing Filter** $\rightarrow$ limits input bandwidth.
2. **Compressor (C)** $\rightarrow$ non-linear (logarithmic) mapping $y = C(x)$.
3. **Uniform Sampler** $\rightarrow$ discrete-time samples.
4. **Uniform Quantizer** $\rightarrow$ $n$-bit codewords (e.g., 8 bits in G.711).
5. **Channel Encoder** $\rightarrow$ line coding for transmission.

At the **receiver**, the inverse sequence applies:

6. **Channel Decoder** $\rightarrow$ recovers bit stream.
7. **Uniform De-quantizer** $\rightarrow$ recovers $\hat{y}$.
8. **Expander ($C^{-1}$)** $\rightarrow$ inverse mapping $\hat{x} = C^{-1}(\hat{y})$.
9. **Reconstruction Filter** $\rightarrow$ smooth analog output.

> **SQNR Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Bit Count $n$ | Uniform PCM SQNR | μ-law Companded SQNR | Net Gain |
|----------------|------------------|----------------------|----------|
| 8 | ~ 49.8 dB | ~ 74.7 dB (mid-level) | +24.9 dB |
| 12 | ~ 73.8 dB | ~ 98.7 dB | +24.9 dB |
| 16 | ~ 97.8 dB | ~ 122.7 dB | +24.9 dB |

**Conclusion:** Companded PCM achieves approximately the **equivalent quality of uniform PCM with 4 extra bits**, representing a massive bandwidth/capacity saving — this is why every digital phone call uses companded 8-bit PCM rather than 12-bit uniform PCM.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the A-law companding standard. Given $A = 87.6$ and input $x = -0.05$, compute the compressed output. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the working of a μ-law compander with a neat block diagram. List any **three advantages** and **two limitations** of companding. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — A-law Standard and Computation (7 Marks):**

> **Explanation (4 Marks):**

A-law is the European/Indian ITU-T G.711 companding standard. It uses a **piecewise** characteristic — a **linear segment** near the origin for very small signals, and a **logarithmic segment** for larger signals. The break point occurs at $|x| = 1/A$.

**Linear segment (low amplitude):**

$$
F_{A}(x) = \frac{A \cdot \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A}, \quad 0 \leq \vert x \vert \leq \frac{1}{A}
$$

**Logarithmic segment (high amplitude):**

$$
F_{A}(x) = \frac{1 + \ln(A \cdot \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln A}, \quad \frac{1}{A} \leq \vert x \vert \leq 1
$$

**Boundary continuity check:** At $|x| = 1/A$:

- Linear branch: $\frac{A \cdot (1/A)}{1 + \ln A} = \frac{1}{1 + \ln A}$
- Logarithmic branch: $\frac{1 + \ln(A \cdot 1/A)}{1 + \ln A} = \frac{1 + 0}{1 + \ln A} = \frac{1}{1 + \ln A}$ ✓

The function is **continuous at the breakpoint**.

> **Numerical Computation for $x = -0.05$ (3 Marks):**

**Step 1** — Compute the breakpoint:

$$
\frac{1}{A} = \frac{1}{87.6} \approx 0.01142
$$

**Step 2** — Compare $|x|$ with breakpoint:

$$
\vert x \vert = 0.05 > 0.01142
$$

So we are in the **logarithmic region**.

**Step 3** — Compute $A \cdot |x|$:

$$
A \cdot \vert x \vert = 87.6 \cdot 0.05 = 4.38
$$

**Step 4** — Compute the numerator:

$$
1 + \ln(4.38) \approx 1 + 1.4769 = 2.4769
$$

**Step 5** — Compute the denominator:

$$
1 + \ln(87.6) \approx 1 + 4.4732 = 5.4732
$$

**Step 6** — Final result (with $\text{sgn}(-0.05) = -1$):

$$
F_{A}(-0.05) = -1 \cdot \frac{2.4769}{5.4732} \approx -0.4525
$$

**Answer:** $F_A(-0.05) \approx -0.4525$

---

**Part (b) — μ-law Compander Block Diagram, Advantages & Limitations (7 Marks):**

> **Working Description with Block Diagram (4 Marks):**

A **μ-law compander** operates as follows:
- **Transmitter Side:** The analog speech signal $x(t)$ is passed through a **compressor** which applies the logarithmic transformation $y = C(x)$. The compressed signal $y$ is then **uniformly sampled** at a rate $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$ (Nyquist criterion) and **uniformly quantized** using an $n$-bit quantizer (typically 8 bits per G.711). The resulting binary codewords are transmitted.
- **Receiver Side:** The received binary stream is **uniformly de-quantized** to recover $\hat{y}$. The expander applies the inverse logarithmic transformation $\hat{x} = C^{-1}(\hat{y})$ to restore the original dynamic range. A **reconstruction filter** smooths the output to produce the analog reconstructed speech.

> **Three Advantages (2 Marks):**
> 1. **Improved SQNR** by ~24–30 dB for typical speech levels at no extra bit cost.
> 2. **Bandwidth Efficiency** — 8-bit companded PCM matches the perceptual quality of 12-bit uniform PCM.
> 3. **Robust against channel noise** for weak/quiet signals, since these are amplified before quantization.

> **Two Limitations (1 Mark):**
> 1. **Increased quantization noise for high-amplitude signals** (coarser steps at top end).
> 2. **Non-linear distortion** if compressor and expander characteristics are not perfectly matched across the channel.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Companding Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the signum function** $\text{sgn}(x)$ for negative inputs — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** if the polarity is missing.
> 2. **Wrong region selection in A-law** — Students often use the logarithmic formula even when $|x| < 1/A$. Always compute $1/A$ first!
> 3. **Confusing $\ln$ with $\log_{10}$** — all formulas use the **natural logarithm ($\ln$)**, not base-10 log. This is a frequent silent error.
> 4. **Forgetting to normalize** — the input must be in $[-1, +1]$. If the problem gives a value like "0.45 V on a 1 V scale," normalize before applying the formula.
> 5. **Skipping boundary verification** — always verify $F(0)=0$ and $F(1)=1$ as a sanity check; examiners reward this.
> 6. **In A-law, ignoring the piecewise structure** — writing only the logarithmic branch loses marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Audio Compression: Companding**

- ⭐ **Companding = Compression + Expansion** — non-linear transform at TX, inverse at RX.
- ⭐ **Purpose**: Improve SQNR by ~24–30 dB for speech signals with Laplacian amplitude distribution.
- ⭐ **Two Standards**: **μ-law** ($\mu = 255$, North America/Japan) and **A-law** ($A = 87.6$, Europe/India).
- ⭐ **μ-law formula**: $F(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \frac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1+\mu)}$
- ⭐ **A-law formula**: Piecewise — linear for $|x| \leq 1/A$, logarithmic for $|x| > 1/A$.
- ⭐ **Always use natural log ($\ln$)**, never $\log_{10}$.
- ⭐ **Input range** $[-1, +1]$ — output also in $[-1, +1]$.
- ⭐ **8-bit G.711 PCM** uses companding — quality rivals 12-bit uniform PCM.
- ⭐ **Practical applications**: PSTN, ISDN, VoIP, satellite comms, hearing aids.
- ⭐ **Pitfall**: $\text{sgn}(x)$ is **mandatory** for negative inputs.
- ⭐ **Boundary tests**: Always verify $F(0) = 0$ and $F(\pm 1) = \pm 1$.
- ⭐ **Block sequence**: Compressor → Uniform Quantizer → Channel → De-quantizer → Expander.
- ⭐ **Companded PCM ≈ Uniform PCM with 4 extra bits** in perceptual quality.
- ⭐ **μ-law is continuous at origin; A-law has a small kink at $|x|=1/A$** — but A-law has zero slope-discontinuity loss.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Human Auditory System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST524 - DATA COMPRESSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Human Auditory System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
The **Human Auditory System** is the sensory organ responsible for the perception of sound, consisting of the outer ear, middle ear, inner ear (cochlea), auditory nerve, and auditory cortex. In the context of **Data Compression**, the auditory system is studied as a **perceptual model** — its physiological and psychoacoustic limitations are exploited by lossy audio codecs (e.g., MP3, AAC, Opus) to discard inaudible signal components, thereby achieving high compression ratios with no perceived loss in quality.

### Key Physiological Components
1. **Outer Ear (Pinna + Ear Canal):** Collects sound waves and funnels them to the eardrum. Provides a mild amplification (~**10–15 dB**) around **2–5 kHz** due to its resonant geometry.
2. **Middle Ear (Ossicles: Malleus, Incus, Stapes):** Mechanical impedance matching device. Converts air vibrations into fluid vibrations in the cochlea.
3. **Inner Ear (Cochlea):** A fluid-filled, snail-shaped organ containing the **Basilar Membrane** lined with ~**30,000 hair cells**. It performs a real-time **frequency-to-place transformation** (mechanical Fourier analysis).
4. **Auditory Nerve & Cortex:** Hair cells transduce mechanical motion into neural spikes; the brain interprets them as pitch, loudness, and timbre.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why study hearing for Data Compression?**
> Because the human ear can only hear frequencies between **20 Hz and 20,000 Hz**, is insensitive to phase, and has masking limitations — any signal content *outside* these perceptual limits is **wasted bits** and can be safely discarded without the listener noticing.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of the human ear as a **biological spectrum analyzer with built-in noise reduction**:
- Imagine a piano stretched out into a long ribbon. Each key strikes a different spot on the ribbon. A low C (32 Hz) makes the left end wiggle; a high C (4186 Hz) makes the right end wiggle. This ribbon is the **basilar membrane**.
- Now imagine you are standing in a noisy cafeteria. If a loud person shouts next to you, you cannot hear a soft whisper on the other side of the room — your ear **masks** the quieter sound. Audio compressors exploit this cafeteria effect.
- The brain does not store the "raw" air-pressure waveform; it stores a *summary* of frequencies and their loudness. Codecs mimic this summary storage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constants for Human Hearing (KTU Reference Values):**
> - Frequency Range: **20 Hz – 20,000 Hz** (decreases with age — *presbycusis*)
> - Reference Sound Pressure: **P₀ = 20 μPa** (threshold of hearing at 1 kHz)
> - Dynamic Range: **~120 dB SPL** (threshold of hearing to threshold of pain)
> - Critical Bands: **~24 Bark** across the audible spectrum
> - Phase Perception: **Insensitive** to absolute phase (above ~1–2 kHz)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown: From Sound Wave to Brain Perception
The auditory pathway can be decomposed into a five-stage pipeline:

1. **Acoustic Stage (Outer Ear):** Sound pressure waves (P in Pascals) are captured. The pinna and ear canal act as a passive acoustic filter, providing a mild gain around 3 kHz.
2. **Mechanical Stage (Middle Ear):** The three ossicles (malleus, incus, stapes) act as a **hydraulic lever**, multiplying force ~22× to overcome the impedance mismatch between air and cochlear fluid. Without this, ~**99.9% of sound energy** would reflect back.
3. **Hydromechanical Stage (Cochlea):** Pressure waves propagate along the basilar membrane. Due to varying stiffness and width, each frequency has a **Characteristic Place** of maximum displacement (high frequencies near the base, low frequencies near the apex). This is the famous **place theory of hearing** (Georg von Békésy, Nobel Prize 1961).
4. **Electrochemical Stage (Hair Cells):** Inner hair cells (~**3,500**) convert mechanical displacement into neural action potentials via the **shearing action** of the stereocilia against the tectorial membrane.
5. **Cognitive Stage (Auditory Cortex):** The brain decodes neural firing patterns into subjective attributes: **loudness, pitch, timbre, and spatial location**.

### 2.2 Psychoacoustic Phenomena Critical to Compression

#### A. Absolute Threshold of Hearing (ATH)
The minimum SPL detectable by a young, healthy ear as a function of frequency. It is the lowest curve in the famous **Fletcher-Munson (1933) / Robinson-Dadson (1956) equal-loudness contours**. It is non-flat — the ear is *most sensitive around 2–4 kHz* (coinciding with the resonance of the ear canal and the survival-critical baby-cry frequencies).

#### B. Frequency Masking (Simultaneous Masking)
A **loud tone at frequency f_mask** raises the hearing threshold in a narrow band around f_mask. A weaker tone at a nearby frequency becomes inaudible. The masked threshold can be approximated by a **spreading function**.

#### C. Temporal Masking
- **Pre-masking (Backward masking):** A loud sound masks a softer one occurring *5–20 ms before* it.
- **Post-masking (Forward masking):** A loud sound masks softer sounds for *50–200 ms after* it ends.
- This effect is exploited in audio codecs to spread quantization noise in time.

#### D. Critical Bands
A **Critical Band** is the frequency bandwidth within which a second tone interferes with the perception of a first tone. Below the band → tones heard separately. Inside the band → tones fuse and one masks the other. There are approximately **24 critical bands** spanning 0–16 kHz. The bandwidth increases with center frequency: ~**100 Hz at low f**, ~**4 kHz at high f**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table — it appears every KTU exam year.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|-------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level (SPL) | $L = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$ | $P_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ ; dB SPL |
| 2 | Loudness in Sones | $S = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$ | $L_N$ = Loudness Level in Phons |
| 3 | Loudness Level in Phons | $L_N$ = SPL of 1 kHz tone judged equally loud | dB |
| 4 | Bark Scale (Critical Band Rate) | $z = 13\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{0.76\,f}{1000}\right) + 3.5\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{f}{7500}\right)^{\!2}$ | $f$ in Hz ; $z$ in Bark |
| 5 | Frequency from Bark | $f = 52548 / (1.4 + z)$ for $z<15$; linear interp. above | Hz |
| 6 | Critical Bandwidth (approx.) | $BW \approx 25 + 75\,(1 + 1.4\,(f/1000)^2)^{0.69}$ | Hz |
| 7 | Number of Critical Bands (0–16 kHz) | $\approx 24$ | Bark |
| 8 | ATH approximation (Terhardt) | $A(f) = 3.64(f/1000)^{-0.8} - 6.5 e^{-0.6(f/1000-3.3)^2} + 10^{-3}(f/1000)^4$ | dB SPL |
| 9 | Spreading Function (masking) | Slope ≈ +27 dB/Bark (lower side), −27 dB/Bark (upper side, Schroeder) | dB/Bark |
| 10 | Hearing range | $f \in [20,\ 20000]$ Hz | Decreases with age |
| 11 | Dynamic range | $0$ to $120$ dB SPL | $0$ = ATH, $120$ = pain |
| 12 | Phase sensitivity threshold | $\gtrsim 2$ kHz | Insensitive above |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **MP3 (MPEG-1 Layer III):** Uses a 576-sample polyphase filterbank + 32-band MDCT; computes a psychoacoustic model (Model 1 or 2) to derive masking thresholds; quantizes subbands above the threshold.
- **AAC, Opus, Vorbis:** All rely on the same psychoacoustic principles but with more sophisticated spreading models.
- **Hearing Aids:** Boost frequencies where the patient's ATH is elevated (audiogram-based amplification).
- **Noise Cancellation:** Active noise-cancelling headphones exploit the ear's phase insensitivity and temporal masking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sound Pressure Level (dB SPL) from a Physical Pressure
**Problem:** A loudspeaker produces a sound with RMS pressure $P = 0.2$ Pa. Express its level in dB SPL.

**Given:** $P_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa, $P = 0.2$ Pa.

**Step 1 — Write the definition of dB SPL.**
The decibel scale for acoustic pressure is defined as the logarithmic ratio of the measured RMS pressure to a reference pressure:

$$
L_{\text{SPL}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**
We substitute $P = 0.2$ Pa and $P_0 = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa into the formula:

$$
L_{\text{SPL}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.2}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio inside the logarithm.**

$$
\frac{0.2}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.2}{0.000020} = 10{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the logarithm.**

$$
\log_{10}(10{,}000) = \log_{10}(10^4) = 4
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by 20.**

$$
L_{\text{SPL}} = 20 \times 4 = 80\ \text{dB SPL}
$$

**Final Answer:** The loudspeaker produces **80 dB SPL** (a typical loud conversation level).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Converting Frequency to Critical Band Rate (Bark)
**Problem:** A pure tone of $f = 1000$ Hz is played. Compute its position on the Bark scale.

**Given:** $f = 1000$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Recall the empirical Traunmüller / Zwicker formula.**

$$
z = 13 \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.76\,f}{1000}\right) + 3.5 \arctan\!\left(\frac{f}{7500}\right)^{\!2}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the first arctan term.**

$$
\frac{0.76 \times 1000}{1000} = 0.76
$$

$$
\arctan(0.76) \approx 0.6488\ \text{rad}
$$

$$
13 \times 0.6488 \approx 8.4344
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the second arctan term.**

$$
\frac{1000}{7500} = 0.1333
$$

$$
(0.1333)^2 = 0.01777
$$

$$
\arctan(0.01777) \approx 0.01777\ \text{rad}\ \text{(small-angle approx.)}
$$

$$
3.5 \times 0.01777 \approx 0.0622
$$

**Step 4 — Sum both terms.**

$$
z = 8.4344 + 0.0622 = 8.4966\ \text{Bark}
$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check using the inverse formula.**
For low Bark values ($z < 15$):

$$
f \approx \frac{52548}{1.4 + z} = \frac{52548}{1.4 + 8.4966} = \frac{52548}{9.8966} \approx 5310\ \text{Hz}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The "inverse" approximation $f = 52548/(1.4+z)$ is only valid in the **low-frequency regime**. At 1 kHz the linear regime ends; use the full Zwicker relation for accurate reverse conversions.

**Final Answer:** $z \approx \mathbf{8.5\ \text{Bark}}$ — 1 kHz lies near the middle of the 9th critical band.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Loudness Conversion (Phons → Sones)
**Problem:** A tone has a loudness level of 60 phons. Compute its loudness in sones.

**Given:** $L_N = 60$ phons.

**Step 1 — Recall the sone scale definition.**
By definition, **1 sone = 40 phons**. A doubling of perceived loudness corresponds to a +10 phon increase.

$$
S = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $L_N = 60$.**

$$
S = 2^{(60 - 40)/10} = 2^{20/10} = 2^2
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
S = 4\ \text{sones}
$$

**Final Answer:** A 60-phon tone has a perceived loudness of **4 sones** (i.e., "4× as loud" as the 40-phon reference).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Where Each Effect Is Used in Codecs

| Psychoacoustic Phenomenon | Compression Strategy That Exploits It | Example Codec |
|---------------------------|---------------------------------------|---------------|
| ATH (silence below threshold) | Discard subband energy below ATH | All MP3/AAC |
| Frequency masking | Quantize masked subbands coarsely | MP3, AAC, Opus |
| Temporal masking | Spread quantization noise in time | Vorbis, Opus |
| Critical band resolution | Use non-uniform (Bark) subband widths | MP3 polyphase filterbank |
| Phase insensitivity | Real-valued MDCT (no need for phase bits) | AAC, Opus |
| Binaural masking (MP3/SBC) | Joint-stereo mid/side coding | MP3 joint stereo |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Sound to Perception Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sound Source] --> B[Outer Ear Pinna and Canal]
    B --> C[Mechanical Pressure Gain approx 15 dB]
    C --> D[Eardrum Vibration]
    D --> E[Middle Ear Ossicles Malleus Incus Stapes]
    E --> F[Impedance Matching approx 22x]
    F --> G[Cochlea Basilar Membrane]
    G --> H[Place Dependent Frequency Selectivity]
    H --> I[Inner Hair Cell Transduction]
    I --> J[Auditory Nerve Spike Train]
    J --> K[Brainstem and Auditory Cortex]
    K --> L[Perceived Attributes Pitch Loudness Timbre]
    L --> M[Perceptual Model in Audio Codec]
    M --> N[Bit Allocation and Quantization Decision]
```

### 4.2 Critical Band Partitioning (Bark Scale Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Full Audio Band 0 to 20 kHz] --> B[Critical Band 1: 0 to 100 Hz]
    A --> C[Critical Band 2: 100 to 200 Hz]
    A --> D[Critical Band 3: 200 to 300 Hz]
    A --> E[Critical Band 4: 300 to 400 Hz]
    A --> F[Critical Bands 5 to 24]
    F --> G[Higher Bands Wider Bandwidth]
    B --> H[ATH Curve 0 dB to 4 kHz dip]
    C --> H
    D --> H
    E --> H
    H --> I[Masking Threshold Applied Per Band]
    I --> J[Bit Allocation Below Threshold Discarded]
```

### 4.3 Masking Spreading Function Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Masker Tone at f_mask] --> B[Masking Curve Spreads in Bark]
    B --> C[Lower Side Slope plus 27 dB per Bark]
    B --> D[Upper Side Slope minus 27 dB per Bark]
    C --> E[Masked Region Below Threshold]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Codec Discards Components in Masked Region]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-level functional architecture** is used here because a true anatomical cross-section of the cochlea cannot be represented in Mermaid. The flowchart preserves the **signal-flow logic** that the KTU examiner expects in a Module 4 question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the absolute threshold of hearing (ATH). Why is it non-flat across frequency?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Absolute Threshold of Hearing (ATH)** is the minimum sound pressure level (in dB SPL) that can be detected by a young, healthy human listener as a function of frequency. It is defined with reference to $P_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ at 1 kHz.

The ATH is **non-flat** because the human ear is most sensitive around **2–5 kHz** (where ATH ≈ 0 dB SPL) and progressively less sensitive at very low frequencies (≈ 70 dB SPL at 20 Hz) and very high frequencies (≈ 20 dB SPL at 16 kHz). This is due to the **resonance of the ear canal (~2.7 kHz)** and the mechanical properties of the basilar membrane. Audio codecs (MP3, AAC) use the ATH as a frequency-dependent lower bound below which no bits need be allocated.

*(3 marks: 1 — definition, 1 — non-flatness reason, 1 — codec relevance.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between frequency masking and temporal masking. Why are both useful in audio compression?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Frequency (Simultaneous) Masking | Temporal (Non-Simultaneous) Masking |
|---|---|---|
| When | Both tones present **at the same time** | Tones separated in **time** |
| Mechanism | Strong tone raises threshold in its critical band | Strong tone raises threshold for ~5–200 ms before/after |
| Use in codecs | Spectral bit allocation | Spreading quantization noise across time |
| Slope | ~27 dB/Bark spreading function | Exponential decay (post-) and pre- masking curve |

**Why useful in compression:** Both allow the encoder to identify **perceptually irrelevant** signal components. By quantizing these components more coarsely (or to zero), bits are saved without audible degradation. MP3, AAC, and Opus all compute masking thresholds using both effects.

*(3 marks: 1 — distinction table, 1 — definition, 1 — codec use.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of the human auditory system** from outer ear to auditory cortex. Discuss the role of the **basilar membrane** in frequency analysis. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **Bark scale** formula and compute the **critical band rate** for $f = 4$ kHz. Also compute the **critical bandwidth** at this frequency using the standard approximation. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The human auditory system can be divided into four functional blocks:

1. **Outer Ear (Pinna + Auditory Canal):** Captures sound waves and channels them to the tympanic membrane (eardrum). It acts as a **passive acoustic resonator** with a peak gain of ~10–15 dB near 2.7 kHz.

2. **Middle Ear (Tympanic Membrane + 3 Ossicles):** The malleus, incus, and stapes form a mechanical lever that **matches the acoustic impedance** between air (low impedance) and the cochlear fluid (high impedance), achieving an efficient ~22× pressure gain. Without this, ~99.9% of energy would reflect.

3. **Inner Ear (Cochlea + Basilar Membrane):** The basilar membrane is a tapered, fluid-coupled structure. Its **stiffness decreases and width increases** from base (high f) to apex (low f). Each frequency produces a maximum displacement at a unique **characteristic place** (Békésy's travelling wave). This implements a **continuous mechanical Fourier transform** — a healthy cochlea resolves ~24 critical bands.

4. **Central Auditory System (Auditory Nerve → Brainstem → Cortex):** Inner hair cells convert mechanical displacement into neural spikes. The brain decodes the spike train into perceived pitch, loudness, and timbre.

**Role of the Basilar Membrane in Frequency Analysis:**
The membrane is **non-uniform** (stiff at base, floppy at apex). When a tone of frequency $f$ enters, a **travelling wave** propagates from base to apex; the wave amplitude peaks at a position $x(f)$ where the local resonance matches $f$. High frequencies peak near the base; low frequencies near the apex. This **place theory** provides the cochlea's spectral resolution and is the physiological basis of the **critical-band concept** exploited by perceptual codecs.

*(7 marks: 1 — outer ear, 1 — middle ear, 1 — cochlea, 2 — basilar membrane mechanics, 1 — central system, 1 — place theory)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Bark Scale Formula:**
The Bark scale is an empirically derived psychoacoustic scale proposed by Eberhard Zwicker (1961). It partitions the audible spectrum into **24 critical bands** of unequal width. The standard formula is:

$$
z = 13 \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.76\,f}{1000}\right) + 3.5 \arctan\!\left(\frac{f}{7500}\right)^{\!2}
$$

where $f$ is in Hz and $z$ is in Bark.

**Step 2 — Evaluate at $f = 4000$ Hz.**

First term:

$$
\frac{0.76 \times 4000}{1000} = 3.04
$$

$$
\arctan(3.04) \approx 1.2522\ \text{rad}
$$

$$
13 \times 1.2522 \approx 16.279
$$

Second term:

$$
\frac{4000}{7500} = 0.5333
$$

$$
(0.5333)^2 = 0.2844
$$

$$
\arctan(0.2844) \approx 0.2770\ \text{rad}
$$

$$
3.5 \times 0.2770 \approx 0.9696
$$

Sum:

$$
z = 16.279 + 0.9696 = \mathbf{17.25\ \text{Bark}}
$$

So 4 kHz lies in the **17th critical band** (Bark index ≈ 17.25).

**Step 3 — Critical Bandwidth at 4 kHz.**
Standard approximation (Schroeder):

$$
BW \approx 25 + 75\,(1 + 1.4\,(f/1000)^2)^{0.69}
$$

Substitute $f = 4000$ Hz:

$$
(f/1000)^2 = 16
$$

$$
1.4 \times 16 = 22.4
$$

$$
1 + 22.4 = 23.4
$$

$$
(23.4)^{0.69} \approx 9.10\ \text{(using log: } 0.69 \times \log_{10}(23.4) = 0.69 \times 1.3692 = 0.945 \Rightarrow 10^{0.945} \approx 8.81\text{)}
$$

$$
BW \approx 25 + 75 \times 8.81 \approx 25 + 660.75 \approx \mathbf{686\ \text{Hz}}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- $z(4\ \text{kHz}) \approx 17.25$ Bark
- Critical Bandwidth $BW \approx 686$ Hz

*(7 marks: 1 — formula statement, 3 — derivation, 1 — Bark value, 2 — bandwidth calculation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Model Paper]`

**(a)** What is **masking**? Derive the **dB SPL level of a sound** whose RMS pressure is **0.05 Pa**, and explain how its masking effect on a nearby 1 kHz tone would be calculated using the **spreading function**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain **Fletcher-Munson equal-loudness contours**. How are they used in audio codec design? Sketch the qualitative shape of the curves for 40, 60, and 80 phons. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Definition of Masking:**
**Masking** is a psychoacoustic phenomenon in which the perception of one sound (the *maskee*) is reduced or eliminated by the presence of another sound (the *masker*). It forms the cornerstone of perceptual audio coding.

**Step 1 — Convert 0.05 Pa to dB SPL.**
Using $L = 20 \log_{10}(P/P_0)$ with $P_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$:

$$
L = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.05}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(2500)
$$

$$
\log_{10}(2500) = \log_{10}(2.5 \times 10^3) = 3 + 0.3979 = 3.3979
$$

$$
L = 20 \times 3.3979 = 67.96\ \text{dB SPL} \approx 68\ \text{dB SPL}
$$

**Step 2 — Effect on a nearby 1 kHz tone (masking computation).**
The 68 dB SPL masker raises the hearing threshold in its critical band. Procedure:

(i) Compute the masker's Bark position: $z_m = z(f_m)$ using the Bark formula.

(ii) Compute the probe tone's Bark position: $z_p = z(1000) = 8.5$ Bark.

(iii) Compute the **distance** $\Delta z = \vert z_m - z_p \vert$ in Bark.

(iv) Apply the spreading function:

$$
S(\Delta z) = 
\begin{cases}
27 \cdot \Delta z, & \Delta z > 0 \text{ (masker above probe)} \\
-27 \cdot \Delta z, & \Delta z < 0 \text{ (masker below probe)}
\end{cases}
$$

(v) The masked threshold at the probe's frequency is:

$$
T_{\text{masked}} = L_{\text{masker}} + S(\Delta z)\ \text{dB SPL}
$$

(vi) If the probe tone's SPL is **below** $T_{\text{masked}}$, it is inaudible and the codec allocates **zero bits** to it.

**Practical example:** If the masker is at 1.2 kHz, $\Delta z \approx 0.8$ Bark, so $S = -27 \times 0.8 = -21.6$ dB. The masked threshold becomes $68 - 21.6 = 46.4$ dB SPL. Any tone at 1 kHz quieter than 46.4 dB SPL is masked.

*(7 marks: 1 — masking definition, 2 — dB calculation, 3 — spreading-function procedure, 1 — practical example)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Fletcher-Munson Equal-Loudness Contours:**
Equal-loudness contours (first measured by **Fletcher and Munson, 1933**, refined by **Robinson and Dadson, 1956**, and standardized as **ISO 226:2003**) are curves on a frequency-vs-SPL graph that connect points perceived to be **equally loud**. The vertical axis is the SPL required (at each frequency) for a tone to sound as loud as a 1 kHz reference tone at a given level (in phons).

**Key Observations:**

- At **1 kHz**, the phon and dB SPL values coincide by definition.
- Below 500 Hz and above 5 kHz, the ear is **less sensitive**, so a much higher SPL is required to match the perceived loudness of a 1 kHz tone.
- The contours are **flat near 3–4 kHz** (most sensitive region) and rise sharply at the frequency extremes.
- All contours become flatter as loudness increases — at very high SPLs (≥ 100 phons), the ear's response approaches flatness.

**Use in Audio Codec Design:**

- The **lowest contour (0 phon)** defines the **absolute threshold of hearing (ATH)**, the floor below which nothing is audible.
- Codecs compute the ATH at every subband and use it as a **masking floor** for bit allocation.
- Modern codecs (AAC, Opus) use a refined ATH model such as the **Terhardt** or **ISO 226:2003** formula.

**Qualitative Sketch of 40, 60, 80 Phon Contours:**

```
SPL (dB)
  100 |                          *  (80 phon)
   80 |                  *  *  *      
   60 |              *      (60 phon)
   40 |        *  *           (40 phon)
   20 |    *  *
    0 |*__*________________________  frequency (Hz)
      20  100 1k   3k   10k  20k
```

All curves are **U-shaped**, dipping lowest near 3–4 kHz, with the curves compressing towards flatness as the phon level increases.

*(7 marks: 2 — concept of equal loudness, 1 — ISO 226 reference, 2 — ATH link to codecs, 2 — qualitative sketch description)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the reference pressure** $P_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ in dB SPL — losing 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Mixing up dB SPL and dB FS** (digital full-scale). KTU exam questions specifically ask for **dB SPL** in this module.
> 3. **Reporting Bark values without units** — always write "*z = 8.5 Bark*" not "*z = 8.5*".
> 4. **Using $1.4\,(f/1000)^2$ without the +1 inside the parenthesis** in the critical-bandwidth formula — a classic bracket error.
> 5. **Sketching Fletcher-Munson curves as straight lines or horizontal** — the U-shape is the whole point of the question.
> 6. **Confusing phons and sones**: phons = *loudness level* (matched to 1 kHz); sones = *loudness* (perceived magnitude, doubles every +10 phons).

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- The **human ear** = outer + middle + inner ear + neural pathway. The cochlea is a **biological spectrum analyzer**.
- **Basilar membrane** performs **place-based frequency analysis** (Békésy, 1961 Nobel Prize).
- Audible range: **20 Hz – 20 kHz**; dynamic range **0 – 120 dB SPL**.
- **Reference pressure** $P_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$; **dB SPL** = $20 \log_{10}(P/P_0)$.
- **ATH (Absolute Threshold of Hearing)** is **non-flat**, minimum near **2–4 kHz**.
- **Fletcher-Munson contours** = equal-loudness curves; lowest contour = ATH.
- **Masking types:** frequency (simultaneous) and temporal (pre/post).
- **Critical bands:** ~**24 Bark**, increasing bandwidth with frequency.
- **Bark formula:** $z = 13 \arctan(0.76f/1000) + 3.5 \arctan((f/7500)^2)$.
- **Critical bandwidth:** $BW \approx 25 + 75(1 + 1.4(f/1000)^2)^{0.69}$ Hz.
- **Phons** = loudness *level*; **Sones** = loudness *magnitude* ($S = 2^{(L_N-40)/10}$).
- **Ear is phase-deaf** above ~2 kHz → MDCT (real coefficients) suffices.
- **Compression uses:** ATH, frequency masking, temporal masking, critical bands, phase deafness.
- **Codecs using this model:** MP3, AAC, Opus, Vorbis, AC-3, Dolby Digital.
- **Key constants to memorize:** 20 Hz, 20 kHz, 20 μPa, 0 dB SPL at 1 kHz, 24 Bark, 120 dB dynamic range.
- **Important formula pitfalls:** no absolute value bars in tables (use `\vert`); show every step in derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heinrich Georg Barkhausen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST524 - DATA COMPRESSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heinrich Georg Barkhausen

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Scientist: Heinrich Georg Barkhausen (1881 – 1956)

**Heinrich Georg Barkhausen** was a **German physicist** born on **2 December 1881** in Bremen, Germany, and he passed away on **20 February 1956** in Dresden. He is one of the most influential figures in 20th-century applied physics, credited with multiple foundational discoveries that bridge **ferromagnetism, electronics, and acoustics**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Barkhausen's Four Major Contributions to Engineering & Physics:**
> 1. **Barkhausen Effect (1919)** – Discovery of discrete jumps in magnetization in ferromagnetic materials.
> 2. **Barkhausen Criterion (1921)** – Mathematical condition for sustained sinusoidal oscillations in feedback amplifiers.
> 3. **Barkhausen Noise** – The audible acoustic emissions produced by the Barkhausen effect.
> 4. **The Bark Scale (1946)** – A psychoacoustic frequency scale jointly developed with Eberhard Zwicker, measuring perceived frequency in human hearing.

He served as a Professor of Communication Engineering at the **Technische Hochschule Dresden** (now TU Dresden) and was a Fellow of the prestigious **Leopoldina German National Academy of Sciences**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Barkhausen Effect

Imagine you are pushing a heavy cargo **crate across a rough, gravel-strewn floor**.

- If you push gently, the crate **does not move** (small applied field, no net magnetization change).
- Push a little harder and the crate **suddenly jerks forward**, then stops again. It moves in **discrete hops**, not smoothly.
- Each "hop" represents the **irreversible, sudden movement of a magnetic domain wall** past an impurity or defect in the crystal lattice.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Barkhausen Effect** states that when a magnetic field is applied to a ferromagnetic material, the resulting magnetization changes are **not continuous**. Instead, they occur in **discrete, irreversible jumps** caused by domain walls snapping past microscopic pinning sites (defects, grain boundaries, dislocations). These jumps are accompanied by **short audible pulses (Barkhausen noise)** detectable as a "rushing" sound in a coil wound around the sample.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Barkhausen Oscillation Criterion

Think of a **swing in a playground**.

- To keep the swing oscillating forever, the child must push it at **exactly the right moment** in the cycle (synchronization = phase condition).
- The push must be **just strong enough** to compensate for friction (energy condition = magnitude condition).
- Barkhausen's criterion is the **engineer's formal mathematical version** of "push at the right time, with the right force," but for electronic feedback oscillators (e.g., sine-wave generators, RF carrier sources, audio oscillators).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Barkhausen Criterion** specifies the *necessary and sufficient* condition for a linear feedback amplifier to operate as a **sustained sinusoidal oscillator**:
> - The **magnitude of the loop gain** $A\beta$ must equal **unity**.
> - The **total phase shift** around the closed loop must be **zero** (or an integer multiple of $2\pi$).

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics, Constants & Notations

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $A$ | Forward-path (amplifier) gain | Dimensionless (or dB) |
| $\beta$ | Feedback-network transfer function | Dimensionless |
| $f$ | Frequency | **Hertz (Hz)** |
| $z$ | Critical-band rate (Bark scale) | **Bark** |
| $\mu_0$ | Permeability of free space | $\mathbf{4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ H/m}$ |
| $H$ | Magnetic field intensity | **A/m** |
| $B$ | Magnetic flux density | **Tesla (T)** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Bark** unit is named in his honour. $1\ \text{Bark}$ corresponds to one **critical band** of human hearing (the cochlear filter bandwidth at threshold). The auditory frequency range $0\ \text{Hz}$ to $16\ \text{kHz}$ maps to approximately $0$ to $24\ \text{Bark}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Barkhausen Criterion — Theoretical Foundation

Consider a **basic negative-feedback amplifier** with forward gain $A(j\omega)$ and feedback factor $\beta(j\omega)$. The closed-loop transfer function is:

$$
T(j\omega) = \frac{A(j\omega)}{1 + A(j\omega)\,\beta(j\omega)}
$$

For the system to **sustain a sinusoidal output without an external input**, the denominator must vanish, leading to **infinite closed-loop gain** at that frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Setting the denominator to zero** is the genesis of the **Barkhausen criterion**. The condition $1 + A\beta = 0$ is the *mathematical fingerprint* of every sinusoidal oscillator in the universe of linear feedback theory.

---

## 2.2 Why and How: Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Loop Transmission Analysis:**
The signal travels: Input $\rightarrow$ Amplifier (gain $A$) $\rightarrow$ Feedback network (gain $\beta$) $\rightarrow$ Summing junction $\rightarrow$ back to the amplifier. The product $A\beta$ is the **loop gain**.

**Step 2 — Self-Sustained Oscillation Requirement:**
A real oscillator must continue generating output even when its input is **zero**. The only way the closed-loop expression produces a finite output from a zero input is for the **denominator to approach zero**.

**Step 3 — Magnitude Condition:**
For oscillation, $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$. The amplifier must **exactly replenish** the energy lost (or fed back) in each cycle. If $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$, the oscillation decays; if $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$, the output grows until non-linearity clips it (distortion).

**Step 4 — Phase Condition:**
The total phase shift around the loop must be an **integer multiple of $2\pi$ radians** ($0^\circ$ or $360^\circ$). This ensures **positive feedback** — every signal that traverses the loop returns **in phase**, reinforcing itself.

**Step 5 — Practical Implementation:**
The phase condition is what determines the **frequency of oscillation** $f_0$, while the magnitude condition sets the **amplitude stability boundary**. Practical designs (Wien bridge, RC phase-shift, Colpitts, Hartley, Crystal) differ only in *how* they satisfy these two conditions.

---

## 2.3 The Barkhausen Effect — Physics of Discrete Magnetization

In a **ferromagnetic material** (Fe, Co, Ni, Gd, and their alloys), the bulk sample consists of **magnetic domains** — small regions where atomic magnetic moments are aligned uniformly. Adjacent domains are separated by **domain walls** (Bloch / Néel walls).

> [!NOTE]
> When an external field $H$ is applied, the domain walls **move** to enlarge the domains whose magnetization is aligned with $H$. However, the walls **do not glide smoothly**. They are **pinned** by lattice defects, impurities, grain boundaries, and dislocations. As $H$ increases, the wall bows out elastically, then **snaps catastrophically** past a pinning site — a single Barkhausen jump. This jumping releases a tiny burst of elastic energy, the **Barkhausen noise**, which can be heard as a crackling "snow" sound in a pickup coil.

**Energy relation for a single jump:**
$$
\Delta E_{\text{acoustic}} = \mu_0\, M_s\, \Delta H \cdot V_{\text{domain}}
$$

where:
- $M_s$ is the **saturation magnetization** (A/m),
- $\Delta H$ is the **field increment** needed to unpin the wall (A/m),
- $V_{\text{domain}}$ is the **active domain volume** (m³).

---

## 2.4 The Bark Scale (Zwicker-Barkhausen Scale)

The human ear analyzes sound using **non-uniform bandwidth filters** along the basilar membrane. To quantify perceived frequency, Barkhausen and Zwicker proposed a **warped frequency scale**:

$$
z = \frac{26.81}{1 + \frac{1960\ \text{Hz}}{f}} - 0.53
$$

The inverse mapping (Bark $\rightarrow$ Hz) is:
$$
f = 1960\ \text{Hz} \left( \frac{1}{26.81 \big/ (z + 0.53)} - 1 \right)^{-1}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Bark scale is foundational in **audio coding** (MP3, AAC) and **perceptual models** for audio compression — directly relevant to advanced data-compression study paths. It models how the human auditory system groups frequencies into approximately 24 critical bands across the $20\ \text{Hz}$ to $16\ \text{kHz}$ range.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Condition | Physical Meaning | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ | Loop-gain magnitude condition | **Sine-wave oscillator design** |
| 2 | $\angle A\beta = 0, \pm 2\pi, \pm 4\pi,\dots$ | Loop-phase condition | Determines **oscillation frequency** |
| 3 | $1 + A(j\omega_0)\beta(j\omega_0) = 0$ | Characteristic equation | Root of closed-loop transfer function |
| 4 | $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ (RC oscillator) | Frequency of RC-network feedback | **Wien bridge, Phase-shift** |
| 5 | $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ (LC oscillator) | Resonant frequency | **Colpitts, Hartley, Crystal** |
| 6 | $z = \dfrac{26.81}{1 + 1960 \big/ f} - 0.53$ | Frequency $\rightarrow$ Bark conversion | **Psychoacoustic coding** |
| 7 | $M(t) = \sum_k \Delta M_k\, u(t - t_k)$ | Sum of Barkhausen jumps | **Magnetization noise modeling** |
| 8 | $\Delta E = \mu_0 M_s \Delta H V$ | Energy per Barkhausen jump | **NDE of ferromagnetic materials** |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Telecommunications & RF Engineering:** Every RF carrier generator, local oscillator in a superheterodyne receiver, and tone generator relies on the **Barkhausen criterion** to lock onto a stable frequency.
2. **Materials Testing:** Barkhausen noise analysis (BNA) is a **non-destructive evaluation (NDE)** method used to detect residual stress, surface hardness, and grain structure in steel rails, turbine blades, and pressure vessels.
3. **Audio & Speech Coding:** The **Bark scale** underpins perceptual audio codecs — MP3, AAC, Ogg Vorbis — that exploit auditory masking within each critical band to drop imperceptible bits.
4. **Magnetometry & Sensors:** Modern spintronic sensors and fluxgate magnetometers incorporate Barkhausen-switching principles for low-field detection.
5. **Medical Imaging (early work):** Barkhausen's acoustic emissions inspired the development of **magneto-acoustic** imaging prototypes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Barkhausen Criterion

### **Starting Point: The Negative-Feedback Amplifier**

Consider the canonical single-loop feedback block. The forward path contains an amplifier with gain $A(j\omega)$, and the feedback network (typically passive) has a transfer function $\beta(j\omega)$. Let $X_i(j\omega)$ be the source signal and $X_o(j\omega)$ the output. The error signal at the summing junction is:

$$
X_\varepsilon(j\omega) = X_i(j\omega) - \beta(j\omega)\, X_o(j\omega)
$$

The forward amplifier multiplies the error by $A$:

$$
X_o(j\omega) = A(j\omega)\, X_\varepsilon(j\omega)
$$

Substituting the expression for $X_\varepsilon$:

$$
X_o(j\omega) = A(j\omega) \left[ X_i(j\omega) - \beta(j\omega) X_o(j\omega) \right]
$$

Distribute the gain:

$$
X_o(j\omega) = A(j\omega)\, X_i(j\omega) - A(j\omega)\beta(j\omega) X_o(j\omega)
$$

Collect $X_o$ terms on the left-hand side:

$$
X_o(j\omega) + A(j\omega)\beta(j\omega) X_o(j\omega) = A(j\omega)\, X_i(j\omega)
$$

Factor $X_o$:

$$
X_o(j\omega) \left[ 1 + A(j\omega)\beta(j\omega) \right] = A(j\omega)\, X_i(j\omega)
$$

Thus the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$
T(j\omega) = \frac{X_o(j\omega)}{X_i(j\omega)} = \frac{A(j\omega)}{1 + A(j\omega)\beta(j\omega)}
$$

### **Setting the Oscillation Condition**

For an oscillator, the system must produce a **non-zero output $X_o$ even when the input $X_i = 0$** (free-running). The only way this is mathematically possible is if the denominator becomes zero at a specific real positive frequency $\omega_0$:

$$
1 + A(j\omega_0)\beta(j\omega_0) = 0
$$

This is the **characteristic equation** of the oscillator.

### **Splitting into Magnitude and Phase**

Rewrite the equation in polar form. Let $A(j\omega) = \vert A(\omega) \vert \, e^{j\phi_A(\omega)}$ and $\beta(j\omega) = \vert \beta(\omega) \vert \, e^{j\phi_\beta(\omega)}$. Then:

$$
A(j\omega_0)\beta(j\omega_0) = \vert A(\omega_0) \vert \vert \beta(\omega_0) \vert\, e^{j[\phi_A(\omega_0) + \phi_\beta(\omega_0)]} = -1
$$

The complex number $-1$ has magnitude $1$ and phase $\pi$ (or $-\pi$, $\pm 3\pi$, $\dots$). Equating magnitudes and phases:

$$
\boxed{\; \vert A(\omega_0)\beta(\omega_0) \vert = 1 \;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; \phi_A(\omega_0) + \phi_\beta(\omega_0) = \pm 2n\pi, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots \;}
$$

**Reading of the result:**
- The first boxed equation is the **magnitude condition** — the loop must amplify by exactly unity each cycle.
- The second boxed equation is the **phase condition** — the signal must return in phase to reinforce itself (positive feedback).

> [!NOTE]
> In many textbooks, the phase condition is written as $\angle A\beta = 0$ (or $360^\circ$). This is because the negative sign $-1$ in the algebraic formulation is absorbed by **shifting the phase reference** of the amplifier (an inverting amplifier is said to contribute $180^\circ$). The numerical value is the same: total phase shift is $0 \pmod{2\pi}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bark Scale Mapping

The Barkhausen–Zwicker psychoacoustic scale approximates the **critical-band scale** of the human cochlea. The mapping is anchored by two experimental points:
- $f = 500\ \text{Hz} \;\Rightarrow\; z \approx 4.5\ \text{Bark}$ (mid-frequency region)
- $f = 4000\ \text{Hz} \;\Rightarrow\; z \approx 16.5\ \text{Bark}$

A general rational form $z(f) = \dfrac{a}{1 + b/f} + c$ is fitted to the data. The least-squares fit yields:

$$
z = \frac{26.81}{1 + \frac{1960\ \text{Hz}}{f}} - 0.53
$$

**Worked check at $f = 1000\ \text{Hz}$:**

$$
z = \frac{26.81}{1 + \frac{1960}{1000}} - 0.53
$$

$$
z = \frac{26.81}{1 + 1.96} - 0.53
$$

$$
z = \frac{26.81}{2.96} - 0.53
$$

$$
z = 9.0574 - 0.53 = 8.527\ \text{Bark}
$$

This corresponds well with the experimentally measured critical-band center at $1\ \text{kHz}$ (about $8.5\ \text{Bark}$).

**Worked check at $f = 4000\ \text{Hz}$:**

$$
z = \frac{26.81}{1 + \frac{1960}{4000}} - 0.53
$$

$$
z = \frac{26.81}{1 + 0.49} - 0.53
$$

$$
z = \frac{26.81}{1.49} - 0.53
$$

$$
z = 17.993 - 0.53 = 17.46\ \text{Bark}
$$

The accepted experimental value at $4\ \text{kHz}$ is around $17.4$–$17.5\ \text{Bark}$, confirming the formula.

---

## 3.3 Energy Released in a Single Barkhausen Jump

A domain wall sweeps an area $A_w$ under the influence of an applied field step $\Delta H$. The change in magnetic moment is $\Delta m = M_s A_w d$, where $d$ is the wall displacement. The energy dissipated as acoustic and thermal energy is:

$$
\Delta E = \mu_0 M_s \Delta H \cdot V_{\text{active}}
$$

For a typical iron sample with $M_s = 1.7 \times 10^6\ \text{A/m}$, $\Delta H = 10\ \text{A/m}$, and $V = 10^{-15}\ \text{m}^3$ (a single domain), we get:

$$
\Delta E = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) (1.7 \times 10^6)(10)(10^{-15})
$$

$$
\Delta E \approx 2.14 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{J}
$$

This is roughly $10^5$ times the thermal energy $k_B T$ at room temperature ($4.14 \times 10^{-21}\ \text{J}$), confirming that a single jump is **classically detectable** by a sensitive pickup coil — yet another reason Barkhausen's 1919 experiment was feasible with the electronics of his era.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Barkhausen Oscillator Feedback Loop

```mermaid
graph LR
    inNode["Input X_i"] --> sumNode(("Summing Junction"))
    sumNode --> errNode["Error Signal X_eps"]
    errNode --> ampNode["Amplifier Gain A"]
    ampNode --> outNode["Output X_o"]
    outNode --> fbNode["Feedback Network Beta"]
    fbNode --> sumNode
    ampNode -.phase.-> phaseNode["phi_A + phi_Beta = 2n pi"]
    ampNode -.magnitude.-> magNode["mag A Beta = 1"]
    outNode --> oscNode["Sustained Sinusoid at omega_0"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The blue nodes (`inNode`, `errNode`, `outNode`, `fbNode`) trace the **signal path** through the loop.
- The `sumNode` (drawn as a circle) is the **subtracting summing junction** of the negative feedback configuration.
- The dashed arrows from `ampNode` to `phaseNode` and `magNode` represent the **Barkhausen conditions** that the loop gain must simultaneously satisfy.
- The final node `oscNode` represents the **sustained sinusoidal output** at the design frequency $\omega_0$.

---

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Barkhausen Effect in a Ferromagnet

```mermaid
graph TB
    matNode["Ferromagnetic Specimen"] --> domNode["Magnetic Domain Structure"]
    domNode --> wallNode["Domain Walls"]
    wallNode --> pinNode["Pinning Sites Defects Impurities"]
    pinNode --> fieldNode["External Field H Increases"]
    fieldNode --> bowNode["Wall Bows Elastically"]
    bowNode --> snapNode["Wall Snaps Past Pinning Site"]
    snapNode --> jumpNode["Discrete Barkhausen Jump in M"]
    jumpNode --> coilNode["Pickup Coil Detects Delta Phi"]
    coilNode --> ampNode["Amplifier and Audio Output"]
    ampNode --> soundNode["Barkhausen Noise Audible Crackle"]
    jumpNode --> ndeNode["NDE Signal for Stress Analysis"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The top-to-bottom flow represents the **chronology** of a single Barkhausen event.
- The path from `coilNode` to `soundNode` corresponds to the **direct acoustic listening experiment** that Barkhausen himself performed in 1919.
- The alternative branch from `jumpNode` to `ndeNode` represents the modern **Non-Destructive Evaluation (NDE)** application for residual stress measurement in industrial components.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Three Barkhausen-Locked Oscillator Topologies

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph wienNode["Wien Bridge Oscillator"]
        wien1["RC Series Arm"] --> wien2["Op-Amp"]
        wien2 --> wien3["RC Parallel Arm"]
        wien3 --> wien1
    end
    subgraph rcpsNode["RC Phase Shift Oscillator"]
        rcps1["3 RC Sections 60 deg each"] --> rcps2["Inverting Op-Amp 180 deg"]
        rcps2 --> rcps3["Total Phase 360 deg"]
        rcps3 --> rcps1
    end
    subgraph collNode["Colpitts LC Oscillator"]
        coll1["Transistor Amplifier"] --> coll2["LC Tank with C1 C2"]
        coll2 --> coll3["Capacitive Voltage Divider Feedback"]
        coll3 --> coll1
    end
    wienNode -.satisfies.-> bcNode["Barkhausen Criterion mag A beta equals 1 phase equals 0 mod 2pi"]
    rcpsNode -.satisfies.-> bcNode
    collNode -.satisfies.-> bcNode
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Three subgraphs represent the **three classic oscillator families** that all obey the same Barkhausen criterion.
- Note the **distinct feedback mechanism** in each: Wien uses a frequency-selective RC network, RC Phase Shift uses three cascading RC sections, and Colpitts uses a capacitive divider across an LC tank.
- The single node `bcNode` (bottom right) shows the **unifying principle**: despite different hardware, every sinusoidal oscillator must satisfy the same two equations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. State the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations and explain the role of each condition.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The Barkhausen criterion specifies that for a linear feedback amplifier to operate as a sustained sinusoidal oscillator, two conditions must be simultaneously satisfied at the desired frequency of oscillation $\omega_0$:

1. **Magnitude condition:** The loop gain magnitude must be exactly unity,
   $\quad \vert A(j\omega_0)\beta(j\omega_0) \vert = 1$.
   This ensures the amplifier replenishes the energy lost per cycle, keeping the amplitude from decaying or growing.

2. **Phase condition:** The total phase shift around the loop must be an integer multiple of $2\pi$ radians,
   $\quad \angle A(j\omega_0) + \angle \beta(j\omega_0) = 2n\pi, \;\; n \in \mathbb{Z}$.
   This ensures positive feedback — the signal returns in phase and reinforces itself.

Both conditions together form the characteristic equation $1 + A(j\omega_0)\beta(j\omega_0) = 0$.

---

**Q2. What is the Barkhausen effect? Name the unit named after Barkhausen in psychoacoustics.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The **Barkhausen effect** (1919) is the phenomenon in which the magnetization of a ferromagnetic material changes **discontinuously** when a smoothly varying external magnetic field is applied. These discrete jumps are caused by the **sudden, irreversible movement of magnetic domain walls** past microscopic pinning sites such as lattice defects, grain boundaries, and impurities. Each jump produces a short acoustic emission called **Barkhausen noise**, detectable as a crackling sound in a pickup coil.

The unit named after Barkhausen in psychoacoustics is the **Bark**, which quantifies perceived frequency on a scale based on the critical-band filtering of the human cochlea. The audible range $0\ \text{Hz}$ to $16\ \text{kHz}$ maps to approximately $0$ to $24\ \text{Bark}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester, Model Paper Pattern]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. (a)** Derive the Barkhausen criterion from the closed-loop transfer function of a feedback amplifier. State and explain both conditions clearly. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the feedback structure:**
For a single-loop negative feedback amplifier, the closed-loop transfer function is
$$
T(j\omega) = \frac{A(j\omega)}{1 + A(j\omega)\beta(j\omega)}
$$

**Step 2 — Set input to zero for free-running oscillation:**
For sustained oscillations without external input, $X_i = 0$ but $X_o \neq 0$. The only way this is possible is if the denominator approaches zero:
$$
1 + A(j\omega_0)\beta(j\omega_0) = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to magnitude and phase form:**
Writing $A(j\omega_0)\beta(j\omega_0) = \vert A\beta \vert e^{j(\phi_A + \phi_\beta)} = -1$, and equating magnitude and phase separately:

- **Magnitude condition:** $\vert A(j\omega_0)\beta(j\omega_0) \vert = 1$
- **Phase condition:** $\phi_A(\omega_0) + \phi_\beta(\omega_0) = \pm 2n\pi, \; n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:**
- The **magnitude condition** ensures that the loop exactly compensates losses per cycle, keeping amplitude constant.
- The **phase condition** ensures that the signal returned to the input is in phase with the original, creating regenerative (positive) feedback.

If $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$, oscillation decays; if $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$, the output grows until non-linear saturation clips the waveform (distortion in practical oscillators). For stable sinusoidal output, designers target $\vert A\beta \vert$ slightly greater than 1 to overcome the onset of non-linearity, but AGC/amplitude stabilization networks are used to clamp the loop gain to exactly 1 in steady state.

---

**Q.A. (b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain how the Barkhausen criterion is satisfied in a **Wien bridge oscillator** using an op-amp. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation and the required gain condition. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**
The Wien bridge oscillator uses a non-inverting op-amp configuration. The positive feedback network is a **Wien bridge** consisting of a series RC arm ($R_1$, $C_1$) and a parallel RC arm ($R_2$, $C_2$). The negative feedback network is a resistive voltage divider ($R_f$ and $R_g$) that sets the closed-loop gain.

**Feedback factor $\beta$:**
At any frequency $\omega$, the transfer function of the Wien network is
$$
\beta(j\omega) = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}
$$
where $Z_s = R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$ and $Z_p = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{R} + j\omega C} = \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$.

Substituting and simplifying:
$$
\beta(j\omega) = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}
$$

**Magnitude condition** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ gives the required amplifier gain:
$$
A = 3 + j\left(\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)
$$
For a real (purely resistive) gain, the imaginary part must vanish, requiring:
$$
\omega_0 RC = \frac{1}{\omega_0 RC} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}
$$

Hence the **frequency of oscillation:**
$$
\boxed{\; f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \;}
$$

At this frequency, $\beta = 1/3$ (real). The **gain condition** is therefore:
$$
\boxed{\; A \geq 3 \;}
$$

For a non-inverting op-amp, $A = 1 + R_f/R_g \geq 3$, which gives the design constraint:
$$
R_f \geq 2 R_g
$$

In practice, $R_f$ is implemented as a **non-linear element** (small incandescent lamp or thermistor) whose resistance adjusts automatically to keep $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ exactly at steady state, providing low-distortion sinusoidal output.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the physical origin of the **Barkhausen effect** in ferromagnetic materials. With a suitable sketch of M vs H, describe the discrete jumps and the accompanying Barkhausen noise. Discuss at least two engineering applications. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Physical origin:**
A ferromagnetic material (Fe, Co, Ni, Gd) comprises a multitude of small **magnetic domains** within which atomic magnetic moments are aligned parallel. Adjacent domains are separated by **domain walls** (Bloch/Néel walls), and the magnetizations of neighbouring domains point in different directions to minimize the net stray-field energy. The bulk sample is typically demagnetized (zero net $M$) with a random domain orientation.

When an external field $H$ is applied, the **domain walls move** to enlarge the domains whose magnetization is parallel to $H$. However, the wall motion is **not smooth** — it is opposed by microscopic **pinning sites** such as vacancies, interstitials, dislocations, grain boundaries, and non-magnetic inclusions. The wall bows elastically between pinning points, then **snaps catastrophically** past the pin when the local field exceeds a critical value. This produces a **discrete, irreversible jump** in the bulk magnetization, the Barkhausen jump.

**M–H curve behaviour:**
On the macroscopic M–H plot, the smooth idealized curve is in reality a **staircase** of infinitesimal steps. If one zooms in, the curve shows a series of vertical jumps separated by flat segments. The size and density of the jumps depend on the material microstructure, residual stress, and grain size.

**Barkhausen noise:**
Each irreversible wall jump releases a tiny burst of elastic energy that propagates as an acoustic wave and also induces a voltage pulse in a pickup coil. Amplified and fed to a loudspeaker, this gives the characteristic "rushing" or "snow-crackle" sound — the **Barkhausen noise** first heard by Heinrich Barkhausen in 1919.

**Engineering applications:**

1. **Non-Destructive Evaluation (NDE) of Ferromagnetic Components:** Barkhausen noise analysis (BNA) is widely used to measure **residual stress, surface hardness, case depth, and grain size** in steel components such as railway tracks, gears, turbine blades, and welded pressure vessels. Tensile stress increases dislocation density, increases pinning, and increases Barkhausen noise amplitude; compressive stress reduces it.

2. **Magnetic Domain and Materials Research:** BNA is used in physics laboratories to study **domain wall dynamics, magnetic aftereffect, and creep** in thin films and amorphous ribbons, providing insight into spintronic device behaviour.

3. **Hardness and Case-Depth Testing in Gear Manufacturing:** Induction-hardened or carburized gears show distinct BNA signatures correlating with surface hardness profile, enabling rapid in-line quality control.

---

**Q.B. (b)** Derive the **Barkhausen–Zwicker (Bark) scale** formula $z = \dfrac{26.81}{1 + 1960/f} - 0.53$ and evaluate $z$ for $f = 500\ \text{Hz}$ and $f = 4\ \text{kHz}$. Comment on the role of the Bark scale in audio coding (e.g., MP3). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation outline:**
The critical-band scale of the human cochlea is well approximated by an analytic function fitted to psychophysical tuning-curve data. The rational form
$$
z = \frac{a}{1 + b/f} + c
$$
is anchored by three boundary constraints:
- At $f = 0$, $z \to 0$ (the scale starts at zero, as there is no negative frequency).
- The slope at low frequencies must match the low-frequency critical-bandwidth measurements.
- At high frequencies, the function must saturate near $z \approx 24\ \text{Bark}$ for $f \to 16\ \text{kHz}$.

Least-squares fitting of these constraints to the data of Zwicker, Flottorp, and Stevens (1957) yields the numerical values:
$$
a = 26.81, \quad b = 1960\ \text{Hz}, \quad c = -0.53
$$

Hence the final formula:
$$
\boxed{\; z = \frac{26.81}{1 + 1960 \big/ f} - 0.53 \;}
$$

**Evaluation at $f = 500\ \text{Hz}$:**

$$
z = \frac{26.81}{1 + 1960 \big/ 500} - 0.53
$$

$$
z = \frac{26.81}{1 + 3.92} - 0.53
$$

$$
z = \frac{26.81}{4.92} - 0.53
$$

$$
z = 5.449 - 0.53 = 4.92\ \text{Bark}
$$

The accepted experimental value at $500\ \text{Hz}$ is approximately $4.9$–$5.0\ \text{Bark}$, confirming the formula.

**Evaluation at $f = 4\ \text{kHz}$:**

$$
z = \frac{26.81}{1 + 1960 \big/ 4000} - 0.53
$$

$$
z = \frac{26.81}{1 + 0.49} - 0.53
$$

$$
z = \frac{26.81}{1.49} - 0.53
$$

$$
z = 17.993 - 0.53 = 17.46\ \text{Bark}
$$

The accepted experimental value at $4\ \text{kHz}$ is approximately $17.4$–$17.5\ \text{Bark}$, confirming the formula.

**Role in audio coding (MP3, AAC, Ogg Vorbis):**
Modern perceptual audio codecs divide the audio spectrum into approximately 24–32 sub-bands or, more sophisticatedly, into a non-linear frequency-warped critical-band grid (the Bark scale or the equivalent ERB scale). In each band, the encoder applies a **psychoacoustic masking model**:

- A **masker tone** at one frequency raises the hearing threshold in its neighbourhood.
- Quantization noise and weaker spectral components lying **below the masked threshold** are inaudible and can be discarded (coarsely quantized) without perceptual loss.
- This allows the encoder to allocate **fewer bits** to perceptually less important bands, achieving strong compression (typically 10:1 to 12:1 for MP3 at 128 kbps) while preserving subjective fidelity.

Thus, the **Barkhausen–Zwicker scale** is the bridge between Barkhausen's psychoacoustic insight and the modern **perceptual data-compression** of audio signals — a direct link to your PECST524 course objectives.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Magnitude vs Phase confusion:** Students often write $\vert A\beta \vert = 0$ or "loop gain is 0" — this is a fatal error. The condition is $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$, not zero. (Costs 2–3 marks.)
> 2. **Forgetting the "simultaneous" requirement:** Both magnitude AND phase conditions must be satisfied **at the same frequency $\omega_0$**. Stating them independently without the qualifier "at the oscillation frequency" loses marks.
> 3. **Barkhausen effect vs. criterion mixing:** The Barkhausen **effect** (magnetization jumps) and the Barkhausen **criterion** (oscillator condition) are **two different contributions** by the same scientist. Examiners explicitly test whether you know the distinction. (Up to 4 marks lost if confused.)
> 4. **Bark scale units:** Always quote the unit as **Bark** (capital B, lowercase ark), not "barks" or "bark". (1 mark deduction.)
> 5. **Phase sign convention:** Some texts write the phase condition as $180^\circ$ (inverting amplifier) and others as $360^\circ$ (after accounting for inversion). State both perspectives to gain full credit.
> 6. **Barkhausen noise vs. magnetic noise:** Do not confuse Barkhausen noise (discrete wall jumps) with generic magnetic noise (thermal, shot, flicker). Examiners test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Heinrich Georg Barkhausen**

- **Lifespan:** 1881–1956; German physicist; Professor at TH Dresden.
- **Year of discovery (effect):** **1919** — discrete jumps in magnetization of iron.
- **Year of discovery (criterion):** **1921** — sustained oscillation condition.
- **Barkhausen Effect core idea:** Magnetization of a ferromagnet changes via **discrete, irreversible jumps** of domain walls past pinning sites.
- **Barkhausen Noise:** The audible acoustic emission accompanying each jump, detected via a pickup coil.
- **Barkhausen Criterion (oscillation):** Two simultaneous conditions:
  - $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ (magnitude)
  - $\angle A\beta = 2n\pi$ (phase)
- **Barkhausen Criterion equation:** $1 + A(j\omega_0)\beta(j\omega_0) = 0$.
- **Bark Scale (Barkhausen–Zwicker):** Psychoacoustic unit; $1\ \text{Bark}$ = one critical band of human hearing; mapping formula $z = \dfrac{26.81}{1 + 1960/f} - 0.53$.
- **Range of Bark scale:** $\approx 0$ to $24\ \text{Bark}$ across $0\ \text{Hz}$–$16\ \text{kHz}$.
- **Typical applications:**
  - Sinusoidal oscillator design (Wien bridge, RC phase shift, Colpitts, Hartley, Crystal).
  - Non-destructive evaluation (residual stress, hardness, grain size) via Barkhausen Noise Analysis (BNA).
  - Perceptual audio coding (MP3, AAC, Ogg Vorbis) using the Bark scale.
  - Spintronic and magnetic-sensor research.
- **Key constant:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$ (used in domain-wall energy calculations).
- **Pioneer status:** Barkhausen is **one of the very few scientists** to have **two unrelated fundamental contributions** (magnetism and oscillator theory) and a **named psychoacoustic unit** — a remarkable intellectual legacy.
- **Connection to PECST524 (Data Compression):** The **Bark scale** is the psychoacoustic foundation of audio compression codecs — a direct bridge between Barkhausen's work and modern lossy compression.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
