<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Prediction (LP)** is a mathematical operation in which the current sample (or symbol) of a discrete-time signal (or data stream) is estimated as a **linear combination** of a finite number of immediately preceding samples. The difference between the actual sample and its predicted estimate is called the **prediction error** (or **residual**), which is the quantity that is actually encoded and stored/transmitted in a compression system.

Mathematically, for a discrete signal $x_n$, an **$m$-th order linear predictor** produces an estimate:

$$\hat{x}_n = \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i \, x_{n-i}$$

where:
- $\hat{x}_n$ = predicted value of the $n$-th sample
- $a_i$ = **predictor coefficients** (weights)
- $m$ = **order** of the predictor
- $x_{n-i}$ = previous (past) samples

The **prediction error** is:

$$e_n = x_n - \hat{x}_n = x_n - \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i \, x_{n-i}$$

The transmitted/encoded quantity in the compression system is $e_n$, not $x_n$, because $e_n$ typically has a much smaller variance and a more compact, peaked probability distribution (often Laplacian) — making it highly amenable to entropy coding.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — The Weather Forecaster:**
> Imagine a meteorologist trying to forecast tomorrow's maximum temperature ($\hat{x}_n$). She doesn't guess randomly — she glances at the temperatures of the last few days ($x_{n-1}, x_{n-2}, \dots$) and assumes tomorrow will resemble a *weighted average* of the recent past. If yesterday was 30 °C and the day before was 28 °C, tomorrow is *likely* near 29 °C, not 5 °C. The "surprise" in the actual tomorrow's temperature ($e_n = x_n - \hat{x}_n$) is small and easy to communicate. That tiny surprise is what gets stored — exactly the philosophy of linear prediction in compression.

In data compression, neighbouring samples of audio, image, or text are usually **strongly correlated** (they "look like" each other). A linear predictor exploits this correlation. The better the prediction, the smaller the residual, the fewer the bits required.

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Order ($m$):** Number of past samples used. Higher order = more flexibility but more coefficients to transmit.
> - **Predictor Coefficients ($a_i$):** The weights; finding their optimal values is the central problem of LP.
> - **Residual / Prediction Error ($e_n$):** The unpredicted "new information."
> - **Prediction Gain ($G_p$):** Ratio of original signal variance to residual variance. Measured in **decibels (dB)**.
> - **Mean Squared Error (MSE):** Performance metric minimized during predictor design.
> - **Autocorrelation Function $R(k)$:** Statistical measure of how sample $x_n$ correlates with sample $x_{n+k}$.
> - **Stationarity Assumption:** Linear prediction theory assumes the signal is **wide-sense stationary** (its statistical properties do not change over time).

A *typical* prediction gain in speech signals using a 10th-order predictor is around **10–14 dB**, while for images using 2D predictors (e.g., JPEG-LS) it is around **8–12 dB**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The design and operation of a linear prediction system for compression proceeds in five stages:

1. **Choose the predictor order $m$.**  
   Trade-off: a higher $m$ captures more complex correlations but requires more side-information (the coefficients) to be transmitted to the decoder.

2. **Estimate the autocorrelation function $R(k)$ of the source.**  
   For a stationary source of $N$ samples, the biased autocorrelation estimate is:  
   $$R(k) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1-k} x_n \, x_{n+k}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, m$$

3. **Derive the optimal predictor coefficients by minimizing the MSE.**  
   Differentiating the mean squared error with respect to each $a_i$ and setting it to zero produces a system of linear equations — the **Yule–Walker (or normal) equations**.

4. **Compute the prediction error (residual) sequence.**  
   The encoder subtracts the predicted value from the actual sample to produce $e_n$, which is then entropy-coded (e.g., Huffman, arithmetic).

5. **Transmit the residual $+$ the coefficients $a_i$ (as side information).**  
   At the decoder, the coefficients and the residuals are used to reconstruct the original signal: $x_n = e_n + \sum a_i x_{n-i}$.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why minimize MSE?** The Gaussian distribution is the *maximum-entropy* distribution for a given variance, so minimizing residual variance directly minimizes the bit-rate under entropy coding.
- **Why is autocorrelation needed?** The statistical dependence between $x_n$ and $x_{n-k}$ is quantified by $R(k)$, which determines the optimal weights.
- **Why transmit coefficients?** The decoder must know the *same* predictor to reconstruct the signal.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation you must memorize for KTU ESE questions on Linear Prediction.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Linear predictor (order $m$) | $\hat{x}_n = \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i \, x_{n-i}$ | Time-domain estimate |
| 2 | Prediction error (residual) | $e_n = x_n - \hat{x}_n$ | What is actually encoded |
| 3 | Mean squared prediction error | $E[e_n^2] = R(0) - 2 \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i R(i) + \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} a_i a_j R(i-j)$ | Optimization objective |
| 4 | Yule–Walker normal equations | $\sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i R(i-j) = R(j), \quad j = 1, \dots, m$ | Linear system for $\{a_i\}$ |
| 5 | Matrix form of Yule–Walker | $\mathbf{R} \, \mathbf{a} = \mathbf{r}$ | $\mathbf{R}$ = Toeplitz autocorrelation matrix, $\mathbf{a} = [a_1,\dots,a_m]^T$, $\mathbf{r} = [R(1),\dots,R(m)]^T$ |
| 6 | Minimum MSE after prediction | $E_{\min} = R(0) - \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i R(i)$ | Achieved variance of residual |
| 7 | Prediction gain (linear) | $G_p = \dfrac{R(0)}{E_{\min}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Prediction gain (dB) | $G_p^{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{R(0)}{E_{\min}} \right)$ | Expressed in **decibels (dB)** |
| 9 | Autocorrelation estimate | $R(k) = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-k} x_n \, x_{n+k}$ | Biased estimate |
| 10 | Levinson–Durbin recursion (order $m$) | $k_m = \dfrac{R(m) - \sum_{i=1}^{m-1} a_i^{(m-1)} R(m-i)}{E_{m-1}}$ | Reflection coefficient $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ ensures stability |
| 11 | Coefficient update | $a_m^{(m)} = k_m$, $\; a_i^{(m)} = a_i^{(m-1)} - k_m \, a_{m-i}^{(m-1)}$ | Standard recursion |
| 12 | Forward error at order $m$ | $E_m = (1 - k_m^2)\, E_{m-1}$ | Monotonically decreasing |

> **Note on notation:** Absolute values such as $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ are typeset with `\vert` (not `|`) to keep markdown tables intact.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Linear prediction is the cornerstone of several major compression systems:

- **Speech coding:** CELP, LPC-10, MELP — used in GSM, VoIP, Bluetooth headsets.
- **Lossless audio:** FLAC, Apple Lossless (ALAC) use **fixed** linear predictors.
- **Lossless image compression:** JPEG-LS uses a 2D *Median Edge Detector* predictor (a non-linear variant of LP).
- **Predictive coding in video:** H.264/AVC, H.265/HEVC, AV1 all use **intra-prediction** modes that are essentially directional linear predictors.
- **Time-series forecasting, biomedical signal processing, seismic data compression.**

The elegance of LP is that it converts a *correlated* source into an *almost uncorrelated* residual, which is the fundamental purpose of any decorrelation-based compressor.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Yule–Walker Equations (Optimal Predictor)

### Step 1 — Define the mean squared prediction error

We want to find coefficients $a_1, a_2, \dots, a_m$ that minimize the expected squared error:

$$E[e_n^2] = E\!\left[\left(x_n - \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i x_{n-i}\right)^{\!2}\right]$$

Expanding the square (using linearity of expectation and assuming $x_n$ is a zero-mean wide-sense stationary process):

$$E[e_n^2] = E[x_n^2] - 2\sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i E[x_n x_{n-i}] + \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} a_i a_j E[x_{n-i} x_{n-j}]$$

### Step 2 — Introduce the autocorrelation function

Define the autocorrelation as $R(k) = E[x_n \, x_{n+k}]$, which depends only on the lag $k$ under stationarity. The expression becomes:

$$E[e_n^2] = R(0) - 2 \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i R(i) + \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} a_i a_j R(i-j)$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to each $a_j$ and equate to zero

Take the partial derivative of $E[e_n^2]$ with respect to $a_j$ (for $j = 1, 2, \dots, m$):

$$\frac{\partial E[e_n^2]}{\partial a_j} = -2 R(j) + 2 \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i R(i-j) = 0$$

### Step 4 — Simplify to obtain the normal equations

Rearranging and dividing by 2:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i R(i-j) = R(j), \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

These are the celebrated **Yule–Walker equations** — a system of $m$ linear equations in $m$ unknowns $\{a_1, \dots, a_m\}$.

### Step 5 — Write in compact matrix form

The autocorrelation matrix is **Toeplitz** (constant along each diagonal):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
R(0) & R(1) & R(2) & \cdots & R(m-1) \\
R(1) & R(0) & R(1) & \cdots & R(m-2) \\
R(2) & R(1) & R(0) & \cdots & R(m-3) \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
R(m-1) & R(m-2) & R(m-3) & \cdots & R(0)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3 \\ \vdots \\ a_m
\end{bmatrix}
&=
\begin{bmatrix}
R(1) \\ R(2) \\ R(3) \\ \vdots \\ R(m)
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{r}$ directly is $O(m^3)$, but the **Levinson–Durbin recursion** exploits the Toeplitz structure to solve in $O(m^2)$.

### Step 6 — Compute the minimum MSE

Substituting the optimal coefficients back into the MSE expression gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\min} &= R(0) - \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i R(i) \\
E_{\min} &= R(0) - \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{r}
\end{aligned}
$$

The prediction gain in dB is then:

$$G_p^{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{R(0)}{E_{\min}} \right)$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A signal has autocorrelation values $R(0) = 10$, $R(1) = 8$, $R(2) = 5$. Find the optimal 2nd-order linear predictor coefficients and the prediction gain.

### Solution

**Step A — Form the Yule–Walker system** for $m=2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(0)\, a_1 + R(1)\, a_2 &= R(1) \\
R(1)\, a_1 + R(0)\, a_2 &= R(2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting numbers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
10 a_1 + 8 a_2 &= 8 \\
8 a_1 + 10 a_2 &= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Solve by Cramer's rule.** Determinant $\Delta = 10 \cdot 10 - 8 \cdot 8 = 100 - 64 = 36$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 &= \frac{\begin{vmatrix}8 & 8 \\ 5 & 10\end{vmatrix}}{\Delta} = \frac{80 - 40}{36} = \frac{40}{36} = \frac{10}{9} \\
a_2 &= \frac{\begin{vmatrix}10 & 8 \\ 8 & 5\end{vmatrix}}{\Delta} = \frac{50 - 64}{36} = \frac{-14}{36} = -\frac{7}{18}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Compute minimum MSE:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\min} &= R(0) - a_1 R(1) - a_2 R(2) \\
E_{\min} &= 10 - \tfrac{10}{9} \cdot 8 - \left(-\tfrac{7}{18}\right) \cdot 5 \\
E_{\min} &= 10 - \tfrac{80}{9} + \tfrac{35}{18} \\
E_{\min} &= \tfrac{180 - 160 + 35}{18} = \tfrac{55}{18} \approx 3.056
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Prediction gain:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_p^{\text{dB}} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10}{55/18}\right) \\
G_p^{\text{dB}} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{180}{55}\right) \\
G_p^{\text{dB}} &= 10 \log_{10}(3.2727) \approx 5.15 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A 2nd-order linear predictor reduces the signal's dynamic range by **5.15 dB** for this correlation structure, which corresponds to roughly **1.7 bits per sample** of entropy reduction.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Predictor Order

| Order $m$ | Complexity | Coefficient side-info (bits) | Typical gain (audio) | When to use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Trivial | 0 | 0 dB | No prediction; raw transmission |
| 1 | $O(1)$ | $\approx 16$ | 2–4 dB | Slowly varying signals |
| 2–3 | $O(m^2)$ | $\approx 48$ | 4–8 dB | Low-correlation images (JPEG-LS fallback) |
| 4–8 | $O(m^2)$ | $\approx 128$ | 8–12 dB | Speech signals (CELP) |
| 10–16 | $O(m^2)$ | $\approx 256$ | 10–16 dB | High-fidelity audio (FLAC, Opus) |
| >16 | $O(m^2)$ | >256 | Diminishing returns | Rarely justified; numerical instability |

> [!NOTE]
> A fundamental stability result (inherited from Levinson–Durbin): the predictor is **guaranteed stable** (i.e., its $z$-transform has all poles inside the unit circle) if and only if every reflection coefficient satisfies $\vert k_i \vert < 1$. The Levinson–Durbin recursion enforces this automatically, whereas direct matrix inversion does not.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Linear Predictive Coding (LPC) Compression System

<!-- Mermaid block — strict alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown inside labels -->
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputX["Input Signal x of n"] --> delay1["Unit Delay z^-1"]
    delay1 --> delay2["Unit Delay z^-1"]
    delay2 --> delay3["Unit Delay z^-1"]
    delayM["..."] --> delayN["Unit Delay z^-1"]

    delay1 --> coef1["Coefficient a1"]
    delay2 --> coef2["Coefficient a2"]
    delay3 --> coef3["Coefficient a3"]
    delayN --> coefM["Coefficient am"]

    coef1 --> summer["Summing Junction"]
    coef2 --> summer
    coef3 --> summer
    coefM --> summer
    summer --> predicted["Predicted x hat of n"]
    predicted --> subtractor["Subtractor Minus"]
    inputX --> subtractor
    subtractor --> error["Prediction Error e of n"]
    error --> entenc["Entropy Encoder Huffman or Arithmetic"]
    entenc --> channel["Channel or Storage"]

    coef1 --> sideinfo["Side Info Coefficients"]
    coef2 --> sideinfo
    coef3 --> sideinfo
    coefM --> sideinfo
    sideinfo --> channel

    channel --> entdec["Entropy Decoder"]
    entdec --> errorRx["Recovered e of n"]
    errorRx --> adder["Adder Plus"]
    sideinfo --> reconstruct["Coefficient Recovery"]
    reconstruct --> mul1["Multiply by a1"]
    reconstruct --> mul2["Multiply by a2"]
    reconstruct --> mul3["Multiply by a3"]
    reconstruct --> mulN["Multiply by am"]

    delayR1["Delay z^-1"] --> mul1
    delayR2["Delay z^-1"] --> mul2
    delayR3["Delay z^-1"] --> mul3
    delayRN["Delay z^-1"] --> mulN

    mul1 --> adder
    mul2 --> adder
    mul3 --> adder
    mulN --> adder
    adder --> outputXr["Reconstructed x of n"]
    outputXr --> delayR1
    delayR1 --> delayR2
    delayR2 --> delayR3
    delayR3 --> delayRN
```

> **Reading guide:** The upper half is the **encoder** (predicts, subtracts, encodes error). The lower half is the **decoder** (decodes error, applies the *same* predictor, and adds the residual back). The "side information" (the $a_i$ coefficients) is sent once per signal block.

## 4.2 Logical / Functional Flow of Predictor Coefficient Estimation

<!-- Mermaid block — block-level functional architecture, no physical drawing attempted -->
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawX["Raw Source Samples x1 x2 ... xN"] --> seg["Partition into Blocks of N samples"]
    seg --> ac["Compute Autocorrelation R of k for k = 0 to m"]
    ac --> yw["Form Toeplitz Matrix R and Vector r"]
    yw --> ld["Apply Levinson Durbin Recursion O of m squared"]
    ld --> coeff["Optimal Coefficients a1 to am"]
    coeff --> store["Store as Side Information"]

    rawX --> pred["Run Predictor over each sample"]
    pred --> res["Compute Residual e of n = x of n minus predicted"]
    res --> ent["Entropy Encode Residual"]
    ent --> tx["Transmit or Store Compressed Bitstream"]

    store --> tx
    coeff --> pred
```

## 4.3 Feedback Loop Inside the Decoder (Markov-Style Reconstruction)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    errIn["Decoded Residual e of n"] --> sumA["Adder"]
    a1mul["a1 times x of n minus 1"] --> sumA
    a2mul["a2 times x of n minus 2"] --> sumA
    aMmul["am times x of n minus m"] --> sumA
    sumA --> xout["Reconstructed x of n"]
    xout --> d1["Delay 1"]
    xout --> d2["Delay 2"]
    xout --> dM["Delay m"]
    d1 --> a1mul
    d2 --> a2mul
    dM --> aMmul
```

> **Key insight:** The decoder uses *its own reconstructed* past samples (not the originals) to predict the next sample. This is why the predictor **must be causal and stable** — any error in the residual compounds forward through the feedback loop.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**  
> *Define linear prediction in the context of data compression. What is meant by the "prediction error" and why is it the quantity that is encoded rather than the original signal sample?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Linear prediction is a technique in which the current sample of a signal is estimated as a **linear weighted sum** of a finite number of past samples. Mathematically,

$$\hat{x}_n = \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i x_{n-i}$$

The *prediction error* (or *residual*) is the difference between the actual and the predicted sample: $e_n = x_n - \hat{x}_n$.

The residual is encoded because, for highly correlated signals, $e_n$ has **much smaller variance** and a more **peaked (typically Laplacian) distribution** than $x_n$. By Shannon's source-coding theorem, a lower-variance signal can be encoded with fewer bits, yielding better compression. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for error formula, 1 mark for compression rationale.)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**  
> *State and explain the Yule–Walker equations. What role does the autocorrelation function play in determining the optimal predictor coefficients?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Yule–Walker equations** are the set of $m$ linear equations obtained by minimizing the mean squared prediction error $E[e_n^2]$ with respect to the predictor coefficients $a_1, \dots, a_m$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i R(i-j) = R(j), \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

where $R(k) = E[x_n x_{n+k}]$ is the **autocorrelation function** of the stationary source. The autocorrelation function quantifies the *statistical dependence* between samples separated by lag $k$. Higher $R(k)$ values indicate stronger correlation, which the predictor must exploit. Solving the Yule–Walker system yields the **optimal MSE coefficients**. **(1 mark for statement, 1 mark for derivation insight, 1 mark for role of autocorrelation.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> **Examiner's Note (KTU 2024):** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **7 + 7** sub-parts. You are given an internal choice (Or option). Both alternatives below are fully solved for your reference.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**  
**(a)** Derive the Yule–Walker equations for an $m$-th order linear predictor. Express them in matrix form. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** For a 2nd-order predictor with autocorrelation values $R(0) = 5$, $R(1) = 4$, $R(2) = 2$, find the optimal coefficients and the prediction gain in dB. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

- Start with predictor $\hat{x}_n = \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i x_{n-i}$ and error $e_n = x_n - \hat{x}_n$. **(1 mark)**
- Define MSE: $E[e_n^2] = E\!\left[\left(x_n - \sum a_i x_{n-i}\right)^2\right]$. Expand and substitute $R(k) = E[x_n x_{n+k}]$. **(2 marks)**
- Differentiate w.r.t. $a_j$ and equate to zero: $\partial E/\partial a_j = -2R(j) + 2\sum a_i R(i-j) = 0$. **(2 marks)**
- Obtain $\sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i R(i-j) = R(j)$ for $j = 1, \dots, m$. Write matrix form $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{r}$. **(2 marks)**

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

System of equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
5 a_1 + 4 a_2 &= 4 \\
4 a_1 + 5 a_2 &= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Determinant: $\Delta = 25 - 16 = 9$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 &= \frac{4 \cdot 5 - 4 \cdot 2}{9} = \frac{12}{9} = \frac{4}{3} \\
a_2 &= \frac{5 \cdot 2 - 4 \cdot 4}{9} = \frac{-6}{9} = -\frac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Minimum MSE:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\min} &= R(0) - a_1 R(1) - a_2 R(2) \\
E_{\min} &= 5 - \tfrac{4}{3} \cdot 4 - \left(-\tfrac{2}{3}\right) \cdot 2 \\
E_{\min} &= 5 - \tfrac{16}{3} + \tfrac{4}{3} = \tfrac{15 - 16 + 4}{3} = \tfrac{3}{3} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Prediction gain in dB:

$$
G_p^{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{5}{1}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(5) \approx 6.99 \text{ dB}
$$

**Final answer:** $a_1 = 4/3$, $a_2 = -2/3$, $G_p \approx 6.99$ dB. **(3 marks for solving system, 2 marks for MSE, 2 marks for gain.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**  
**(a)** Explain the Levinson–Durbin algorithm. Why is it preferred over direct matrix inversion for solving the Yule–Walker equations? **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** A signal has autocorrelation $R(0) = 1$, $R(1) = 0.8$, $R(2) = 0.5$. Compute the reflection coefficients $k_1$ and $k_2$ and the prediction error powers $E_0$, $E_1$, $E_2$ using the Levinson–Durbin recursion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Explanation (7 marks):**

The Levinson–Durbin algorithm solves the Yule–Walker equations *recursively* in $O(m^2)$ time, building the order-$p$ solution from the order-$(p-1)$ solution. **(1 mark)**

- Initialize: $E_0 = R(0)$. **(1 mark)**
- For $p = 1, 2, \dots, m$:  
  (i) compute reflection coefficient: $k_p = \dfrac{R(p) - \sum_{i=1}^{p-1} a_i^{(p-1)} R(p-i)}{E_{p-1}}$. **(1 mark)**  
  (ii) update coefficients: $a_p^{(p)} = k_p$, and $a_i^{(p)} = a_i^{(p-1)} - k_p a_{p-i}^{(p-1)}$ for $i < p$. **(1 mark)**  
  (iii) update error: $E_p = (1 - k_p^2) E_{p-1}$. **(1 mark)**

Advantages over direct inversion: (1) $O(m^2)$ complexity instead of $O(m^3)$, (2) exploits the Toeplitz structure of $\mathbf{R}$, (3) yields intermediate reflection coefficients that **guarantee stability** of the resulting predictor. **(2 marks)**

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Order 0:** $E_0 = R(0) = 1$.

**Order 1:**

$$
k_1 = \frac{R(1)}{E_0} = \frac{0.8}{1} = 0.8
$$

$$
a_1^{(1)} = k_1 = 0.8
$$

$$
E_1 = (1 - k_1^2) E_0 = (1 - 0.64)(1) = 0.36
$$

**Order 2:**

$$
k_2 = \frac{R(2) - a_1^{(1)} R(1)}{E_1} = \frac{0.5 - 0.8 \cdot 0.8}{0.36} = \frac{0.5 - 0.64}{0.36} = \frac{-0.14}{0.36} \approx -0.389
$$

$$
a_2^{(2)} = k_2 \approx -0.389
$$

$$
a_1^{(2)} = a_1^{(1)} - k_2 a_1^{(1)} = 0.8 - (-0.389)(0.8) = 0.8 + 0.311 = 1.111
$$

$$
E_2 = (1 - k_2^2) E_1 = (1 - 0.151) (0.36) \approx 0.305
$$

**Summary:** $k_1 = 0.8$, $k_2 \approx -0.389$, $E_0 = 1$, $E_1 = 0.36$, $E_2 \approx 0.305$. **(2 marks each for $k_1$ and $E_1$; 3 marks for $k_2$, coefficient update, and $E_2$.)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in $e_n$:** Many students write $e_n = \hat{x}_n - x_n$ — the correct order is **actual minus predicted**. A sign flip corrupts both the encoder and decoder outputs and costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to send the coefficients:** In exam answers on LPC systems, you must explicitly state that the $\{a_i\}$ are sent as **side information** to the decoder. Omission costs **1 mark** on system-design questions.
> 3. **Confusing $R(0)$ and MSE:** $R(0)$ is the *signal* variance (input), $E_{\min}$ is the *residual* variance (output). Prediction gain is the **ratio** of these — not the difference. A common error: writing $G_p = R(0) - E_{\min}$ instead of $G_p = R(0) / E_{\min}$. Costs up to **2 marks**.
> 4. **In Levinson–Durbin, students often forget to update the *previous* coefficients** when moving from order $(p-1)$ to $p$. The recursion is $a_i^{(p)} = a_i^{(p-1)} - k_p a_{p-i}^{(p-1)}$, *not* $a_i^{(p)} = a_i^{(p-1)}$.
> 5. **Units of prediction gain:** When the problem says "in dB," multiply $10 \log_{10}(\cdot)$ for power ratios. Forgetting the factor of **10** vs. 20 (used for amplitude ratios) is a frequent error.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Prediction — Things You Must Remember Before Walking into the ESE Hall:**

- [ ] **Definition:** $\hat{x}_n = \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i x_{n-i}$; **Residual:** $e_n = x_n - \hat{x}_n$. (Always *actual minus predicted*.)
- [ ] **Goal:** Minimize $E[e_n^2]$ ⇒ fewer bits to encode ⇒ better compression.
- [ ] **Yule–Walker equations:** $\sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i R(i-j) = R(j)$ for $j = 1, \dots, m$ — solve these to get the *optimal* $a_i$.
- [ ] **Matrix form:** $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{r}$; the matrix $\mathbf{R}$ is **Toeplitz** (constant diagonals).
- [ ] **Minimum MSE:** $E_{\min} = R(0) - \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i R(i)$.
- [ ] **Prediction gain (dB):** $G_p^{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{R(0)}{E_{\min}} \right)$.
- [ ] **Levinson–Durbin recursion:** $O(m^2)$, gives reflection coefficients $k_p$ with $\vert k_p \vert < 1$ for stability.
- [ ] **Error recursion:** $E_p = (1 - k_p^2) E_{p-1}$ ⇒ error power decreases monotonically with order.
- [ ] **Stability criterion:** $\vert k_p \vert < 1$ for all $p$ (a sufficient and necessary condition for the all-pole predictor to be stable).
- [ ] **Causality:** The predictor uses *only past* samples — never future ones — so it is causal and realizable in real-time systems.
- [ ] **Side information:** Predictor coefficients must be transmitted alongside the residual; their cost must be amortized over the block length.
- [ ] **Applications:** Speech coding (CELP, LPC-10), lossless audio (FLAC), lossless image (JPEG-LS), video intra-prediction.
- [ ] **Trade-off:** Higher $m$ ⇒ more gain but more side-info and risk of overfitting on short blocks. Typical $m \in [4, 16]$ for most practical compressors.
- [ ] **Stationarity:** The Yule–Walker theory assumes a *wide-sense stationary* source. Non-stationary data (speech bursts, edges in images) requires **block-adaptive** or **adaptive** linear prediction (e.g., ADPCM).
- [ ] **LP vs. transform coding:** LP operates in the *time/spatial* domain on neighbouring samples, whereas transform coding (DCT, DWT) operates in the *frequency* domain. Modern hybrid coders combine both.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: µ-Law and A-Law Companding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST524 - DATA COMPRESSION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** µ-Law and A-Law Companding # Module 4: µ-Law and A-Law Companding ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Companding** is the combined process of **COM**pression and ex**PANDING** applied to a signal to improve the Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) over a wide dynamic range. In Pulse Code Modulation (PCM), the analog signal is first **compressed** (non-uniformly quantized) before encoding, and the received digital signal is **expanded** (inverse operation) at the receiver to reconstruct the original signal. **µ-Law Companding** and **A-Law Companding** are the two internationally standardized **logarithmic companding techniques** used in 8-bit PCM telephony systems (ITU-T G.711 standard). They provide non-uniform quantization by assigning smaller step sizes to low-amplitude signals and larger step sizes to high-amplitude signals, thereby improving the perceptual quality of voice signals. - **µ-Law** (mu-Law): Standardized in **North America** and **Japan** (ITU-T G.711). - **A-Law**: Standardized in **Europe**, **India**, and most of the rest of the world (ITU-T G.711). ### 1.2 The Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **The Whisper-to-Shout Translator (The Compander Analogy):** > > Imagine you are recording a conversation in a room where one person **whispers** softly and another **shouts** loudly. A normal linear microphone treats the whisper (small sound) and the shout (large sound) using the same scale — this causes the whisper to be lost in quantization noise. The **compander acts like a "smart translator"**: > > 1. **Compression (at the transmitter):** The translator *amplifies the whispers* (small amplitudes are stretched out) and *gently compresses the shouts* (large amplitudes are squeezed closer together). This makes the entire dynamic range fit nicely into a fixed number of levels. > 2. **Expansion (at the receiver):** A *reverse translator* exactly restores the original whisper and shout relationship so the listener hears the conversation naturally. > > The "translator" uses a **logarithmic curve** because human hearing is naturally logarithmic (Weber's Law). The compander mimics the ear's own response, making the quantization noise perceptually uniform across all amplitudes. ### 1.3 Need for Companding in PCM > [!NOTE] > **Why Companding is Essential:** > - Voice signals exhibit a high dynamic range (~40 dB) and non-uniform amplitude distribution (most samples are low amplitude). > - In **uniform quantization**, the **SQNR is poor for low-amplitude signals** because the quantization step Δ is fixed. > - Companding provides **finer quantization at low amplitudes** (where most speech energy lies) and **coarser quantization at high amplitudes**, improving the **overall perceptual SQNR**. ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > - **Standard µ (mu) value:** **µ = 255** (ITU-T G.711 standard for North America/Japan) > - **Standard A value:** **A = 87.6** (ITU-T G.711 standard for Europe/India) > - **Number of bits per sample:** **n = 8 bits** > - **Total number of levels:** **L = 2ⁿ = 256** > - **Number of segments:** **8 (4 positive + 4 negative)** > - **Levels per segment:** **16** > - **Normalized input range:** **−1 ≤ x ≤ +1** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The µ-Law Compression Equation The µ-Law compression characteristic is given by the **logarithmic compression law**: $$ F_{\mu}(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \frac{\ln\left(1 + \mu \vert x \vert\right)}{\ln(1 + \mu)} $$ where the variables are defined as: - **x** = Normalized input sample, with −1 ≤ x ≤ +1 - **µ** = Compression parameter (typically **255**) - **sgn(x)** = Signum function: returns +1 for x ≥ 0 and −1 for x < 0 - **F_µ(x)** = Compressed output, with −1 ≤ F_µ(x) ≤ +1 ### Operational Logic Steps 1. **Take the absolute value** of the input sample: this removes the sign so the logarithm can be applied to a non-negative number. 2. **Multiply by µ = 255**: this scales the input so that even small amplitudes produce a meaningful log response. 3. **Add 1** inside the logarithm: this prevents the logarithm of zero when x = 0. 4. **Divide by ln(1 + µ)**: this normalizes the output so that the maximum input |x| = 1 maps to the maximum output |F_µ| = 1. 5. **Reapply the original sign** using sgn(x) so the compression remains symmetric for positive and negative signals. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** µ-Law Companding is used in all North American digital telephone systems (T1 lines, VoIP endpoints, digital switching offices). Every Skype/VoIP call originating in the US applies µ-Law at 8 kHz sampling with 8-bit encoding to produce the standard **64 kbps** digital voice bitstream. ## 2.2 The A-Law Compression Equation The A-Law compression characteristic is a **piecewise-defined logarithmic curve** with a linear segment near zero: $$ F_A(x) = \begin{cases} \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A}, & 0 \le \vert x \vert \le \dfrac{1}{A} \\[10pt] \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{1 + \ln\left(A \vert x \vert\right)}{1 + \ln A}, & \dfrac{1}{A} \le \vert x \vert \le 1 \end{cases} $$ where the variables are defined as: - **A** = Compression parameter (typically **87.6**) - **x** = Normalized input, −1 ≤ x ≤ +1 - **F_A(x)** = Compressed output, −1 ≤ F_A(x) ≤ +1 ### Operational Logic Steps 1. **Check the amplitude region** — determine whether |x| lies in the lower linear region (|x| ≤ 1/A) or the upper logarithmic region (1/A ≤ |x| ≤ 1). 2. **For small signals (|x| ≤ 1/A ≈ 0.0114):** Apply the **linear segment** so that very small signals are scaled uniformly and quantization noise for whispers is kept low. 3. **For large signals (|x| ≥ 1/A):** Apply the **logarithmic segment** so that the dynamic range of loud sounds is compressed. 4. **Divide by (1 + ln A)** to normalize the maximum output to 1. 5. **Reapply the sign** with sgn(x) for symmetric handling of positive and negative samples. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** A-Law is used in European and Indian telecommunication networks (E1 trunks at 2.048 Mbps carrying 30 voice channels). It is the dominant standard in GSM cellular base stations and most VoIP international gateways. ## 2.3 Comparison: µ-Law vs. A-Law | Feature | µ-Law (mu-Law) | A-Law | |---|---|---| | Compression curve | Continuous logarithmic | Piecewise (linear + log) | | Parameter | µ = **255** | A = **87.6** | | Region Used | North America, Japan | Europe, India, most of world | | Slope at origin | dF/dx (at x=0) = µ/ln(1+µ) ≈ **8.59** (steep) | dF/dx (at x=0) = A/(1+ln A) ≈ **16** (steeper) | | Small-signal behavior | Logarithmic from origin | **Linear** near origin | | Continuity at boundary | Continuous everywhere | Continuous at \|x\| = 1/A | | Output bit rate | 64 kbps @ 8 kHz, 8 bits | 64 kbps @ 8 kHz, 8 bits | | Standard | ITU-T G.711 (µ-Law variant) | ITU-T G.711 (A-Law variant) | | Tandem encoding penalty | Higher (worse) | Lower (better) | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | µ-Law compression | F_µ(x) = sgn(x) · ln(1 + µ\vert x \vert) / ln(1 + µ) | µ = 255 | | 2 | A-Law compression (linear) | F_A(x) = sgn(x) · A\vert x \vert / (1 + ln A) | valid for 0 ≤ \vert x \vert ≤ 1/A | | 3 | A-Law compression (log) | F_A(x) = sgn(x) · [1 + ln(A\vert x \vert)] / (1 + ln A) | valid for 1/A ≤ \vert x \vert ≤ 1 | | 4 | Number of segments | **8** (4 positive, 4 negative) | Standard for 8-bit PCM | | 5 | Levels per segment | **16** | Linear within each segment | | 6 | Total quantization levels | **256** (= 2⁸) | One sign bit + 7 magnitude bits | | 7 | Segment index to step size | Δ_k = Δ₁ · 2^(k−1) | Doubles each segment | | 8 | First segment step size | Δ₁ = 2 / (L · M) = 2/256 for normalized range | M = number of segments per polarity | | 9 | SQNR improvement | Up to **~24 dB** over uniform PCM | Depends on signal statistics | | 10 | Bit rate (8 kHz, 8 bits) | R_b = n · f_s = **64 kbps** | Standard voice channel rate | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Reality Check:** Both µ-Law and A-Law produce the same output bit rate of **64 kbps** per voice channel. The 13-segment (US) / 15-segment (Europe) approximation is used for **digital implementation** in actual hardware, where 8 segments (4 positive + 4 negative) are uniformly subdivided. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: µ-Law Output at a Given Input **Problem:** Compute the µ-Law compressed output for x = 0.4, given µ = 255. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify sign and magnitude.** The input x = 0.4 is positive, so sgn(x) = +1 and |x| = 0.4. **Step 2 — Substitute into the µ-Law equation.** $$ F_{\mu}(x) = (+1) \cdot \frac{\ln\left(1 + 255 \cdot 0.4\right)}{\ln(1 + 255)} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator argument.** $$ 1 + 255 \cdot 0.4 = 1 + 102 = 103 $$ So the numerator becomes ln(103). **Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator.** $$ \ln(1 + 255) = \ln(256) = \ln(2^8) = 8 \cdot \ln(2) \approx 8 \times 0.6931 = 5.5452 $$ > Conversion logic: Recognizing that 256 = 2⁸ converts a complex log to a simple multiple of ln(2). This is a standard trick used in the derivation of µ-Law to simplify the denominator. **Step 5 — Evaluate the numerator.** $$ \ln(103) \approx 4.6347 $$ **Step 6 — Compute the ratio.** $$ F_{\mu}(0.4) = \frac{4.6347}{5.5452} \approx 0.8357 $$ **Final Answer:** F_µ(0.4) ≈ **+0.8357** Notice that the input 0.4 (only 40% of full scale) is expanded to 0.8357 (83.57% of compressed full scale). This demonstrates the **expansion of small signals** — the essence of companding. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: A-Law Output at a Given Input **Problem:** Compute the A-Law compressed output for x = 0.02, given A = 87.6. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify which piece of the piecewise function applies.** The boundary between the linear and logarithmic regions is at 1/A: $$ \frac{1}{A} = \frac{1}{87.6} \approx 0.0114 $$ Since |x| = 0.02 > 0.0114, the **logarithmic segment** applies. **Step 2 — Substitute into the A-Law (log) equation.** $$ F_A(0.02) = (+1) \cdot \frac{1 + \ln(87.6 \cdot 0.02)}{1 + \ln(87.6)} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the argument of the inner log.** $$ 87.6 \cdot 0.02 = 1.752 $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate ln(1.752).** $$ \ln(1.752) \approx 0.5607 $$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the numerator.** $$ 1 + 0.5607 = 1.5607 $$ **Step 6 — Evaluate the denominator.** $$ 1 + \ln(87.6) = 1 + 4.4726 = 5.4726 $$ > Conversion logic: ln(87.6) is computed using the identity ln(87.6) = ln(8.76 × 10) = ln(8.76) + ln(10) ≈ 2.1701 + 2.3026 = 4.4727. **Step 7 — Compute the ratio.** $$ F_A(0.02) = \frac{1.5607}{5.4726} \approx 0.2852 $$ **Final Answer:** F_A(0.02) ≈ **+0.2852** --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Critical Point Comparison **Problem:** Verify that for the same input amplitude x = 0.01, both µ-Law and A-Law produce outputs close to the linear region of A-Law. ### Solution Table | Input x | µ-Law Output (µ=255) | A-Law Output (A=87.6) | Region for A-Law | |---|---|---|---| | 0.001 | 0.1914 | 0.01460 | Linear (\vert x \vert < 1/A) | | 0.01 | 0.4380 | 0.1460 | Linear (\vert x \vert < 1/A) | | 0.05 | 0.6620 | 0.3889 | Logarithmic | | 0.20 | 0.7893 | 0.7014 | Logarithmic | | 0.50 | 0.8751 | 0.8445 | Logarithmic | | 1.00 | 1.0000 | 1.0000 | End of log range | > Conversion logic: The values show that A-Law is **less aggressive** than µ-Law for very small signals (A-Law uses linear scaling) but **more aggressive** for moderate signals. This is why A-Law has **lower distortion in tandem encoding** (multiple compress-expand cycles). --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Segment Step Size Relationship **Problem:** Show that the step size doubles for each successive segment in the 8-segment companding scheme. ### Step-by-Step Proof **Step 1 — Define the segment step sizes.** For the 15-segment A-Law approximation (Europe, 8 segments per polarity): - Segment 1: step size Δ₁ - Segment 2: step size Δ₂ = 2·Δ₁ - Segment 3: step size Δ₃ = 4·Δ₁ - Segment 4: step size Δ₄ = 8·Δ₁ **Step 2 — Express the total dynamic range.** The total normalized range is from 0 to +1, distributed across 4 positive segments, each with 16 levels: $$ \text{Total range} = 16 \cdot \Delta_1 + 16 \cdot \Delta_2 + 16 \cdot \Delta_3 + 16 \cdot \Delta_4 $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the doubling relationship.** $$ 1 = 16\Delta_1\left(1 + 2 + 4 + 8\right) = 16\Delta_1 \cdot 15 $$ **Step 4 — Solve for Δ₁.** $$ \Delta_1 = \frac{1}{16 \times 15} = \frac{1}{240} \approx 0.00417 $$ > Conversion logic: The geometric series 1 + 2 + 4 + 8 = 15 reflects the doubling pattern. This is the foundation of the **piecewise-uniform companding** used in digital PCM codecs. **Step 5 — Compute all step sizes.** | Segment k | Step Size Δ_k | Numerical Value | |---|---|---| | 1 | 1/240 | 0.00417 | | 2 | 2/240 | 0.00833 | | 3 | 4/240 | 0.01667 | | 4 | 8/240 | 0.03333 | This doubling pattern produces the **characteristic 4:1 ratio** between the largest and smallest step sizes, giving ~24 dB of companding gain. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Companding System Block Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow) The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the end-to-end flow of a companded PCM system, from the analog source through compression, encoding, transmission, decoding, expansion, and final reconstruction. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TX["TRANSMITTER SECTION"] analogSignal["ANALOG
SIGNAL x(t)"] sampler["SAMPLER
fs = 8 kHz"] compressor["COMPRESSOR
u-Law or A-Law
Characteristic"] quantizer["QUANTIZER
256 Levels
8-bit"] encoder["ENCODER
Binary Coder"] end channel["DIGITAL CHANNEL
64 kbps Bitstream"] subgraph RX["RECEIVER SECTION"] decoder["DECODER
Binary Decoder"] expander["EXPANDER
Inverse u-Law or A-Law
Characteristic"] reconstructor["RECONSTRUCTOR
Lowpass Filter"] recoveredSignal["RECOVERED
SIGNAL y(t)"] end analogSignal --> sampler sampler --> compressor compressor --> quantizer quantizer --> encoder encoder --> channel channel --> decoder decoder --> expander expander --> reconstructor reconstructor --> recoveredSignal ``` ## 4.2 µ-Law vs A-Law Compression Curve Comparison The following Mermaid graph shows the conceptual shape of the two compression characteristics, plotting the compressed output F(x) against the normalized input x for x in [0, 1]. ```mermaid flowchart TB inputAxis["INPUT x: 0.0 to 1.0"] uLawCurve["U-LAW CURVE
Continuous Logarithmic
Steep slope at origin
Slope = 8.59 at x=0"] aLawCurve["A-LAW CURVE
Piecewise: Linear + Log
Linear for x less than 0.0114
Logarithmic above"] outputAxis["OUTPUT F(x): 0.0 to 1.0"] inputAxis --> uLawCurve uLawCurve --> outputAxis inputAxis --> aLawCurve aLawCurve --> outputAxis ``` ## 4.3 Companding Process Flow (Transmitter Side Detail) ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Start: Analog Voice Signal"] step1["Step 1: Bandlimit to 4 kHz"] step2["Step 2: Sample at 8 kHz"] step3["Step 3: Normalize to -1 to +1"] step4["Step 4: Apply Compressor F(x)"] step5["Step 5: Uniformly Quantize 256 levels"] step6["Step 6: Encode to 8-bit binary word"] step7["Step 7: Transmit 64 kbps bitstream"] start --> step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6 --> step7 ``` ## 4.4 Companding Process Flow (Receiver Side Detail) ```mermaid flowchart TD rcvStart["Start: Received 64 kbps bitstream"] rcvStep1["Step 1: Decode 8-bit word"] rcvStep2["Step 2: Map to quantized level"] rcvStep3["Step 3: Apply Expander F-inverse(x)"] rcvStep4["Step 4: Denormalize to original scale"] rcvStep5["Step 5: Lowpass filter reconstruction"] rcvEnd["End: Reconstructed Analog Signal y(t)"] rcvStart --> rcvStep1 --> rcvStep2 --> rcvStep3 --> rcvStep4 --> rcvStep5 --> rcvEnd ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define companding. Why is it required in PCM systems?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > Companding is the process of **compression** at the transmitter followed by **expansion** at the receiver of a PCM system. It is required because voice signals have a wide dynamic range and a non-uniform amplitude distribution, with most energy concentrated in low amplitudes. Uniform quantization leads to poor Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) for low-amplitude signals. Companding provides **finer quantization for small signals** and **coarser quantization for large signals**, thereby improving the overall SQNR by approximately **24 dB** and making quantization noise perceptually uniform. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **Distinguish between µ-Law and A-Law companding.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > - **µ-Law** uses a continuous logarithmic compression curve with parameter µ = 255. It is used in **North America and Japan**. > - **A-Law** uses a piecewise characteristic — a **linear segment for small signals** (|x| ≤ 1/A) and a **logarithmic segment for larger signals** — with parameter A = 87.6. It is used in **Europe and India**. > - µ-Law has **higher slope at the origin** (~8.59), providing more expansion for very small signals, but suffers from **higher distortion in tandem encoding**. > - A-Law has a steeper initial slope (~16) and a **linear region near zero**, making it more robust for **international connections involving multiple compress-expand cycles**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a)** Derive the µ-Law compression expression. Compute the compressed output for x = 0.6 with µ = 255. (7 Marks) **(b)** Explain the 8-segment A-Law companding scheme with a neat sketch. Show that the step size doubles in each successive segment. (7 Marks) ### Model Solution to Question A #### Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Derivation of µ-Law Expression (4 Marks):** The µ-Law compression characteristic is defined as: $$ F_{\mu}(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \frac{\ln\left(1 + \mu \vert x \vert\right)}{\ln(1 + \mu)} $$ **Derivation outline:** 1. Start with the requirement that compression must be **symmetric**, **monotonic**, and **pass through (±1, ±1)**. 2. Assume a logarithmic form: y = a · ln(1 + b·x). 3. Apply boundary conditions: - At x = 0: y = 0 → satisfied automatically by the log form. - At x = 1: y = 1 → gives a = 1/ln(1 + b). 4. Substitute parameter µ for b to obtain the standard form. **Step 2 — Numerical Computation for x = 0.6 (3 Marks):** Given µ = 255, x = 0.6, sgn(x) = +1: $$ F_{\mu}(0.6) = \frac{\ln(1 + 255 \cdot 0.6)}{\ln(1 + 255)} = \frac{\ln(154)}{\ln(256)} $$ **Evaluation:** - Numerator: ln(154) ≈ 5.0370 - Denominator: ln(256) = 8 · ln(2) ≈ 5.5452 $$ F_{\mu}(0.6) = \frac{5.0370}{5.5452} \approx 0.9083 $$ **Final Answer:** F_µ(0.6) ≈ **+0.9083** #### Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Explain the 8-Segment Scheme (3 Marks):** In the digital implementation of A-Law, the normalized range [0, 1] is divided into **4 positive segments** (and symmetrically 4 negative segments, giving 8 total). Each segment is divided into **16 uniform quantization levels**. The segment boundaries are chosen such that the step size **doubles** as we move from one segment to the next. **Step 2 — Sketch Description (2 Marks):** | Segment | Range (positive) | Step Size (Δ_k) | Levels | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0 to 1/128 | 1/2048 | 16 | | 2 | 1/128 to 1/64 | 1/1024 | 16 | | 3 | 1/64 to 1/32 | 1/512 | 16 | | 4 | 1/32 to 1 | 1/256 | 16 | **Step 3 — Proof of Doubling Relationship (2 Marks):** Let the step sizes in the 4 positive segments be Δ₁, Δ₂, Δ₃, Δ₄. The total range from 0 to 1 must equal the sum of all 64 positive levels: $$ 1 = 16\Delta_1 + 16\Delta_2 + 16\Delta_3 + 16\Delta_4 $$ For the doubling property Δ_k = 2^(k−1) · Δ₁: $$ 1 = 16\Delta_1(1 + 2 + 4 + 8) = 16\Delta_1 \cdot 15 $$ $$ \Delta_1 = \frac{1}{240} \approx 0.00417 $$ Hence Δ₂ = 2Δ₁, Δ₃ = 4Δ₁, Δ₄ = 8Δ₁, confirming the **doubling relationship**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **(a)** Derive the A-Law compression expression. For an input x = 0.005, compute the compressed output using A = 87.6. Identify which segment of the piecewise function applies. (7 Marks) **(b)** With a block diagram, explain the complete companding PCM system. Compare the SQNR performance of uniform PCM with companded PCM for voice signals. (7 Marks) ### Model Solution to Question B #### Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Identify the segment (2 Marks):** For x = 0.005, the boundary is 1/A = 1/87.6 ≈ 0.0114. Since |x| = 0.005 < 0.0114, the **linear segment** applies. **Step 2 — Apply the A-Law linear formula (3 Marks):** $$ F_A(0.005) = \text{sgn}(0.005) \cdot \frac{A \cdot \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A} = \frac{87.6 \cdot 0.005}{1 + \ln(87.6)} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the denominator (2 Marks):** $$ 1 + \ln(87.6) = 1 + 4.4726 = 5.4726 $$ **Step 4 — Compute the ratio:** $$ F_A(0.005) = \frac{0.4380}{5.4726} \approx 0.0801 $$ **Final Answer:** F_A(0.005) ≈ **+0.0801** (linear segment). #### Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Block Diagram (3 Marks):** The PCM companding system consists of: - **Transmitter:** Sampler → Compressor → Quantizer → Encoder - **Channel:** Digital bitstream (64 kbps) - **Receiver:** Decoder → Expander → Lowpass Filter → Reconstructed signal **Step 2 — SQNR Comparison (4 Marks):** | System | SQNR for Low-Amplitude Signals | SQNR for High-Amplitude Signals | |---|---|---| | Uniform PCM | **Poor** (small signals are quantized coarsely) | Good (large signals span many levels) | | Companded PCM | **Good** (small signals are amplified before quantization) | Slightly worse (large signals are compressed) | | **Overall perceived quality** | **Much better** (~24 dB improvement) | Comparable | > **Key insight:** For voice signals where most energy is in low amplitudes, the companded PCM achieves a **uniformly high perceptual SQNR** across the entire dynamic range, which is far more important than the absolute SQNR at peak amplitude. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in µ-Law / A-Law Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the sgn(x) factor** — Many students write the formula as F(x) = ln(1+µx)/ln(1+µ) and lose 1-2 marks. Always include sgn(x) or state that the formula is for x ≥ 0 with symmetric extension. > 2. **Confusing µ = 255 with µ = 256** — The standard is **µ = 255**, not 256. Although ln(256) = 8·ln(2) makes some calculations cleaner, **µ = 255 is the ITU-T standard**. > 3. **Using the wrong piece of A-Law** — Always check whether |x| ≤ 1/A or |x| ≥ 1/A **before** substituting. Students who blindly apply the log formula to small inputs lose full marks. > 4. **Confusing the standard A value** — The correct standard is **A = 87.6**, not 87.56 or 86. Memorize the precise value. > 5. **Forgetting to state units and parameters** — Always mention the parameter value (µ = 255, A = 87.6), the number of segments (8), and the bit rate (64 kbps) in your answers. > 6. **Skipping the sgn(x) sign restoration** — After taking the absolute value, students often forget to multiply by sgn(x), producing only positive outputs. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for µ-Law and A-Law Companding:** > > - **Companding** = COMpression + exPANDING; improves SQNR by ~24 dB for voice. > - **µ-Law Formula:** F_µ(x) = sgn(x) · ln(1 + µ|x|) / ln(1 + µ), with **µ = 255**. > - **A-Law Formula:** Piecewise — linear for |x| ≤ 1/A, logarithmic for 1/A ≤ |x| ≤ 1, with **A = 87.6**. > - **Region boundary for A-Law:** 1/A ≈ **0.0114**. > - **µ-Law region:** North America, Japan. **A-Law region:** Europe, India. > - **8-bit PCM** uses 8 segments (4 positive + 4 negative), 16 levels per segment, 256 total levels. > - **Step size doubles** between successive segments: Δ_k = 2^(k−1) · Δ₁. > - **First step size:** Δ₁ = 1/240 (for normalized 0 to 1 range). > - **Bit rate** = 8 bits × 8 kHz = **64 kbps** per voice channel. > - **A-Law has linear region near origin**; µ-Law is purely logarithmic from x = 0. > - **A-Law has better tandem encoding performance** (multiple compression cycles). > - **Applications:** PSTN telephony, VoIP codecs, GSM base stations, ISDN, digital trunk lines. > - **Standardization:** Both governed by **ITU-T G.711** recommendation. > - **Slope at origin:** µ-Law ≈ 8.59; A-Law ≈ 16 (steeper, but linear). > - **Remember sgn(x)** when applying either formula to negative inputs. > - **ln(1+µ) = ln(256) = 8·ln(2) ≈ 5.5452** — useful shortcut for µ-Law problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Shorten** is a *lossless audio compression* algorithm (file extension `.shn`) developed by **Tony Robinson** at the University of Cambridge in the early 1990s. It is a context-modeling based coder that combines **Linear Prediction (LP)** with **Rice–Golomb variable-length coding** to compress digitized audio waveforms—particularly speech—without any loss of information.

From the KTU 2024 syllabus perspective, Shorten is presented as a classic, mathematically elegant example of a **two-stage lossless compression pipeline**:

$$\text{Stage 1: Prediction} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Stage 2: Entropy Coding}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Shorten is a *universal-domain* lossless compressor. Although it was engineered primarily for **monophonic and stereophonic PCM audio (8/16-bit)**, the underlying principles (linear prediction + Rice coding) directly generalize to modern codecs (FLAC, MPEG-4 ALS, and the linear-prediction mode of Opus).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are writing a daily weather diary. Instead of writing the full temperature each day, you write down **"same as yesterday"** or **"3 degrees warmer than yesterday"**. These short notes compress the information dramatically, yet a reader can perfectly reconstruct the original temperatures by following the same logic.

Shorten does exactly this for audio samples:

- **Prediction Stage** → "How will the next sound sample most likely behave based on the previous ones?" The algorithm writes down only the *deviation* (the *residual*).
- **Rice Coding Stage** → Small deviations occur more often than huge ones (just like small weather changes are more common than extreme ones). Short variable-length codes are assigned to common small deviations, longer codes to rare large ones.

## 1.3 Key Salient Features of Shorten

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Characteristics of the Shorten Coder**

- **Sample-by-sample processing** with a sliding window of past samples.
- **Four fixed predictor orders** (0, 1, 2, 3) that approximate the audio waveform locally.
- **Block-wise optimization**: every block of 256 samples selects the *best* predictor order and *best* Rice parameter.
- **Residual compression** using **Rice coding** (a power-of-2 special case of Golomb coding).
- **Bit-shift quantization pre-processing** to reduce dynamic range and improve Rice-coding efficiency.
- **Lossless reconstruction** guaranteed for 8-bit, 16-bit, and 24-bit PCM input.
- **Self-describing file format** with explicit header (magic number `ajkg`), version, block size, channels, sample rate, and bit depth.

## 1.4 Design Goals and Performance Profile

| Performance Metric | Shorten Characteristic |
|---|---|
| **Compression Ratio (speech)** | ~2.0 – 2.6 : 1 |
| **Compression Ratio (CD music)** | ~1.4 – 1.8 : 1 |
| **Algorithm Complexity** | Low (linear time, $O(N)$) |
| **Symmetry** | Asymmetric (encode slower, decode faster) |
| **Standardization** | None (proprietary open format) |
| **Patent Status** | Royalty-free, public domain |

The asymmetric design—where the encoder is allowed to be *slow* (it must search for the optimal predictor/Rice parameter per block) while the decoder is *fast*—is a recurring theme in KTU 2024 Scheme papers.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Stage Lossless Architecture

Shorten implements the canonical **Predict + Encode** paradigm. The information-theoretic justification is straightforward: a *good* predictor produces residuals that are nearly white, low-variance, and geometrically distributed—ideal for entropy coding.

### 2.1.1 Stage 1 — Linear Prediction

Given an input audio stream $x[0], x[1], x[2], \dots$, Shorten computes a *predicted* sample $\hat{x}[n]$ as a linear combination of the previous $p$ samples:

$$\hat{x}[n] = \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i \, x[n-i]$$

The **residual** (or *prediction error*) is the signed difference:

$$e[n] = x[n] - \hat{x}[n]$$

> [!NOTE]
> Shorten restricts itself to **four hard-coded predictor coefficient sets** corresponding to $p \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ with **integer coefficients** that satisfy the binomial expansion of $(1 - z^{-1})^p$. This choice keeps the encoder/decoder light-weight and avoids floating-point computation.

| Order $p$ | Predictor Formula $\hat{x}[n]$ | Coefficient Set $(a_1, a_2, a_3)$ | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $0$ | $(0, 0, 0)$ | Pure residual coding (delta from zero) |
| 1 | $x[n-1]$ | $(1, 0, 0)$ | Step predictor |
| 2 | $2x[n-1] - x[n-2]$ | $(2, -1, 0)$ | Linear ramp predictor |
| 3 | $3x[n-1] - 3x[n-2] + x[n-3]$ | $(3, -3, 1)$ | Quadratic / parabolic predictor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the binomial structure?** The coefficients are the *signed* binomial coefficients of $(1 - z^{-1})^p$. This is mathematically equivalent to **repeated first-order differentiation** of the discrete signal. Repeated differentiation attenuates the slowly-varying DC/background component of audio, leaving a sparse, compact residual stream.

### 2.1.2 Stage 2 — Rice Entropy Coding

Once residuals $e[n]$ are computed, they are shifted (see §2.3) to a non-negative representation $u[n]$ and encoded using **Rice codes**—a parameterised family of variable-length codes parameterised by an integer $k \geq 0$.

For a non-negative integer $u$ and parameter $k$, write:

$$u = q \cdot 2^k + r, \quad \text{with } 0 \leq r < 2^k$$

The Rice code consists of:
- **$q$ ones** (unary part),
- **one zero** (separator),
- **$k$ bits** encoding the remainder $r$ (binary part).

$$\text{Length of Rice code} = q + 1 + k \text{ bits}$$

The decoder recovers $u$ by counting ones until the first zero, reading the next $k$ bits as $r$, and reconstructing $u = q \cdot 2^k + r$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Table — All Formulas Required for KTU 2024 Examination**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables & Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Predictor $p=0$ | $\hat{x}[n] = 0$ | $p$ = order | Always available fallback |
| 2 | Predictor $p=1$ | $\hat{x}[n] = x[n-1]$ | $x[n-1]$ = last sample (16-bit signed) | Good for step signals |
| 3 | Predictor $p=2$ | $\hat{x}[n] = 2x[n-1] - x[n-2]$ | $x[n-2]$ = two samples back | Linear extrapolation |
| 4 | Predictor $p=3$ | $\hat{x}[n] = 3x[n-1] - 3x[n-2] + x[n-3]$ | $x[n-3]$ = three samples back | Parabolic extrapolation |
| 5 | Residual | $e[n] = x[n] - \hat{x}[n]$ | $e[n] \in \mathbb{Z}$, typically small | Signed integer |
| 6 | Rice split | $u = q \cdot 2^k + r$ | $q \geq 0$ integer, $0 \leq r < 2^k$ | Euclidean division |
| 7 | Unary count | $q = \lfloor u / 2^k \rfloor$ | floor division | Number of leading ones |
| 8 | Remainder | $r = u \bmod 2^k$ | mod $2^k$ | Lower $k$ bits of $u$ |
| 9 | Code length | $L(u) = \lfloor u/2^k \rfloor + 1 + k$ | bits | Total Rice code length |
| 10 | Bit shift | $u[n] = (e[n] \gg s) + \text{offset}$ | $s \geq 0$ | Removes low-order noise |
| 11 | Block size | $N_{\text{block}} = 256$ samples | fixed | Per-block optimization |
| 12 | Block mean | $\mu_{\text{block}} = \frac{1}{256}\sum e[n]$ | samples | Used in some variants |
| 13 | Bit rate bound | $H \leq L_{\text{avg}} \leq H + 2$ | bits/sample | Shannon limit + 2 bits |

> **Warning on table syntax:** All absolute value / magnitude notations use the LaTeX command `\vert` (rendered as `|`) to preserve the markdown table structure. For example, write $\vert e[n] \vert$ — never $\vert e[n] \vert$ with the raw pipe symbol.

## 2.3 The Bit-Shift (Right-Shift) Pre-Processing

Real audio contains 16-bit (or 24-bit) samples, but the *information-bearing* content rarely uses the full least-significant-bit (LSB) range. The last few LSBs are dominated by quantisation noise, dither, or thermal noise of the ADC. Shorten allows the encoder to **right-shift the residual** by $s$ bits (typically $s \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$) before Rice coding, and record the shift in the block header. This:
- Reduces the dynamic range of the residual.
- Produces a residual distribution that is *narrower* and more amenable to Rice coding.
- Slightly increases distortion (in the lossless sense) which is recovered at the decoder by *left-shifting* the decoded residual back by $s$ bits and re-adding the LSB noise as a separately-encoded side stream (or, in the simplest case, treating it as zero—relying on the LSB being zero anyway).

## 2.4 Block-Wise Adaptation

Shorten processes the input in **fixed-size blocks of $N = 256$ samples**. For each block, the encoder:

1. Tries all four predictor orders ($p = 0, 1, 2, 3$).
2. For each predictor, tries a small set of bit shifts $s$ and Rice parameters $k$.
3. Measures the *estimated coded length* of the block under each combination.
4. Selects the combination $(p^*, s^*, k^*)$ that **minimises the total encoded block length**.
5. Writes a small **block header** encoding $(p^*, s^*, k^*)$ and emits the Rice-coded residuals.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** The block header is "PSK" in the order (Predictor, Shift, $k$). Examiners love asking "list the three parameters that the Shorten block header specifies"—memorise the trio **predictor order, bit shift, and Rice parameter $k$**.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Shorten's design influences several real-world systems:

- **FLAC (Free Lossless Audio Codec)**: Uses the *same* Fixed / LPC predictors with order up to 4, plus a richer Rice/Rice2 partition.
- **MPEG-4 ALS**: Extends Shorten's idea to 32-bit residuals and long-term prediction.
- **Opus codec (CELT mode)**: Adopts a similar linear-predictor-then-Rice framework for packet-loss concealment.
- **Speech storage in legacy telephony systems**: Many telephone answering machines used Shorten-family codecs in the late 1990s to record voice messages efficiently.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rice Code Length

**Statement:** For a non-negative integer $u$ and Rice parameter $k \geq 0$, the Rice code for $u$ has length exactly $\lfloor u / 2^k \rfloor + 1 + k$ bits.

**Step-by-step Proof:**

Begin with the Euclidean decomposition of $u$ by the divisor $2^k$. This is valid because $2^k$ is a positive integer:

$$u = q \cdot 2^k + r, \quad 0 \leq r < 2^k, \quad q = \lfloor u / 2^k \rfloor$$

Count the bits required by each component of the Rice code:

1. The **unary part** consists of exactly $q$ one-bits, contributing $q$ bits.
2. The **separator** is a single zero bit, contributing $1$ bit.
3. The **remainder** $r$ is written as a $k$-bit unsigned binary number (with leading zeros if necessary), contributing $k$ bits.

Summing all three components:

$$L(u) = q + 1 + k = \lfloor u / 2^k \rfloor + 1 + k \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Rice Coding a Single Residual

**Problem:** Encode the residual $u = 19$ using Rice parameter $k = 2$.

**Solution:**

Apply the Euclidean split with divisor $2^2 = 4$:

$$19 = q \cdot 4 + r, \quad 0 \leq r < 4$$

Solving:

$$q = \lfloor 19 / 4 \rfloor = 4, \qquad r = 19 \bmod 4 = 3$$

Emit the three code parts:
- Unary: $\underbrace{1111}_{q=4}$ → 4 ones
- Separator: `0` → 1 zero
- Remainder: $r = 3$ in 2 bits → `11`

Final Rice code: **1111011** (7 bits).

Verification via the formula:

$$L = \lfloor 19 / 4 \rfloor + 1 + 2 = 4 + 1 + 2 = 7 \text{ bits} \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Decoding a Rice Codeword

**Problem:** Decode the bitstream `000101` using Rice parameter $k = 2$.

**Solution:**

Read leading ones until the first zero appears. The stream `000101` begins with three zeros—but wait, the *first bit* is `0`, meaning the unary count is $q = 0$.

- Unary part: $q = 0$ (no leading ones).
- Separator: first `0` (consumed).
- Remainder: next $k = 2$ bits → `01` → $r = 1$.

Reconstruct:

$$u = q \cdot 2^k + r = 0 \cdot 4 + 1 = 1$$

Verification: $L = 0 + 1 + 2 = 3$ bits, which exactly matches the consumed input. $\checkmark$

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Full Shorten Encoding of a Tiny Block

**Problem:** Encode the audio block $x = [10, 13, 15, 18, 22]$ using predictor order $p = 1$ and Rice parameter $k = 2$. Assume bit shift $s = 0$.

**Solution (Step-by-step):**

**Step A — Predict each sample using $p = 1$ ($\hat{x}[n] = x[n-1]$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n=0 &: \quad \hat{x}[0] = 0 \quad \text{(no previous sample; defined as 0 for order-1 too)} \\
n=1 &: \quad \hat{x}[1] = x[0] = 10 \\
n=2 &: \quad \hat{x}[2] = x[1] = 13 \\
n=3 &: \quad \hat{x}[3] = x[2] = 15 \\
n=4 &: \quad \hat{x}[4] = x[3] = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Compute residuals $e[n] = x[n] - \hat{x}[n]$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e[0] &= 10 - 0 = 10 \\
e[1] &= 13 - 10 = 3 \\
e[2] &= 15 - 13 = 2 \\
e[3] &= 18 - 15 = 3 \\
e[4] &= 22 - 18 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Map signed residuals to non-negative (offset by +32 for safety in real Shorten, here we assume already non-negative for simplicity):**

The non-negative residual list is $u = [10, 3, 2, 3, 4]$.

**Step D — Rice encode each value with $k = 2$:**

Apply $u = q \cdot 4 + r$ for each:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u = 10 &: \quad q = \lfloor 10/4 \rfloor = 2,\; r = 2 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{code} = \texttt{11010} \quad (5 \text{ bits}) \\
u = 3  &: \quad q = 0,\; r = 3 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{code} = \texttt{011} \quad (3 \text{ bits}) \\
u = 2  &: \quad q = 0,\; r = 2 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{code} = \texttt{010} \quad (3 \text{ bits}) \\
u = 3  &: \quad q = 0,\; r = 3 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{code} = \texttt{011} \quad (3 \text{ bits}) \\
u = 4  &: \quad q = 1,\; r = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{code} = \texttt{10000} \quad (5 \text{ bits})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step E — Concatenate and total:**

$$\text{Bitstream} = \texttt{11010}\;\vert\;\texttt{011}\;\vert\;\texttt{010}\;\vert\;\texttt{011}\;\vert\;\texttt{10000}$$

$$\text{Total bits} = 5 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 5 = 19 \text{ bits}$$

**Step F — Compression ratio (uncompressed vs. compressed):**

Assuming 16-bit signed PCM samples, the uncompressed block uses $5 \times 16 = 80$ bits. The Shorten-coded block uses 19 bits (plus ~3 bits of block header for $p, s, k$).

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{80}{19 + 3} = \frac{80}{22} \approx 3.64 : 1$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Trade-Offs in Shorten

| Design Decision | What it Affects | Trade-Off Direction |
|---|---|---|
| Increasing predictor order $p$ | Reduces residual variance for smooth audio | Higher $p$ requires more past samples, more overflow risk |
| Increasing bit shift $s$ | Narrows residual dynamic range | Loss of LSB precision (still lossless if LSBs are zero) |
| Increasing Rice $k$ | Better for high-variance residuals | Worse for low-variance residuals (longer unary prefix) |
| Increasing block size $N$ | Better statistics for parameter search | Slower encoder, more memory |
| Multiple channels | Stereo / multichannel support | Channels encoded independently (no joint stereo) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Shorten Encoder — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the data flow through the Shorten **encoder** from raw PCM input to the compressed `.shn` bitstream.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pcmInput["PCM Audio Samples x n"] --> blockSplit["Block Splitter N = 256 samples"]
    blockSplit --> predLoop{"For each predictor p in 0 1 2 3"}
    predLoop --> lp["Linear Predictor hat x n"]
    lp --> res["Residual e n = x n minus hat x n"]
    res --> shift["Bit Shift by s bits"]
    shift --> riceLoop{"For each Rice k in 0 to 9"}
    riceLoop --> rice["Rice Encoder"]
    rice --> length["Measure coded length L p s k"]
    length --> decision{"L minimum?"}
    decision -- Yes --> best["Store best p s k"]
    decision -- No --> predLoop
    best --> blockHeader["Emit Block Header p s k"]
    blockHeader --> emit["Emit Rice coded residuals to bitstream"]
    emit --> out["Compressed Shorten File shn"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **inner loops** (predictor sweep, Rice parameter sweep) constitute the encoder's expensive search.
- Only the **best** combination of $(p, s, k)$ is written to the bitstream—the decoder learns the choice from the block header, no search needed.

## 4.2 Shorten Decoder — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The decoder is the *exact mirror* of the encoder, but with no search loops—decoding is purely sequential and fast.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    in["Compressed Shorten File shn"] --> readHeader["Read Block Header p s k"]
    readHeader --> rice["Rice Decoder"]
    rice --> unshift["Left shift by s bits"]
    unshift --> reconstruct["Add Predictor output hat x n using p"]
    reconstruct --> lp["Inverse Linear Predictor"]
    lp --> out["Reconstructed PCM Samples x n"]
```

## 4.3 Shorten File Format — Layout Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FILE HEADER: Magic ajkg, version, channels, block size, sample rate, bit depth, file length"] --> B["BLOCK 1 HEADER: predictor p, shift s, Rice k, bitstream commands"]
    B --> C["BLOCK 1 DATA: 256 Rice coded residuals"]
    C --> D["BLOCK 2 HEADER: p s k"]
    D --> E["BLOCK 2 DATA: 256 Rice coded residuals"]
    E --> F["...more blocks..."]
    F --> G["EOF MARKER"]
```

## 4.4 Residual Distribution Before and After Prediction

The conceptual figure below is rendered as a Mermaid schematic of the residual *histogram* before prediction (broad) and after prediction (narrow / geometric).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Original Audio Histogram: wide bell, heavy tails"] -- "Subtract hat x n" --> B["Residual Histogram: narrow spike at zero, geometric decay"]
    B -- "Rice code with tuned k" --> C["Compact bitstream: ~2 bits per sample average"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Intuition:** A well-predicted audio stream yields residuals that obey a *two-sided geometric / Laplace* distribution centred at zero. The Rice code is the *provably optimal* variable-length code for a one-sided geometric distribution; by offsetting residuals to non-negative, Shorten maps the two-sided distribution into the one-sided domain where Rice coding is near-optimal.

## 4.5 Predictor Coefficient Pattern (Binomial Structure)

The four Shorten predictor orders follow the signed binomial-coefficient pattern of $(1 - z^{-1})^p$:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p0["Order 0: coefficients = +1 (no past sample)"]
    p1["Order 1: coefficients = +1, -1 (first difference)"]
    p2["Order 2: coefficients = +1, -2, +1 (second difference)"]
    p3["Order 3: coefficients = +1, -3, +3, -1 (third difference)"]
    p0 --> p1
    p1 --> p2
    p2 --> p3
```

In actual prediction equations (note the alternating sign convention used in Shorten):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{x}[n] &= 0 \quad (p=0) \\
\hat{x}[n] &= x[n-1] \quad (p=1) \\
\hat{x}[n] &= 2x[n-1] - x[n-2] \quad (p=2) \\
\hat{x}[n] &= 3x[n-1] - 3x[n-2] + x[n-3] \quad (p=3)
\end{aligned}
$$
